Islamic physician and philosopher whose Canon of Medicine was used in European universities for 600 years. Represents how Dar al-Islam advanced beyond classical knowledge.

Pachacuti

INCA EMPIRE · r. 1438–1471

The Inca emperor who transformed a small kingdom into a massive empire through conquest and organization. Built much of Machu Picchu. His reign marks the beginning of the Inca Empire as a major power.

Who wrote the Rihla — a travel account covering over 75,000 miles across Afro-Eurasia and one of the most important primary sources for AP World History?

📝 Term Check — Key People

In your own words: Who was Genghis Khan and why does the AP care?
AP EXPECTS
Founder of the Mongol Empire (1162–1227). United the Mongol tribes and launched the conquests that would create the largest contiguous land empire in history. His conquests began the disruption of Song China, Dar al-Islam, and Eastern Europe. He died before the full conquest of China and the destruction of Baghdad — his grandsons finished those jobs. AP significance: Genghis Khan is the starting point for the Mongol disruption of the post-classical world, which is Unit 2's central story. Also note: the Mongols initially caused destruction, but eventually the Pax Mongolica (Mongol Peace) facilitated trade.
In your own words: Who was Kublai Khan and why does the AP care?
AP EXPECTS
Grandson of Genghis Khan. Completed the Mongol conquest of Song China in 1279, establishing the Yuan Dynasty. Hosted Marco Polo at his court. AP significance: Kublai Khan represents the transition from Song to Mongol-ruled China. The Yuan Dynasty shows how the Mongols (initially outsiders) adopted Chinese administrative structures while disrupting others. He also attempted to expand into Japan and Southeast Asia — and failed both times, showing Mongol limits.
In your own words: Who was Marco Polo and why does the AP care?
AP EXPECTS
A Venetian merchant who traveled to China and spent years at Kublai Khan's court (1271–1295). His account of his travels introduced many Europeans to the wealth and sophistication of East Asia. AP significance: (1) he traveled safely because of Mongol-facilitated stability (Pax Mongolica); (2) his account sparked European interest in Asian trade — helping motivate the later Age of Exploration; (3) he's a primary source for understanding the Mongol Yuan Dynasty. His travels show how cultural knowledge travels through individual people.
In your own words: Who was Ibn Battuta and why does the AP care?
AP EXPECTS
A Moroccan Islamic scholar (1304–1368) who traveled more than 75,000 miles across Afro-Eurasia — more than anyone in history before the modern era. His travel account (the Rihla) is one of the most important primary sources for AP World History Units 1 and 2. AP significance: (1) his travels show how connected the Islamic world was — he moved from one Sufi lodge to another across continents; (2) his accounts of sub-Saharan Africa, India, and China are invaluable; (3) the AP will likely give you excerpts from the Rihla as DBQ documents. He traveled farther than Marco Polo and wrote in far more detail.
In your own words: Who was Al-Khwarizmi and why does the AP care?
AP EXPECTS
A Muslim mathematician (780–850 CE) working under the Abbasid Caliphate. Developed algebra — the word "algebra" comes from his book title al-kitab al-mukhtasar fi hisab al-jabr. The word "algorithm" comes from a Latin version of his name. AP significance: Al-Khwarizmi is the go-to example of the Islamic Golden Age's genuine intellectual innovation. He didn't just preserve Greek math — he created an entirely new field. Use him whenever a prompt asks about Islamic scholarly contributions or the relationship between Dar al-Islam and global knowledge.
In your own words: Who was Ibn Sina (Avicenna) and why does the AP care?
AP EXPECTS
A Persian Islamic physician and philosopher (980–1037 CE). His Canon of Medicine systematically synthesized Greek, Persian, and Islamic medical knowledge. European universities used it as a core medical textbook for approximately 600 years after his death. AP significance: Ibn Sina is the go-to example of Islamic medical advances AND of how Islamic knowledge transferred to Europe. Use him as evidence of Dar al-Islam's role as the world's intellectual leader in this period, and as evidence that Islamic scholars went beyond preserving ancient knowledge — they improved it.
In your own words: Who was Pachacuti and why does the AP care?
AP EXPECTS
The Inca emperor (r. 1438–1471) who transformed a small Andean kingdom into a massive empire through military conquest and administrative reorganization. Built much of Machu Picchu. AP significance: Pachacuti marks the beginning of the Inca Empire as a major power — his reign is when the mit'a system, road network, and administrative structures were consolidated. He's evidence that empires are often built by individual leaders with exceptional organizational vision, not just by geographic luck or population size.
Section 10

Glossary of Key Terms

How to Use This Section

Rate every term — I Know, Unsure, or Don't Know. Once you've rated all of them, the ones you know disappear. Everything else shows you the definition and a space to practice writing it in your own words.

Section 11

Causes, Consequences & Significance

This is one of the most important sections for AP essays. The exam does not just want you to know what happened — it wants you to explain WHY it happened and what difference it made.

Song China's Economic and Technological Dominance

📌 Causes
  • Champa rice: Enabled double-cropping and a massive population boom, creating a larger labor force and domestic market.
  • Civil service exam: Created a competent, meritocratic bureaucracy capable of managing a complex, large state.
  • Grand Canal: Enabled cheap, efficient internal trade, allowing goods and food to move across the empire.
⚡ Consequences
  • China's innovations (gunpowder, printing, compass, paper money) spread along trade routes and eventually transformed the entire world.
  • Urban expansion and merchant class growth reshaped Chinese society.
🔭 Significance

Song China demonstrates that premodern societies could achieve extraordinary economic and technological sophistication. It directly challenges Eurocentric narratives of history. It also shows the connection between agricultural surplus, population growth, and economic development — a pattern the AP loves.

The Fall of the Abbasid Caliphate (1258 CE)

📌 Causes
  • Mongol military expansion under Hulagu Khan swept westward from Central Asia.
  • The Abbasids had been weakening for centuries due to internal fragmentation and regional governors asserting independence.
⚡ Consequences
  • The House of Wisdom was destroyed, along with an incalculable amount of accumulated knowledge.
  • Turkish sultanates rose to fill the power vacuum.
  • However, Islam itself survived and continued to spread. The Mongols who conquered Islamic territories often converted to Islam themselves.
🔭 Significance

Shows that political structures can collapse while cultural systems survive. The fall of the Abbasid Caliphate ended centralized Islamic political power, but Dar al-Islam continued to expand as a cultural zone. This is a key example of the difference between political and cultural continuity.

Mansa Musa's Hajj (1324 CE)

📌 Causes
  • Mali's immense wealth from the gold-salt trade gave Mansa Musa the resources to make an unprecedented pilgrimage.
  • As a Muslim ruler, performing the hajj was both a religious obligation and a political statement.
⚡ Consequences
  • Gold prices crashed across the Mediterranean for a decade.
  • Mali appeared on European maps for the first time.
  • He brought back scholars and architects from Cairo who helped build mosques and schools in Timbuktu.
🔭 Significance

The hajj functioned not just as a religious pilgrimage but as a vehicle for massive economic disruption and cultural exchange. This is evidence that religious practices can have major economic and political consequences. It also demonstrates West Africa's deep integration into the global economy of Dar al-Islam.

European Decentralization and Feudalism

📌 Causes
  • The fall of the Western Roman Empire (476 CE) removed the centralizing force that had held Europe together.
  • Ongoing invasions (Vikings, Magyar cavalry, Saracens) made security a constant local concern.
⚡ Consequences
  • Fragmented political power meant Europe could not project military or economic force beyond its borders in this period.
  • The Catholic Church filled some of the vacuum, providing cultural unity and preserving Latin learning.
🔭 Significance

This is the baseline from which Europe transforms. Understanding that Europe in 1200 is weak and fragmented makes the European expansion of Units 3–4 (1450 onward) far more interesting and comprehensible. Why did Europe explode onto the world stage in the 1400s and 1500s? Partly because the centralizing states that form in this period gave them the resources and organization to do so.

Choose ONE of the four developments above. Write a mini-paragraph explaining its causes, consequences, and why it matters for understanding AP World History broadly.
GUIDANCE
A strong causation paragraph: (1) States the development clearly. (2) Names 2–3 specific causes with explanation of how they led to the development. (3) Identifies 2–3 specific consequences. (4) Connects the significance to a broader AP theme (economic development, religious spread, political fragmentation, cultural exchange, etc.). Your paragraph should be 5–8 sentences and use specific historical evidence (names, dates, places).
Section 12

Timeline of Unit 1 (c. 1200–1450)

Use this timeline as a quick reference and to practice periodization. Notice how events in different regions overlap — while Mansa Musa is doing his hajj in 1324, the Aztecs are founding Tenochtitlán in 1325. The world is moving simultaneously.

c. 960

Song Dynasty Founded in China

Begins one of China's most sophisticated dynasties; sets the economic and intellectual baseline for Unit 1.

c. 1095

First Crusade Launched

European Christians attempt to retake Jerusalem; begins centuries of contact between Europe and Dar al-Islam; cultural exchange follows.

c. 1200

AP World History Begins

The official starting point of the course; Song China, Dar al-Islam, and other regions are all at varying stages of development.

1206

Genghis Khan Unites Mongols / Delhi Sultanate Founded

Two major developments in the same year: the Mongols begin their world-conquering expansion; Islam arrives as a ruling power in South Asia.

1226

Mali Empire Rises to Power

West Africa's great empire begins its period of dominance; controls gold-salt trade across the Sahara.

1258

Mongols Sack Baghdad; Abbasid Caliphate Destroyed

End of the political center of Dar al-Islam; House of Wisdom burned; massive disruption to the Islamic world; Turkish sultanates rise to fill the vacuum.

1271–1295

Marco Polo Travels to China

Venetian merchant visits Yuan China; his written account sparks European interest in Asian wealth and trade.

1279

Song Dynasty Falls; Yuan Dynasty Established

Kublai Khan completes the Mongol conquest of China; the Song era ends; China under Mongol rule begins.

c. 1300s

Ibn Battuta Begins His Travels

The world's greatest medieval traveler covers over 75,000 miles across Afro-Eurasia; his writings are primary sources for AP World History.

1324

Mansa Musa's Hajj to Mecca

West African wealth displayed on world stage; gold prices crash in Egypt and Middle East; Mali put on European maps for the first time.

1325

Tenochtitlán Founded by Aztecs

Capital of the Aztec Empire established on an island in Lake Texcoco; grows into one of the world's largest cities by the early 1500s.

c. 1350s

Plague Spreads Across Afro-Eurasia

Bubonic plague, spreading via trade routes (including Mongol-facilitated Silk Roads), kills up to 1/3 of Europe's population; devastates parts of Dar al-Islam and China as well.

1438

Pachacuti Begins Inca Expansion

The Inca Empire begins its rapid expansion across the Andes Mountains; within decades will control the largest empire in the Americas.

c. 1450

End of Unit 1 Period

Transition to the Early Modern period; gunpowder, printing press, and oceanic navigation will transform the world in Unit 3 onward.

In what year did the Mongols destroy the Abbasid Caliphate and burn the House of Wisdom in Baghdad?
In the same year that Mansa Musa completed his legendary hajj (1324), the Aztecs did what one year later in 1325?
Section 13

Historical Thinking Skills

AP World History is not just about memorizing facts. It is about thinking like a historian. The College Board tests specific historical thinking skills, and you will need to demonstrate these skills in every essay, DBQ, and SAQ on the exam.

1. Contextualization

Contextualization means placing a specific historical development within its broader historical context — what was going on in the world (or region) that helps explain why this thing happened or why it matters?

✅ What the AP Wants

A contextualization statement must go BEYOND just saying "this happened before." You need to explain the connection.

WEAK: "The Mongols invaded China before the Song Dynasty fell."

STRONG: "The political instability of the post-classical period, including the fragmentation of the Abbasid Caliphate and the Mongol conquests across Eurasia, created power vacuums that new states (Turkish sultanates, the Yuan Dynasty) rushed to fill, reshaping the political landscape of both Dar al-Islam and East Asia."

Unit 1 contextualization examples to practice:

  • Song China's economic power developed in the context of the post-classical period's expanding trade networks, including the Silk Roads.
  • The spread of Islam via Sufi missionaries occurred in the context of the collapse of centralized Abbasid political control, which left a spiritual void that Sufis filled.
  • European feudalism developed in the context of the power vacuum left by the fall of the Roman Empire, combined with constant external military threats.

2. Continuity and Change Over Time (CCOT)

This skill asks you to identify what stayed the same and what changed over a given time period. For Unit 1, think about what was true in 1200 CE and what had changed by 1450 CE.

CONTINUITIES (What Stayed the Same)
  • Most societies remain agrarian (farming-based), with the majority of people as peasants or serfs
  • Major belief systems (Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Christianity, Confucianism) remain geographically dominant in their regions
  • Long-distance trade routes (Silk Roads, Indian Ocean, trans-Saharan) continue to function and expand
  • China remains the world's largest economy throughout this period
  • Patriarchal social structures dominate most of the world's civilizations
CHANGES (What Shifted)
  • Mongol conquests disrupted multiple major political systems (Song China, Abbasid Caliphate)
  • Turkish sultanates replace Arab caliphates as the political leaders of Dar al-Islam
  • New states emerge: Aztec Empire, Inca Empire, Delhi Sultanate, Mali Empire all rise to power
  • The spread of Islam accelerates via Sufi missionaries and Indian Ocean trade
  • Paper money, printing, and gunpowder begin spreading from China westward

3. Comparison

Comparison asks you to identify similarities and differences between two or more historical developments, often across different regions or time periods.

Great Unit 1 comparisons:

  • Song China vs. medieval Europe: Both had hierarchical political systems, but one was centralized and merit-based while the other was decentralized and birth-based.
  • Sufism vs. Bhakti movement: Both were mystical movements within their respective religions that emphasized personal spiritual experience over strict religious law; both emerged partly in response to the perceived rigidity of mainstream practice.
  • Aztec tribute system vs. Inca mit'a system: Both were ways of extracting labor and resources from subject peoples; both built massive infrastructure; but they operated differently (tribute vs. labor obligation).

4. Causation

Causation asks you to explain WHY something happened (causes) and what effects it produced (consequences).

Practice causation questions for Unit 1:

  • What caused the Song Dynasty's economic dominance? (Champa rice, civil service system, innovations in commerce)
  • What caused the spread of Islam into Southeast Asia? (Trade routes, Sufi missionaries, appeal of Islamic egalitarianism to some social groups)
  • What caused European decentralization? (Fall of Rome, external invasions, absence of strong central government)

5. Argumentation (Thesis Building)

Every LEQ, DBQ, and SAQ that asks you to write more than a sentence requires a clear, defensible thesis — a historically specific argument that answers the prompt and can be supported with evidence.

🎯 What Makes a Good Thesis?
  • It answers the question directly — no vague or obvious statements
  • It is historically defensible — you can support it with specific evidence
  • It goes beyond the obvious — makes a specific, nuanced argument

BAD: "There were many similarities and differences between Song China and the Delhi Sultanate." (This is a non-argument — of course there are differences and similarities.)

GOOD: "Both Song China and the Delhi Sultanate maintained power through administrative elites, but differed fundamentally in the religious basis of their authority — the Song relied on Confucian meritocracy while the Delhi Sultanate drew legitimacy from Islamic law and military strength."

Practice Thesis Writing: Write a one-sentence thesis for this prompt: "Compare the methods used by Song China and Dar al-Islam to advance and spread knowledge in the period 1200–1450."
SAMPLE THESIS
"While both Song China and Dar al-Islam advanced knowledge through institutional structures — China through the civil service examination system's emphasis on Confucian scholarship, and Dar al-Islam through the House of Wisdom's translation and innovation work — the Islamic world played the more globally transformative role by actively synthesizing Chinese, Greek, Indian, and Persian knowledge and transmitting it across trade routes stretching from Spain to Southeast Asia."
Section 14

Common Command Terms

Command terms are the action words in AP exam questions that tell you what to do. Getting them wrong is one of the most common reasons students lose points. Here are all the command terms you will encounter in AP World History, what they mean, and how to respond to them correctly.

TermWhat It MeansHow to Respond (Unit 1 Example)
Identify Name or point out something. Low demand — you don't need to explain, just state. "Identify one innovation of the Song Dynasty." Answer: The magnetic compass (or gunpowder, paper money, champa rice, etc.)
Describe Give key features or characteristics. A little more than "identify" — you're painting a picture. "Describe the role of Sufis in spreading Islam." Explain who they were, how they traveled, their spiritual approach, and where they went.
Explain Make clear how or why something happened, or what its effects were. Show the connection, don't just state the fact. "Explain one effect of the civil service exam on Song China's government." Don't just say it staffed the government — explain HOW: it created a meritocratic bureaucracy, which made the government more competent and stable than hereditary systems.
Analyze Examine something in detail, looking at its parts, its causes, and its effects. Higher level than explain. "Analyze how Dar al-Islam's scholars contributed to global knowledge." Break it down: what they preserved, what they innovated, how they transferred it, and why this mattered.
Compare Point out similarities AND differences between two things. Both similarities and differences are required unless the prompt says otherwise. "Compare political structures in Song China and medieval Europe." Address both similarities (hierarchical, relied on elites) and differences (centralized vs. decentralized, merit vs. birth).
Evaluate Make a judgment about the relative importance, validity, or success of something, with supporting evidence. "Evaluate the significance of Mansa Musa's hajj." Don't just describe it — make a judgment: Was it historically significant? What was its impact? What does it reveal about Mali and global connections?
Contextualize Place the development in its broader historical context. Explain what was happening that helps make sense of this event. "Contextualize the rise of the Delhi Sultanate." Connect it to the broader spread of Islam, Turkish migration out of Central Asia, and the pattern of non-Arab groups taking over Islamic political structures.
Corroborate Show how multiple sources or pieces of evidence support (or complicate) an argument. Used mainly in DBQs. When multiple documents in a DBQ all show the same pattern (e.g., Islamic innovation), you can say "This is corroborated by Document 3, which..."
A prompt asks you to "explain" how the civil service exam affected Song China. What must your response include that a simple "identify" or "describe" response would NOT?
Section 15

Common Pitfalls & How to Avoid Them

These are the mistakes that students make again and again on the AP exam. Each one can cost you real points. Read through these carefully and actively try to catch yourself making these mistakes when you write practice essays.

❌

Pitfall 1: Calling Pre-1500 Societies "Primitive"

The Problem: This is both historically wrong and harmful to your score. Song China had an economy three times the size of all of Europe. Timbuktu had a world-class university. The Inca built 40,000 km of roads without wheels or horses. Islamic scholars were teaching Europeans mathematics. "Primitive" is not a historical argument — it is a bias.

✅ The Fix: Use neutral, analytical language. Say "less economically complex" or "different organizational structures" rather than "primitive" or "backward."

❌

Pitfall 2: Ignoring the Americas

The Problem: Students often skip the Americas in Unit 1 because the AP gives less specific guidance on it. But the Aztecs and Incas appear again in Unit 4 (conquest and colonization), and writing about them in Unit 1 gives you contextualization points in future essays. Ignoring them entirely will hurt you in both units.

✅ The Fix: Learn the Aztec chinampa system and tribute system, and the Inca mit'a system and road network. These are the specific details the AP rewards.

❌

Pitfall 3: Mongols = Only Destruction

The Problem: Yes, the Mongols were incredibly violent — they destroyed Baghdad, killed millions, and burned the House of Wisdom. But writing about them as only destructive misses half the story. The Mongol Empire also created the Pax Mongolica (Mongol Peace), a period of relative stability across Eurasia that allowed trade to flourish along the Silk Roads. Marco Polo traveled to China because of Mongol-facilitated peace and safety.

✅ The Fix: For any question about the Mongols, acknowledge BOTH the destruction AND the trade facilitation / Pax Mongolica. Show nuance.

❌

Pitfall 4: Feudalism Was the Same Everywhere

The Problem: Japan also had a feudal system with samurai, lords, and peasants. Students often conflate Japanese and European feudalism because both are called "feudalism." But they developed independently, for different reasons, with different specifics. The AP will test this — especially in comparison questions.

✅ The Fix: When writing about feudalism, specify which society you are discussing. Japanese feudalism developed from a different context (no Roman legacy, different geography) and had different features (samurai culture, shogunate) than European feudalism.

❌

Pitfall 5: Islam Is Monolithic

The Problem: Students write about "Islam" as if it were one thing. But by 1200, Islam had already split into Sunni and Shia branches. There were Turkish sultanates and Arab caliphates. There were strict legal scholars (ulama) and mystical Sufis. Islam was diverse and contested.

✅ The Fix: Acknowledge internal diversity. When possible, specify which branch, region, or group of Muslims you are discussing.

❌

Pitfall 6: Mixing Up Units — Especially Zheng He

The Problem: Zheng He's famous voyages happened in the early 1400s, which technically falls within the Unit 1 time frame (1200–1450). But Zheng He is really a Unit 2 topic — his voyages are about maritime trade networks and the Indian Ocean trade route. If you use Zheng He in a Unit 1 essay, make sure you are using him correctly: as evidence of Chinese maritime capability, not as a Unit 1 political or social development.

✅ The Fix: Know your chronology. The Mongol invasions are Unit 2 even though they begin in this period. The bubonic plague is Unit 2. The Crusades are between Units 1 and 2. Be careful about what fits where.

❌

Pitfall 7: Weak Thesis Statements

The Problem: The most common DBQ and LEQ mistake is a thesis that just restates the prompt or makes an obvious, unsupported claim. "Song China and the Delhi Sultanate were both important empires" is not a thesis. It tells the reader nothing.

✅ The Fix: Your thesis needs a historically specific, defensible claim. Practice writing one-sentence theses for every prompt you see. Force yourself to make an actual argument, not just a topic sentence.

A student writes: "The Inca were primitive because they didn't have a written language." What pitfall is this student committing, and what evidence from Unit 1 would you use to push back on it?
Section 16

Sample DBQ — With Full Breakdown

📊 DBQ Basics

The Document-Based Question (DBQ) is worth 25% of your AP exam score. You will get 7 documents and 60 minutes (including 15 minutes of reading time). You need to write a full essay with a thesis, contextualization, and analysis of the documents.

"Evaluate the extent to which trade networks between 1200–1450 fostered cultural exchange across different regions of the world."
1
Decoding the Prompt

Command term: "Evaluate the extent" — This means you need to make a judgment about HOW MUCH trade fostered cultural exchange. Not just "did it" but "how much, under what conditions, and where did it fall short?"
Topic: Trade networks (Silk Roads, Indian Ocean, trans-Saharan) facilitating cultural exchange
Time period: 1200–1450 (Unit 1 exactly)
Key phrase: "across different regions" — You need multiple regions, not just one

2
Thesis

A strong thesis for "evaluate the extent" must make a judgment — not just "yes it did" or "no it didn't."

✅ Sample Thesis

"While trade networks between 1200 and 1450 were powerful vehicles for cultural exchange — spreading Islam along the Indian Ocean, transferring Chinese innovations across Eurasia via the Silk Roads, and transmitting West African wealth and Islamic learning through the trans-Saharan route — their transformative cultural impact was often limited to elites and port cities, and the degree of exchange varied significantly by region, route, and the openness of receiving societies."

Why This Works: It answers the "to what extent" question (significantly, but with important limitations) · Names three specific trade routes · Sets up a nuanced argument (exchange was real but limited) — not just "yes it did"

3
Contextualization Paragraph
📝 Sample Contextualization

"The post-classical period from 1200 to 1450 was defined by the growing complexity of long-distance trade networks that had been developing for centuries. The Silk Roads connecting China to the Mediterranean, the Indian Ocean routes linking East Africa to South and Southeast Asia, and the trans-Saharan caravan routes crossing the African continent all reached new levels of integration during this era. This expansion of trade was enabled by several key factors: Chinese innovations (the magnetic compass, paper money, and advanced shipbuilding) made long-distance maritime trade safer and more efficient; the camel, adopted by Saharan traders centuries earlier, made desert crossings manageable; and the political stability provided by the Abbasid Caliphate in the Islamic world had created a vast, connected network of merchants and scholars across Afro-Eurasia. It was within this context of expanding, increasingly interconnected trade networks that the cultural exchanges examined below took place."

4
Essay Outline (Organize Thematically, Not Document-by-Document)
  • Body ¶1 — The Indian Ocean Trade Route as a Vehicle for Cultural Exchange: Show how Islam spread along the Indian Ocean route to Southeast Asia via merchants and Sufis. Outside evidence: Delhi Sultanate; Swahili coast cities like Kilwa.
  • Body ¶2 — The Silk Roads and the Transfer of Chinese Innovations: Show how Chinese technologies (gunpowder, paper, printing, compass) spread westward. Outside evidence: The House of Wisdom translating Chinese texts; Al-Khwarizmi building on Indian mathematics.
  • Body ¶3 — The Trans-Saharan Route and West African Cultural Connections: Show how Islam spread to West Africa via trans-Saharan merchants and scholars. Outside evidence: Mali's adoption of Islamic law; University of Sankore in Timbuktu.
  • Complexity Paragraph (This is what makes high-scoring responses stand out): Acknowledge limits — cultural exchange was often uneven; elites benefited more than commoners; some societies resisted external cultural influences. Example: Despite Islamic merchants trading in Southeast Asia for centuries, conversion was slow and often superficial at first.
5
Scoring Breakdown

DBQ Scoring Rubric — Maximum 7 Points

1pt
ThesisMake a historically defensible claim that responds to the prompt and establishes a line of reasoning. Can appear in intro or conclusion.
1pt
ContextualizationWrite a full paragraph describing the broader historical context. Must be connected to the argument. Must go beyond simple description — explain the relevance.
2pts
Evidence: Document ContentUse content from at least 3 documents (1 pt). Use content from at least 6 of 7 documents (2nd pt). Accurately describe and use the documents.
1pt
Evidence: Beyond DocumentsProvide at least one piece of outside historical evidence (not in the documents) that is relevant to your argument.
1pt
Analysis: SourcingFor at least 3 documents, explain how the document's historical situation, purpose, point of view, or audience affects its meaning or reliability.
1pt
Analysis: ComplexityDemonstrate a complex understanding: explain both similarity AND difference, both continuity AND change, explain multiple causes/effects, OR connect to another time period. This is the hardest point to get — plan for it.
Write a sourcing statement for a hypothetical DBQ document: "Document 4 is an account by Ibn Battuta describing Islamic mosques in Southeast Asian port cities." How would you analyze this document's source?
MODEL SOURCING STATEMENT
"Ibn Battuta, as a devout Muslim scholar and traveler, would have been particularly attuned to evidence of Islamic practice and might have emphasized the presence of mosques and Islamic institutions in ways that a non-Muslim observer would not. His purpose in writing the Rihla was partly to document the spread and vitality of Islam across the world, which could lead him to highlight Islamic elements in Southeast Asia while underrepresenting local religious traditions that persisted alongside Islam. This means Document 4 may overstate the depth of Islamic conversion in the region while still serving as reliable evidence that mosque construction and Islamic communities did exist in these port cities by the 14th century."
Section 17

Sample LEQ — With Full Breakdown

📊 LEQ Basics

The Long Essay Question (LEQ) is worth 15% of your AP exam score. You get 40 minutes to write a full essay from memory — no documents. You choose from three prompts (one each from three different time periods). The LEQ tests whether you can construct a historical argument using your own knowledge.

"Compare the political structures of Song China and the Delhi Sultanate in the period c. 1200–1450."
1
Decoding the Prompt

Command term: "Compare" — You must address both similarities AND differences
Topic: Political structures (how they governed, what systems they used, who had power)
The two subjects: Song China and Delhi Sultanate
Time period: c. 1200–1450

2
Brainstorm Before You Write
SONG CHINA
  • Emperor rules as absolute monarch
  • Confucian civil service exam creates Scholar-Gentry class
  • Mandate of Heaven justifies rule
  • Centralized bureaucracy manages vast territory
  • State philosophy: Neo-Confucianism
  • Population: predominantly ethnically Han Chinese
DELHI SULTANATE
  • Sultan rules; often Turkish military general turned ruler
  • Military aristocrats (Turkish nobles) dominate administration
  • Islamic law (Sharia) justifies authority
  • More reliance on local Hindu vassals for governance
  • Official religion: Islam; governed a largely non-Muslim (Hindu) population
  • Ruling class: Turkish/Central Asian Muslims; ruled population: mostly Hindu
⚖️ Similarities (MUST include in your essay)
  • Both maintained hierarchical bureaucracies with educated administrative elites at the top
  • Both depended on agrarian economies and taxed agricultural surplus
  • Both used religious/philosophical frameworks to legitimize their rule (Neo-Confucianism / Islamic law)
  • Both governed multiethnic, diverse populations through a mixture of direct administration and local accommodation
3
Sample Thesis
✅ Sample Thesis

"Both Song China and the Delhi Sultanate maintained centralized administrative hierarchies and used cultural/religious frameworks to legitimize their authority, but they differed fundamentally in the basis of that authority: Song China relied on meritocratic Confucian civil service while the Delhi Sultanate drew legitimacy from Islamic law and military conquest over a largely non-Muslim population — revealing how the same structural need (maintaining power over a complex society) produced very different governmental forms depending on regional tradition and religion."

4
Sample Essay Outline
  • Introduction + Thesis: State your comparison and establish your argument
  • Contextualization: Post-classical period context — growing complexity of states, spread of world religions, Silk Road trade
  • Body ¶1 — SIMILARITIES: Both used administrative elites; both relied on religious legitimacy; both taxed agriculture
  • Body ¶2 — DIFFERENCES: Civil service exam (Song) vs. military aristocracy (Delhi); Confucian philosophy vs. Islamic law; ruling over ethnically unified vs. divided populations
  • Body ¶3 — COMPLEXITY: How these differences reflect broader patterns — China's long tradition of centralized bureaucracy vs. Delhi's nature as a conquest state governing a foreign population
  • Conclusion: Restate and extend the argument
5
LEQ Scoring Breakdown

LEQ Scoring Rubric — Maximum 6 Points

1pt
ThesisHistorically defensible claim that responds to prompt and establishes a line of reasoning. Must go beyond the prompt.
1pt
ContextualizationFull paragraph connecting the topic to a broader historical context. Must explain WHY the context is relevant, not just describe it.
2pts
EvidenceUse specific historical evidence (names, places, events, dates). Two or more pieces of accurate, relevant evidence earn 2 points.
2pts
Analysis & ReasoningDemonstrate comparison (point out both similarity AND difference), causation, or CCOT. Use the skill the prompt demands. Earn 2 points for sophisticated use of the skill with a complex argument.
Section 18

Unit Summary — The Big Picture

Before you close this guide, make sure you can answer all of these questions from memory. If you can answer all of them confidently, you are ready for whatever Unit 1 question the AP throws at you.

🏯 EAST ASIA — Can you answer these?
What is the dynastic cycle? What dynasty is China in during Unit 1?
MODEL ANSWER
The dynastic cycle is the pattern by which Chinese dynasties rise, rule, and fall. A dynasty earns the Mandate of Heaven (divine approval to rule) when it governs well — providing stability, controlling floods, and maintaining order. When it weakens through corruption, disasters, or revolt, it loses the Mandate and a new dynasty replaces it. In Unit 1, China begins under the Song Dynasty (ruling south China), which falls to the Mongols in 1279. The Mongols then establish the Yuan Dynasty under Kublai Khan for the rest of the period.
How does the civil service exam work? What class does it create?
MODEL ANSWER
The civil service exam (keju) tested candidates on Confucian classics through a rigorous multi-stage process. In theory anyone could sit for it, but it required years of expensive preparation, so it mostly benefited wealthy families. Those who passed earned government positions as bureaucrats. This system created the scholar-gentry class — an educated administrative elite whose status came from knowledge and exam success rather than birth alone. It made Chinese government more meritocratic (compared to Europe's hereditary nobility) and cemented Confucian values as the foundation of governance.
Name three Song innovations and explain why each mattered.
MODEL ANSWER
1. Gunpowder — Invented in China, initially for fireworks, then weaponized. Spread via the Silk Road and would eventually revolutionize warfare worldwide.

2. Printing (movable type) — Made books cheaper and more widely available, increasing literacy, spreading Confucian texts, and preserving knowledge at scale.

3. Magnetic compass — Enabled accurate maritime navigation, which fueled Song China's booming trade across the South China Sea and Indian Ocean.

Bonus worth knowing: Paper money (reduced the need to carry heavy coins), advanced iron production, and improved agricultural techniques that supported China's large population.
What is Neo-Confucianism? What are its core values?
MODEL ANSWER
Neo-Confucianism is a revival and philosophical deepening of Confucianism developed during the Song Dynasty, associated with thinkers like Zhu Xi. It blended classical Confucian ethics with metaphysical ideas borrowed from Buddhism and Daoism. Core values include: filial piety (deep respect for parents and ancestors), social hierarchy (the Five Relationships — ruler/subject, parent/child, husband/wife, elder/younger, friend/friend), loyalty to the state, and self-cultivation through education. Despite borrowing from Buddhism, Neo-Confucians often criticized Buddhism as selfish and world-denying. Neo-Confucianism spread beyond China to become the state ideology of Korea, Japan, and Vietnam.
🕌 DAR AL-ISLAM — Can you answer these?
What was the Abbasid Caliphate and when did it fall?
MODEL ANSWER
The Abbasid Caliphate was the third great Islamic empire, ruling from Baghdad beginning in 750 CE. It succeeded the Umayyad Caliphate and represented the peak of Islamic political and cultural power — this is when the Islamic Golden Age flourished, centered on Baghdad's House of Wisdom. The Abbasid Caliphate fell in 1258 CE when the Mongols under Hulagu Khan sacked Baghdad, executed the caliph (reportedly by rolling him in a carpet and trampling him), and destroyed the city. This ended centralized Islamic political authority and was one of the most devastating events in medieval Islamic history.
Who are the Sufis and how did they spread Islam?
MODEL ANSWER
Sufis are Islamic mystics who seek a personal, direct experience of God through prayer, music, poetry, and spiritual discipline — think of them as the "heart" of Islam versus the "law" of orthodox Islam. They spread Islam by: (1) Traveling as missionaries who adapted to local cultures rather than demanding strict conformity; (2) Founding Sufi orders (brotherhoods) that established communities across Asia and Africa; (3) Using music, dance, and storytelling that appealed to ordinary people emotionally. Their tolerant, inclusive approach allowed locals to convert while maintaining many cultural practices, making Islam accessible in South Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, and Central Asia.
Name three intellectual contributions of the Islamic Golden Age.
MODEL ANSWER
1. Mathematics — Al-Khwarizmi developed algebra (the word "algebra" comes from the Arabic al-jabr) and introduced Hindu-Arabic numerals to the wider world. The word "algorithm" comes from his name.

2. Medicine — Ibn Sina (Avicenna) wrote the Canon of Medicine, the standard medical textbook in both the Islamic world and Europe for centuries. It synthesized Greek, Persian, and Islamic medical knowledge.

3. Preservation and translation — Islamic scholars translated and preserved Greek texts (Aristotle, Plato, Euclid) that Europe had lost after Rome's fall. These translations later flowed back into Europe, fueling the Renaissance.

Also worth knowing: Ibn al-Haytham's optics (how the eye sees), advanced astronomical charts, and geographic mapping.
What is the House of Wisdom and why does it matter?
MODEL ANSWER
The House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikmah) was a major intellectual center in Baghdad founded by the Abbasid Caliphate around 830 CE. Scholars from across the Islamic world — and from Greek, Persian, and Indian traditions — gathered there to translate, study, and advance knowledge in mathematics, astronomy, philosophy, medicine, and more. It matters because it was the intellectual engine of the Golden Age: Greek knowledge was translated and expanded there, laying the groundwork for scientific and philosophical advances that would later reach Europe. It was destroyed when the Mongols sacked Baghdad in 1258, symbolizing the end of the Abbasid Golden Age.
🌎 THE REST — Can you answer these?
How did the Aztecs use chinampas? How did they maintain their empire?
MODEL ANSWER
Chinampas are artificial floating islands built in the shallow waters of Lake Texcoco. The Aztecs layered mud, aquatic vegetation, and sediment to create highly fertile agricultural plots capable of producing multiple harvests per year — feeding the massive urban population of Tenochtitlan (200,000+). The Aztec Empire was maintained through: (1) The tribute system — conquered peoples paid food, textiles, jade, cacao, and sacrificial victims to Tenochtitlan; (2) Military power and fear of reconquest; (3) The Triple Alliance with Texcoco and Tlacopan that distributed military obligations. Unlike some empires, the Aztecs often allowed local rulers to stay in power as long as tribute kept flowing.
What is the mit'a system? What was the Inca road network for?
MODEL ANSWER
The mit'a was a labor tax: all Inca subjects owed the state a fixed number of days of labor per year. In return, the state provided food, tools, and clothing during their service period. Mit'a labor built temples, terraced fields, roads, and royal palaces, and staffed the military. There was no money in the Inca economy — the mit'a was how the state got things done.

The Inca road network (Qhapaq Ñan) stretched ~25,000 miles through the Andes — one of the greatest engineering feats of the pre-Columbian world. It served: (1) Military — rapid troop movement to suppress rebellions across a vast, mountainous empire; (2) Economic — transporting tribute goods to the capital Cusco; (3) Communication — relay runners (chasquis) carried messages and quipus across the empire within days.
Who was Mansa Musa and what happened when he went on hajj?
MODEL ANSWER
Mansa Musa (ruled c. 1312–1337) was the emperor of the Mali Empire in West Africa, one of the wealthiest rulers in world history. Mali controlled the trans-Saharan gold and salt trade routes, making him almost incomprehensibly rich. In 1324–1325, he made his hajj (Islamic pilgrimage to Mecca) with an entourage of ~60,000 people, 12,000 servants, 500 slaves carrying golden staffs, and dozens of camels loaded with gold dust. He gave away so much gold in Cairo and along the route that he caused serious inflation, crashing the value of gold in Egypt for over a decade. His hajj had two major consequences: it announced West Africa's wealth to the Islamic world and Europe (Mali appeared on European maps afterward), and it deepened Mali's connections to the wider Islamic trade network.
What is feudalism? What is manorialism? Why was Europe decentralized?
MODEL ANSWER
Feudalism is the political and military system: lords granted land (fiefs) to vassals in exchange for military service and loyalty. The hierarchy ran roughly: king → great nobles → lesser lords → knights. It was a system of mutual obligation, not absolute central authority.

Manorialism is the economic system of the manor (estate): peasants (serfs) were legally bound to the land and owed their lord labor and dues; in return, the lord provided land use and protection. These are two different systems that operated together — feudalism explains political power, manorialism explains the economy.

Why was Europe decentralized? Three reasons: (1) The Western Roman Empire collapsed in 476 CE, destroying roads, centralized administration, and stable currency; (2) Repeated invasions (Vikings from the north, Magyars from the east, Saracens from the south) forced local self-defense; (3) Without a strong central state to collect taxes and pay armies, power fragmented to local lords who could actually provide physical protection.
What is the Bhakti movement? How does it compare to Sufism?
MODEL ANSWER
The Bhakti movement was a Hindu devotional movement (c. 7th–17th centuries) that emphasized direct, personal love of God — bypassing priests, rituals, and caste distinctions. Devotees worshiped their chosen deity (Vishnu, Shiva, etc.) through poetry, hymns, and emotional devotion. It was explicitly anti-hierarchical: saints came from low castes and preached that devotion, not birth, was what mattered to God.

Comparison with Sufism:
✅ Similar: Both emphasized personal emotional connection to the divine; both bypassed religious hierarchy; both spread through music and charismatic teachers; both were inclusive across social class.
❌ Different: Bhakti is rooted in Hinduism, Sufism in Islam. In India, they interacted significantly — their cross-pollination influenced syncretic traditions like early Sikhism, which drew from both. On the AP exam, they're often paired as parallel movements showing how popular religious movements respond to rigid hierarchy.

One-Paragraph Summary of Unit 1

Unit 1 (1200–1450 CE) is a global snapshot — a world tour of six regions, each with distinct political systems, belief frameworks, and economic structures. Song China dominates East Asia with the world's most powerful economy, a sophisticated civil service, and transformative technologies that will ripple outward for centuries. Dar al-Islam stretches across Afro-Eurasia, fragmented politically after the Mongols destroy the Abbasid Caliphate, but culturally vibrant — preserving and innovating ancient knowledge while spreading Islam via trade, conquest, and Sufi missionaries. South and Southeast Asia is a religious battleground between Hinduism, Buddhism, and the arriving Islam, with the Delhi Sultanate bringing Islamic rule to northern India. The Americas develop independently, with the Aztecs building an urban empire in Mesoamerica on chinampas and tribute, and the Incas building a mountain empire in the Andes on roads and mit'a labor. Africa's Mali Empire generates enormous wealth from gold-salt trade, its ruler Mansa Musa stunning the world with his legendary hajj. And Europe, still fragmented and feudal after the collapse of Rome, lags far behind — though the Crusades begin slowly reconnecting it to the wider world. These six regions are the threads of the tapestry; Unit 2 shows how they weave together.

📋 FINAL REVIEW — Which statement best summarizes the key theme of Unit 1?
Checkpoint

Do You Think You Can Move On?

This is not a trick question and there is no wrong answer. The goal is honest self-assessment — the kind of reflection that actually makes studying more efficient. Before you move to Unit 2, take a moment and write it out.

Rate your Unit 1 confidence 1–10. Then describe: what feels solid? What still feels fuzzy? Be specific — name the topics, not just "some stuff."
WHAT "READY" MEANS FOR AP WORLD HISTORY
Ready does not mean you can recite every detail perfectly. It means you can do these things without looking anything up:

✅ Core "Can Do" Checklist:

East Asia: Explain the civil service exam and why it mattered. Name 3 Song innovations and explain each one's significance. Define Neo-Confucianism and its values.

Dar al-Islam: Explain what happened in 1258 and why it matters. Name 3 ways Islam spread and give a regional example of each. Name 2 Islamic scholars and what they contributed.

The Americas: Explain chinampas and the tribute system for the Aztecs. Explain the mit'a system and quipus for the Incas. Know why both developed independently from Afro-Eurasia.

Africa: Explain why Mansa Musa's hajj was historically significant (not just "he was rich"). Know what made Mali wealthy. Know what Timbuktu was.

Europe: Explain the difference between feudalism and manorialism. Explain why Europe was decentralized. Know the Catholic Church's role.

Essays: Write a real thesis (not a topic sentence). Know what contextualization actually means. Know the difference between "identify," "describe," and "explain."

If you can do most of those, you are ready. The fuzzy spots will sharpen as you move forward and see Unit 1 concepts reappear in Units 2–4. You do not need to master everything before moving on — you need to understand it well enough that nothing in Unit 2 surprises you completely.
Section 19

Recommended Resources

Use these resources to supplement your studying. Different formats work for different people — try a few and see what helps the information stick best for you.

Primary Resources (Start Here)

ResourceWhat It Is & How to Use It
AP World History CED (Course and Exam Description) The official College Board document that lists every learning objective, skill, and key concept for the entire course. This is the bible — if it's in the CED, it can be on the exam. Download it free from AP Classroom.
AP Classroom College Board's own platform with assigned practice questions, progress checks, and official AP exam practice. Your teacher may already assign this. The Unit 1 progress check is essential.
Freeman-pedia (YouTube) The review video used as a foundation for this guide. Short, focused, exam-targeted. Watch the Unit 1 review video before your test. His Ultimate Review Packet has practice quizzes and study guide keys.
AMSCO AP World History The most commonly used AP World History textbook. Dense but very well organized by unit. Read the Unit 1 chapter to supplement your class notes.
Princeton Review / Barron's Commercial test prep books. Use their Unit 1 chapters for condensed summaries and practice questions. The practice tests are especially useful.
Khan Academy — AP World History Free video lessons and practice exercises. The "Medieval" section covers most of Unit 1. Good for visual learners who want another explanation of key concepts.
Fiveable AP World study guides and live review sessions. Their Unit 1 guide is excellent for quick summaries and flashcard-style review. Also has live review sessions before the AP exam.
Quizlet Search "AP World History Unit 1" for pre-made flashcard sets. Useful for drilling vocabulary and key facts. Also great for the glossary terms in this guide.

Primary Sources Worth Knowing

The AP exam uses primary source documents. Knowing a few real primary sources for Unit 1 gives you an edge because you can reference them as outside evidence in essays.

  • The Rihla by Ibn Battuta — The travel account of the 14th century Moroccan scholar. Covers Dar al-Islam, sub-Saharan Africa, India, and China. One of the most important primary sources for both Unit 1 and Unit 2.
  • The Travels of Marco Polo — The account of the Venetian merchant's time at Kublai Khan's court in Yuan China. Gives European readers their first detailed description of Chinese civilization.
  • The Sundiata Epic — The oral epic about Sundiata Keita, the founder of the Mali Empire. Shows how African states built and justified political power through epic narrative tradition.
  • Works of Al-Khwarizmi and Ibn Sina — Mathematical and medical texts from the Islamic Golden Age. Evidence of Dar al-Islam's intellectual contributions.
🎓 You Are Ready for Unit 1

You know the six regions. You know the key states, people, and belief systems. You know what caused things and what they led to. You know the skills. You have a full toolkit for any Unit 1 essay.

Now go build that foundation and move on to Unit 2. The trade routes are waiting.

Essay Writing Hub: DBQ, LEQ & SAQ Mastery

📌 This Section Covers ALL Units

The DBQ, LEQ, and SAQ skills here apply to every unit. Bookmark this page and return to it before each major essay you practice. These techniques are the difference between a 3 and a 5.

For the full DBQ and LEQ breakdowns with Unit 1 examples, see Sections 16 and 17 inside Unit 1. This page is your quick reference for every essay type — use it any time you sit down to write.

The 5-Minute Pre-Essay Checklist

Before you write a single word of body, run through this every time:

1
Decode the Command Term

Is it "compare," "evaluate the extent," "explain," "analyze"? Each demands something different. Write the command term at the top of your scratch paper and remind yourself what it requires.

2
Identify the Topic, Time Period, and Scope

Underline the geographic scope, the time period, and the topic. Any essay that misreads the scope loses points immediately.

3
Brainstorm Evidence First (2 minutes)

Before writing your thesis, jot down every piece of evidence you know related to the prompt. Then build your argument around your strongest evidence — not the other way around.

4
Write a Real Thesis (Not a Topic Sentence)

Your thesis must make a historically specific, defensible claim. Test it: could someone reasonably disagree with it? If not, it's probably too obvious.

5
Plan Your Complexity Point

The hardest point on any rubric is complexity. Plan it before you start writing. Options: explain a nuance/exception, connect to another time period, explain both change AND continuity, show a counterargument.

Contextualization — The Point Students Miss Most

Contextualization is worth 1 point on every essay rubric, and students consistently miss it because they confuse it with "background information." It is not the same thing.

❌
NOT Contextualization: "Song China was a powerful dynasty that existed from 960 to 1279 CE." (This is just background — no connection to the argument.)
✅
IS Contextualization: "The post-classical period (1200–1450) was defined by the expansion of long-distance trade networks that connected Afro-Eurasia in unprecedented ways. It was within this context of growing commercial integration — fueled by Chinese innovations like the compass and paper money, and facilitated by the relative political stability of the Abbasid Caliphate — that the developments examined in this essay took place."
📐 The Contextualization Formula

[Broad historical trend or development] + [specific connection to why it matters for THIS prompt]. The connection is what earns the point. "This happened before" is not a connection — "this broader context directly shaped X because Y" is.

Sourcing Documents (DBQ Only)

For every document in your DBQ, identify at least one of these four sourcing lenses. You need to do this explicitly for at least 3 documents to earn the sourcing point.

LensWhat to AskHow to Use It
Historical Situation What was happening historically when this was created? "Written in the aftermath of the Mongol sack of Baghdad, this document reflects widespread fear across Dar al-Islam, which likely exaggerates the scale of destruction to convey the emotional weight."
Audience Who is this for? How does that shape what's included or left out? "Written for a European audience unfamiliar with East Asia, Marco Polo emphasized the exotic wealth of China — which may cause him to exaggerate or sensationalize for dramatic effect."
Purpose Why was this created? What was the author trying to accomplish? "As a religious text meant to inspire Muslim pilgrims, this account of the hajj emphasizes spiritual transformation, which may cause it to underrepresent the commercial and diplomatic dimensions of the pilgrimage."
Point of View Who is the author and what perspective do they bring? "As a Turkish sultan writing about his conquest of northern India, this author has strong incentive to present the Delhi Sultanate's rule as legitimate, potentially minimizing resistance."

SAQ — Short Answer Question Guide

📊 SAQ Basics

SAQs are worth 20% of your AP exam score. You answer 3 SAQs in 40 minutes. Each SAQ has 3 parts (a, b, c), each worth 1 point. No thesis required — but use specific evidence and explain your reasoning. Each part should be 3–5 sentences.

The SAQ formula for each part:

C
Claim

Start with a direct, specific answer to the question. Don't hedge — just answer it.

E
Evidence

Name a specific historical example. Use specific names, dates, events — not vague generalities.

X
Explanation

Explain HOW your evidence supports your claim. This is the "so what" — don't leave the reader to figure out the connection.

✅ SAQ Practice — Unit 1 Example

Prompt: "Describe one way Islam spread into a new region between 1200 and 1450."

Strong response: "Islam spread into Southeast Asia primarily through trade and merchant contact along Indian Ocean routes. Muslim merchants from the Arabian Peninsula and India established trading posts in port cities in modern-day Indonesia and Malaysia. As they settled in these communities, they built mosques and modeled Islamic practice for local populations. Because this spread was peaceful and commercial rather than military, local rulers sometimes converted to Islam as a way of strengthening trade relationships — making conversion an economic and political decision as much as a spiritual one."

This response earns full credit: specific claim, specific evidence (Indian Ocean routes, port cities in Indonesia and Malaysia), and explains the mechanism (commercial motivation for conversion).

📚 Full Essay Practice

For complete DBQ and LEQ examples with sample theses, outlines, and scoring breakdowns, go to Sections 16 and 17 inside Unit 1. Practice writing at least one LEQ and one DBQ under timed conditions before your exam.

🌐 Unit 1 gave you the puzzle pieces. Unit 2 shows how they connect. Take it one trade route at a time.
Unit 02 · c. 1200–1450 CE

Networks of Exchange

02 Indian Ocean Trade
03 Trans-Saharan Trade
04 The Mongol Empire
05 Glossary
★ Part 1 Checkpoint

06 Consequences: Culture

08 Famous Travelers
09 Causes & Effects
10 Timeline
11 Thinking Skills
11b Command Terms
12 Common Pitfalls
13 Sample DBQ
14 Sample LEQ
★★ Unit 2 Summary
AP World History Modern · Unit 2

Networks of Exchange  |  c. 1200–1450 CE

⚖️ Exam Weight
8–10% of the AP Exam. Same time period as Unit 1. Together, Units 1 and 2 own the entire post-classical period. Master both and you've locked down 16–20% of your exam.

What Is This Unit About?

If Unit 1 was a world tour — checking in on each region to see what was happening around 1200 CE — then Unit 2 is about how all those regions are connected to each other. Unit 1 gave you the individual puzzle pieces. Unit 2 shows you how those pieces fit together.

The connection between regions happened through trade. In 1200–1450, three major networks did that connecting:

  • The Silk Roads — overland routes connecting East Asia through Central Asia to Dar al-Islam and Europe
  • The Indian Ocean Trade Network — maritime routes connecting East Africa, the Middle East, South Asia, and Southeast Asia
  • The Trans-Saharan Trade — camel caravan routes crossing the Sahara, connecting sub-Saharan West Africa to North Africa and Dar al-Islam

On top of those trade routes, there is one enormous wildcard: the Mongol Empire. The Mongols explode out of Central Asia in the early 1200s, use those same trade routes to build the largest land empire in history, and are simultaneously the most destructive force of the period AND one of the biggest drivers of cross-cultural exchange. You need to understand both sides of that story.

📐 The Unit 2 Formula — Write This Down

3 Trade Routes  +  The Mongols  +  Consequences of Trade  = Unit 2

Every vocab word, empire, traveler, and disease in this unit fits into one of those four categories. This unit is about connectivity, and connectivity has consequences — good (ideas spread) and catastrophic (so does disease).

Which of the following best describes the central theme of Unit 2?
Section 01

The Silk Roads — The World's Most Famous Trade Network

⭐ HIGH PRIORITY — AP Loves the Silk Roads

The Silk Roads are the most famous trade network in world history — and for good reason. But here is your first AP vocabulary trap: it's not "the Silk Road." It's "the Silk Roads." Plural.

📌 It's the Silk ROADS, Not the Silk Road

The Silk Roads were not one single highway. They were a network — multiple branching routes connecting dozens of major cities across thousands of miles. Some routes went through Central Asian mountain passes; others skirted deserts; others went through the Persian heartland. Merchants almost never traveled the entire route from end to end. Instead, goods changed hands many times — moving from one regional merchant to the next, all the way from China to Europe. Saying "the Silk Road" on the AP exam signals shallow understanding. Say "the Silk Roads" or "the Silk Road network."

The Silk Roads began around 100 BCE when the Han Dynasty of China sent diplomats westward looking for military allies. Those missions opened up trade routes that gradually connected East Asia to Central Asia, Persia, the Middle East, and eventually Europe. By 1200 CE, these routes had been operating for over a thousand years — and under the Mongols (Unit 2's wildcard), they would reach their peak volume of use.

What Was Actually Traded?

The network was named for silk — China's most prized export — but silk was just the most glamorous item on a long list:

RegionExportedImported
China (Song/Yuan)Silk, porcelain, paper, paper money, iron goods, gunpowderHorses, precious metals, glass, wool
Central Asia (hubs)Horses, furs, hides; mainly middlemenLuxury goods from east and west
Dar al-IslamCarpets, glassware, textiles, mathematical knowledge, horsesChinese silk and porcelain, Indian spices
EuropeSilver, wool, fur, timberSilk, spices, porcelain, dyes — consumers of eastern luxuries
💡 Paper Money and Financial Innovation on the Silk Roads

One of the most underappreciated things traveling the Silk Roads was not a physical good — it was the concept of paper money. Song China had introduced paper currency to make large-scale commerce easier. For merchants moving goods across thousands of miles, carrying paper instead of heavy bags of coins was revolutionary. The Silk Roads also saw the use of bills of exchange — early credit instruments where a merchant could deposit money in one city and collect equivalent value in another, eliminating the danger of transporting cash through bandit-prone routes. This is proto-banking. It lays the groundwork for the financial systems we use today.

The Caravanserai — The Silk Roads' Most Important Institution

If you remember only one vocabulary word from the Silk Roads, make it this one: the caravanserai. Break it down: a caravan is a group of merchants traveling together for safety; sarai is a Persian word for "inn." So a caravanserai is literally a "caravan inn."

🏰 What a Caravanserai Actually Looked Like
  • A large walled compound with a single defensible entrance gate
  • Inside: stalls for animals (camels, horses, donkeys), rooms for merchants, storage for goods, a well for water
  • Often included a mosque, bath house, and market stalls — making them mini commercial and cultural centers
  • Built approximately one day's caravan journey apart, so merchants always had a safe place to overnight
  • Often free to use — funded by rulers or merchants to encourage trade that benefited everyone
  • Merchants from dozens of different cultures would meet here: caravanserais were the AP's best example of Silk Roads cultural exchange happening in real time

Key Silk Road Cities

CityLocationWhy It Matters
SamarkandModern Uzbekistan (Central Asia)The AP's #1 Silk Road city. Crossroads of routes between China, Persia, and Europe. Cosmopolitan hub where merchants, scholars, and diplomats met for centuries. Marco Polo passed through.
KashgarWestern China / modern XinjiangThe eastern gateway between China and Central Asia. Chinese merchants typically didn't travel further west — they sold goods here to Central Asian middlemen.
BaghdadModern Iraq (Dar al-Islam)Abbasid capital, Silk Road terminus, and intellectual hub. Home of the House of Wisdom (destroyed 1258 by Mongols). Even after Abbasid collapse, remained a crucial trade city.
ConstantinopleModern IstanbulWestern terminus of the Silk Roads. Goods reaching here were sold to Italian merchants (Venice, Genoa) for resale to European buyers.

The Italian City-States — Europe's Silk Road Connection

Most of Europe was too far away to be a major Silk Road player. The exception: the Italian city-states, particularly Venice and Genoa. These maritime republics had trading colonies throughout the Mediterranean and Black Sea and served as the crucial link between eastern goods and European buyers. The profits from this trade funded the Italian Renaissance. When historians ask why the Renaissance happened in Italy, part of the answer is: silk and spices.

What is THE single most important institution of the Silk Road trade system — a roadside inn providing shelter, water, security, and cultural exchange for merchants?
Fill in: A merchant deposits his silver with a banker in Samarkand and receives a written document allowing him to collect equivalent value in Baghdad. This financial instrument is called a _____ of exchange.
Which Italian city-states served as the primary commercial link between the eastern Silk Road network and European markets?
In your own words, explain WHY the Silk Roads should be called "roads" (plural) and not "the Silk Road." Why does this distinction matter for AP essays?
MODEL ANSWER
The "Silk Roads" were a network of many different overland routes — not one single highway. Multiple branching paths connected China to Central Asia, the Middle East, and eventually Europe, with different routes passing through different cities, mountain passes, and deserts. Merchants almost never traveled the entire length; goods changed hands many times along the way. The distinction matters for AP essays because calling it "the Silk Road" suggests a simple, direct connection between China and Europe — which is wrong. Saying "the Silk Roads" signals that you understand the trade operated through layers of middlemen, multiple routes, and a sprawling geographic system.

📝 Term Check — The Silk Roads

In your own words: What were the Silk Roads and why does the AP care?
AP EXPECTS
A network (not one road!) of overland trade routes connecting China to the Mediterranean via Central Asia. Named for China's silk exports but carried far more: porcelain, paper, gunpowder, spices, paper money, religions, ideas, and diseases. Active from ~100 BCE; reached peak integration during the Pax Mongolica. AP significance: they are the primary vehicle for cross-Eurasian cultural exchange in this period — understanding what traveled the Silk Roads (not just goods, but religions and technologies) is what earns points on essays.
In your own words: What was a Caravanserai and why does the AP care?
AP EXPECTS
A roadside inn/fortress built at roughly one-day's-journey intervals along the Silk Roads. Provided shelter, food, water, and security for merchant caravans. Also included mosques, bath houses, and market stalls — making them mini cultural exchange centers. THE key institution of the Silk Road trade system. AP significance: caravanserais are the concrete example of how Silk Road infrastructure enabled both commerce AND cultural exchange. Use it whenever a prompt asks about how the Silk Roads functioned or what made overland trade possible.
In your own words: What was a Bill of Exchange?
AP EXPECTS
An early credit instrument used by merchants along the Silk Roads. A merchant deposits gold with a banker in one city and receives a written bill. That bill can be redeemed for equivalent value at a partner bank in another city — eliminating the need to carry dangerous quantities of cash across bandit-prone routes. AP significance: bills of exchange are evidence that financial innovation spread along trade routes just like physical goods did. They represent proto-banking — the conceptual ancestor of modern banking and credit systems.
In your own words: What were the Italian City-States and why did they matter?
AP EXPECTS
Particularly Venice and Genoa — maritime trading republics that served as the primary commercial link between the eastern Silk Road network and European markets. Had trading colonies throughout the Mediterranean and Black Sea. Enormous profits from buying eastern goods cheaply and reselling them at markup in Europe funded the Italian Renaissance. AP significance: they show that Europe's intellectual flowering was directly financed by trade profits from the Silk Roads.
In your own words: What was Samarkand and why does the AP care about it?
AP EXPECTS
Located in modern Uzbekistan in Central Asia — the AP's go-to example of a Silk Road trade city. Positioned at the crossroads of routes between China, Persia, and Europe. A cosmopolitan hub for centuries where merchants, scholars, and diplomats from across Afro-Eurasia met and exchanged goods and ideas. Marco Polo passed through it. Use Samarkand whenever a prompt asks for a specific example of a Silk Road city — it's the safest, most AP-recognized choice.
In your own words: What was a Caravan and why did merchants travel in them?
AP EXPECTS
A caravan is a group of merchants traveling together along overland trade routes — primarily for safety. Strength in numbers deterred bandits, who would think twice before attacking a group of dozens or hundreds of armed travelers. The camel was the backbone of overland caravans, especially for the trans-Saharan route. The caravan is to the Silk Roads what the monsoon season is to the Indian Ocean — it's the organizational unit that made the whole system function. AP significance: caravans are why caravanserais existed. They also explain why goods changed hands along the way — most merchants only traveled one leg of the journey, passing goods off to the next caravan at a hub city like Samarkand.
In your own words: What was Kashgar and what role did it play in Silk Road commerce?
AP EXPECTS
Located in western China (modern Xinjiang), Kashgar was the eastern gateway between China and Central Asia. It's where Chinese merchants typically stopped — they sold their goods here to Central Asian middlemen who then carried them westward. This is a crucial AP detail: it shows that the Silk Roads operated through layers of middlemen, not end-to-end merchants. No single merchant traveled from China to Rome. Kashgar represents the handoff point between China's commercial world and Central Asia's. This is why the "Silk Road" singular is a misleading term — Kashgar alone proves the network was a relay system, not a direct highway.
In your own words: What was Baghdad's role in the Silk Roads — and what happened to it?
AP EXPECTS
Baghdad (in modern Iraq) was the Abbasid capital and a major Silk Road terminus and intellectual hub. It was home to the House of Wisdom — the greatest library and translation center of the medieval world. Silk Road goods, scholars, and diplomats flowed through it. In 1258, Hulagu Khan's Mongol forces sacked and destroyed the city, killed the Caliph, and burned the House of Wisdom. Even after this catastrophic destruction, Baghdad remained a significant trade city — showing the durability of trade infrastructure even after political collapse. AP significance: Baghdad appears in both Unit 1 (as an Islamic intellectual center) and Unit 2 (as a Silk Road terminus and the site of the Mongols' most devastating destruction).
In your own words: What was Constantinople's role in the Silk Road system?
AP EXPECTS
Constantinople (modern Istanbul, Turkey) was the western terminus of the Silk Roads — the last major stopping point before goods entered the European market. Eastern goods arriving in Constantinople were purchased by Italian merchants (primarily from Venice and Genoa) who had permanent trading colonies there. Those merchants then resold the goods to European buyers at enormous markups. AP significance: Constantinople is the bridge between the Silk Road world and Europe, and its position explains why Italian city-states (not France or England) became the richest states in Europe during this period. It also sets up Unit 4 — when the Ottoman Turks seize Constantinople in 1453, European merchants begin desperately seeking alternative sea routes to Asia, directly driving the Age of Exploration.
📋 SECTION REVIEW — Silk Roads. Which of the following best explains why goods were rarely transported by a single merchant from China all the way to Europe?
Section 02

The Indian Ocean Trade Network — The World's Greatest Maritime Highway

⭐ HIGHEST PRIORITY — AP Underweights This; Don't Make That Mistake

The Indian Ocean trade network is arguably even more important than the Silk Roads in terms of sheer volume of goods moved and breadth of cultural exchange. While the Silk Roads crossed some of the world's harshest terrain, the Indian Ocean offered a maritime highway connecting East Africa, Arabia, the Persian Gulf, South Asia, Southeast Asia, and China. By 1200, it was the world's premier trading network — and it remained so until European ships entered the Indian Ocean in the late 1400s (Unit 4).

⚠️ The AP Underweights the Indian Ocean — But You Shouldn't

Students and teachers often focus disproportionately on the Silk Roads because European merchants (Marco Polo) participated in them. The Indian Ocean trade was actually larger in volume and more significant in cultural impact. If your essay only discusses the Silk Roads and ignores the Indian Ocean, you are missing the more important story. Balance your attention — if anything, lean into the Indian Ocean.

The Secret Weapon: Monsoon Winds

Understanding the Indian Ocean trade network is impossible without understanding the monsoon winds. These are seasonal wind patterns created by the differential heating of land and ocean.

🌬️ The Monsoon System — Nature's Trade Schedule

Summer Monsoon (May–September): Winds blow from the southwest — from Africa toward India and Southeast Asia. Ships sailing northeast rode this wind from East Africa or Arabia to India.

Winter Monsoon (November–March): Winds reverse and blow from the northeast — back from India toward Africa and Arabia. Ships rode this wind home.

Why this matters: The monsoons created a predictable, reliable schedule for maritime trade. Merchants knew when to sail east and when to sail west. This predictability made long-distance ocean voyages manageable and regular — not death-defying gambles. Without the monsoons, the Indian Ocean trade network could not have functioned the way it did.

Ship Technologies That Made It Possible

  • Lateen Sail: Triangular sails that could be angled to catch wind from the side — not just from directly behind. Gave sailors much better ability to navigate when wind wasn't perfectly at their backs.
  • Dhow: The dominant Arab and East African ship. Triangular lateen sail, shallow draft for coastal navigation, planks sewn with rope instead of nails. Ideally suited to Indian Ocean conditions.
  • Magnetic Compass: Invented in China, spread via trade routes. Allowed direction-finding even when stars were hidden. Critical for open-ocean navigation.
  • Astrolabe: Allowed sailors to determine their latitude by measuring the angle of the sun or stars above the horizon. Developed in the classical world, refined by Islamic astronomers, spread to sailors across the Indian Ocean.
Fill in: The seasonal wind patterns that reverse direction twice per year — enabling a reliable trade schedule across the Indian Ocean — are called _____ winds.
The lateen sail was significant because it:

What Was Traded?

RegionExportedImported
East Africa (Swahili Coast)Gold, ivory, enslaved people, iron, copper, animal hidesPorcelain, silk, cotton textiles, glass beads from India and China
Arabia / Persian GulfHorses, dates, pearls, frankincense, textilesIndian spices, African gold and ivory, Chinese porcelain
India (South Asia)Cotton textiles, spices (pepper, ginger, cardamom), indigo, precious stonesAfrican gold, Arabian horses, Chinese silk and porcelain
Southeast AsiaSpices (nutmeg, cloves, cinnamon — found ONLY here), tropical woods, rice, tinIndian cotton, Chinese silk and porcelain, Islamic knowledge and religion
ChinaSilk, porcelain, tea, paperHorses, spices, precious metals, tropical goods

The Diaspora Community — The Indian Ocean's Key Concept

If the Silk Roads have the caravanserai as their key institution, the Indian Ocean has the diaspora community as its key social concept.

Along Indian Ocean trade routes, merchants didn't just sell goods and leave. They established permanent communities in foreign ports. A Muslim Arab merchant trading regularly with Indian port cities might eventually settle there with his family, build a mosque, and create a small community of fellow Muslim traders. Over generations, these communities became permanent features of port cities — translating languages, brokering deals, and spreading religion, food, and culture into the host society.

🌍 Why Diaspora Communities Mattered
  • They served as translators, cultural brokers, and trusted middlemen between foreign merchants and local populations
  • They spread their religion, language, customs, and cuisine into the host society — often creating hybrid cultures
  • The four largest Muslim-majority populations on Earth today (Indonesia, Pakistan, India, Bangladesh) are all in countries along the Indian Ocean trade routes — a direct legacy of Muslim merchant diaspora communities
  • The Swahili culture of East Africa is itself a product of diaspora: a blend of Bantu African and Arab/Persian cultures that developed over centuries of merchant contact
The most important social institution of the Indian Ocean trade network was:

Key Indian Ocean Trade Cities

CityLocationWhy It Matters
Malacca (Melaka)Strait of Malacca, SE Asia (modern Malaysia)The AP's #1 Indian Ocean city. Controls the narrow strait between the Indian Ocean and South China Sea — the chokepoint all ships had to pass through. Became an Islamic sultanate in the 1400s, showing how trade spread Islam. Sets up Unit 4 when Portugal seizes it in 1511.
KilwaEast African coast (modern Tanzania)The dominant Swahili Coast city-state. Hugely wealthy trading hub connecting African gold and ivory to the Indian Ocean network. Ibn Battuta visited and called it one of the most beautiful cities in the world.
Calicut (Kozhikode)Southwest India (Kerala)One of the most important spice trading ports. Vasco da Gama arrived here in 1498 — the beginning of European intrusion into Indian Ocean trade (Unit 4).
