Name all five Enlightenment thinkers and their key idea. Then explain: how did the Enlightenment make political revolutions possible?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
The five thinkers and their ideas:
Locke: Natural rights (life, liberty, property) — governments exist to protect these; if they fail, people have the right to overthrow them.
Rousseau: Social Contract and popular sovereignty — legitimate government rests on the consent of the governed; "man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains."
Voltaire: Free speech, religion, press; critique of church authority and religious intolerance.
Montesquieu: Separation of powers into branches with checks and balances to prevent tyranny.
Adam Smith: Free market capitalism; laissez-faire; the "invisible hand" of supply and demand; mercantilism is replaced.

How Enlightenment made revolution possible: Before the Enlightenment, most people accepted the existing political order as natural or divinely ordained. Enlightenment thinkers gave people an intellectual framework to critique and challenge that order: if governments exist to protect natural rights (Locke), and if those governments fail to do so, then the people have not just the right but the duty to change them. The AP key: you can't have a revolution until people believe the current order is wrong AND that change is possible — Enlightenment ideas provided both.

The Three Required Documents

📌 The College Board Explicitly Names These Three. Know ALL of Them.
1. Declaration of Independence (1776) — United States

Written primarily by Thomas Jefferson. Declared American independence using Locke's framework: all men are created equal, endowed with unalienable rights (life, liberty, pursuit of happiness), government derives power from consent of the governed, and people have the right to overthrow a failing government. Provided the first successful model of a colony breaking free using Enlightenment justifications.

2. Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen (1789) — France

Freeman-pedia calls this document "the culmination of all the Enlightenment writers' thoughts wrapped into one document." Thomas Jefferson helped write it. Opens with Rousseau's inverted line: "Men are born and remain free and equal in rights." Guarantees freedom of speech, assembly, religion, due process, and popular sovereignty. Unlike the American Declaration (colonial independence), this was about restructuring French society from within — abolishing nobility's privileges.

3. The Jamaica Letter (1815) — Simón Bolívar

Written by Bolívar while in exile in Jamaica — where he had fled after being expelled from Venezuela and was nearly assassinated by Spanish spies. He wrote to a British contact asking for support, articulating his vision for Latin American independence. Key quote: "The hatred that the peninsula [Spain] has inspired in us is greater than the ocean between us." Shows Enlightenment ideas about sovereignty applied to Spanish colonial rule. Bolívar eventually got support, returned, and liberated six countries.

Without looking: what are the three documents specifically named in the AP curriculum for Unit 5? Who wrote each? What Enlightenment ideas does each draw on?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
1. Declaration of Independence (1776) — Written by Thomas Jefferson. Draws on Locke: natural rights, consent of the governed, right to overthrow a failing government. Specifically: "life, liberty, and pursuit of happiness" is Locke's formula (life, liberty, property) with "happiness" substituted. Justification for colonial independence from Britain.

2. Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen (1789) — French National Assembly; Jefferson helped draft it. Draws on Rousseau (popular sovereignty, social contract), Voltaire (free speech, press, religion), and Montesquieu (rule of law). Synthesizes all Enlightenment ideas into one document. About restructuring French society, not colonial independence.

3. The Jamaica Letter (1815) — Written by Simón Bolívar from exile in Jamaica. Draws on Locke and Rousseau (natural rights, popular sovereignty, right of self-determination) applied to Latin American colonial context. Key: written in exile after military defeat, showing Bolívar's commitment even in adversity. The letter articulates the philosophical case for why Spanish colonial rule was illegitimate.
The US Constitution's system of three branches with checks and balances is most directly based on which Enlightenment thinker's ideas?
Section 02

The Three Political Revolutions

📌 What the College Board Specifically Names

The College Board names three revolutions: the American Revolution, the Haitian Revolution, and the Latin American independence movements. The French Revolution is NOT officially named — but its documents and ideas are everywhere. Understand it as essential context.

🌊 The Wave Pattern

Think of these revolutions as a wave that builds and spreads: American (1776) proved it was possible → French (1789) showed it could happen in Europe → Haitian (1791–1804) proved enslaved people could do it → Latin American (1810s–1820s) spread it across a continent → European revolutions of 1848. Each revolution inspired the next.

The American Revolution (1776–1783)

📌 Causes
  • Enlightenment ideas: Locke's natural rights, Montesquieu's separation of powers, consent of the governed
  • "No taxation without representation": Britain taxed colonies without giving them Parliamentary representation — a violation of their rights as British subjects
  • Post-Seven Years' War: Britain tried to tighten colonial control; colonists resented it
⚡ Outcome & Limits
  • First successful model of Enlightenment-based colonial independence
  • Critically: primarily a POLITICAL separation, NOT a social revolution
  • Slavery was maintained; women denied political rights; Founders were wealthy elite who wanted independence but NOT social restructuring
  • The contradiction of "all men are created equal" + chattel slavery defines American history for the next 90 years
Why is the American Revolution considered limited as a revolution? What did it achieve — and who did it leave out?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
The American Revolution achieved political independence from Britain and established the first modern written constitution with separation of powers (Montesquieu), checks and balances, and a Bill of Rights (Locke's natural rights in law). These were real achievements that served as a model for subsequent revolutions.

However, the AP emphasizes its limits: (1) Slavery was preserved — the Declaration of Independence was written by enslaved people's enslavers, and the Constitution protected the slave trade until 1808; (2) women were denied all political rights; (3) the revolution was led by and primarily benefited wealthy colonial elites — property-owning white men; (4) the existing social hierarchy was largely preserved; (5) Indigenous peoples were not included and continued to lose land.

The AP key: use the American Revolution as evidence that Enlightenment ideals were applied selectively — to justify independence for elites while preserving systems of exploitation. Compare to the Haitian Revolution for maximum contrast.

The Haitian Revolution (1791–1804) — The Most Radical Revolution

⭐ Most Tested Revolution on the AP Exam After the American
What Makes This Revolution Unique

The Haitian Revolution is the only successful large-scale enslaved people's revolt in history that resulted in a permanent, independent nation. It is the most radical of all the Unit 5 revolutions.

Saint-Domingue (Haiti) was the most profitable colony in the world in 1791 — producing about 40% of Europe's sugar using the labor of ~500,000 enslaved Africans. The society was divided into: white colonists (political/economic power), free people of color (gens de couleur, often mixed-race property owners but politically subordinate), and enslaved people (the vast majority, outnumbering whites roughly 15:1).

📌 Causes
  • The French Revolution's Declaration of Rights of Man declared "men are born free and equal" — enslaved people in Haiti asked the obvious question
  • Brutal conditions + massive numerical advantage (500,000 enslaved vs. 30,000 whites)
  • Free people of color (like Vincent Ogé) had already begun demanding equal rights
⚡ Consequences
  • First successful large-scale enslaved revolt; first Black republic; second republic in Western Hemisphere
  • Terrified slaveholding societies everywhere (US South, Brazil, Cuba)
  • Napoleon sold Louisiana Territory to US after being humiliated by Haitian defeat — the Haitian Revolution shaped American geography
  • France demanded enormous reparations from Haiti for "lost property" — Haiti paid until the 1950s, stunting its development
👑 Toussaint L'Ouverture — The Key Figure

Toussaint L'Ouverture was an enslaved man who became the greatest military and political leader of the Haitian Revolution. Self-educated in French Enlightenment philosophy. Organized enslaved people into a formidable military force that defeated Spanish, British, and French armies.

The French eventually captured him through treachery — inviting him to negotiate, then arresting him. He died in a French prison in 1803. But the revolution he led could not be stopped: Haiti declared independence on January 1, 1804 — the world's first Black republic.

Why is the Haitian Revolution considered the most radical of all the Unit 5 revolutions? Compare it specifically to the American Revolution — same Enlightenment ideas, very different outcomes. Why?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
The Haitian Revolution is the most radical because: (1) it was led by the enslaved themselves, not by elites using them as cannon fodder; (2) it abolished slavery entirely; (3) it established the world's first Black republic; (4) it demonstrated that enslaved people could defeat the military forces of three major European powers simultaneously.

Comparing to American Revolution: Both drew on the same Enlightenment ideas (natural rights, popular sovereignty, the Declaration of Rights of Man specifically influenced Haitian leaders). But the American Revolution was led by wealthy colonial elites who applied Enlightenment ideas selectively — to justify their own independence while preserving slavery. The Haitian Revolution was led by enslaved people who applied Enlightenment ideas to their most logical conclusion — if all men are born free and equal, then slavery is the ultimate contradiction and must be abolished.

AP key insight: The same ideology (Enlightenment) produced dramatically different results depending on who applied it and what their social position was. This is the AP's favorite comparison for Unit 5 essays.

Latin American Independence Movements (c. 1810–1825)

The Latin American independence movements freed most of Spanish and Portuguese South and Central America in roughly 15 years. Freeman-pedia calls Simón Bolívar "the Tom Brady of this unit" — the dominant force across an enormous area.

📌 Causes
  • Creole resentment: American-born Spaniards excluded from highest government positions (reserved for Peninsulares) — same frustration as the American Revolution
  • Napoleon's invasion of Spain (1808): Created a legitimacy crisis — if the king wasn't legitimate, why obey the colonies' authority?
  • Enlightenment ideas spread through Latin American educated elites via European education
  • Examples of the American and Haitian Revolutions showed it was possible
⚠️ Critical Limit
  • Overwhelmingly led by Creoles — the wealthy, American-born Spanish elite
  • They wanted political independence from Spain but did NOT want social revolution
  • The casta system (racial hierarchy from Unit 4) largely remained intact after independence
  • Indigenous peoples, enslaved Africans, and mestizos did NOT gain meaningful rights
  • Sharp contrast to the Haitian Revolution, which genuinely led by and for the enslaved
Who led the Latin American independence movements? What class did they come from? Why is this significant for understanding what actually changed after independence?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
The Latin American independence movements were led primarily by Creoles — people of full Spanish descent born in the Americas. Key figures: Simón Bolívar (Venezuela, freed six countries), José de San Martín (Argentina, Chile, Peru), and Miguel Hidalgo (Mexico, priest who mobilized Indigenous and mestizo populations — the exception).

Why this matters: Creoles were the second-highest tier of the casta system. They were educated and wealthy but resented being excluded from the highest political offices. They wanted to replace the Peninsulares (Spanish-born) at the top of the hierarchy — NOT to abolish the hierarchy itself. After independence, the casta system largely remained. Indigenous peoples still faced exploitation. Enslaved Africans in many areas remained enslaved. The same economic elite that had run the colonies under Spain often continued to run the new republics.

AP comparison: This is why the Latin American revolutions are considered limited in social scope, similar to (but somewhat worse than) the American Revolution. Both contrast sharply with the Haitian Revolution, where the formerly enslaved became the citizens of the new state.
Which of the four Unit 5 revolutions produced the most radical social change?
Section 03

Nationalism — The New Political Force

Alongside the political revolutions, a new ideology emerged that would reshape the map of the world: nationalism — the idea that people who share a common language, culture, religion, or history form a natural "nation" and should have their own political state.

The College Board uses nationalism in two ways: (1) nationalism used to BUILD new unified states, and (2) nationalism used to CHALLENGE existing empires. Both matter equally for the AP exam.

Nationalism to BUILD: German Unification Under Bismarck

Germany in the early 19th century was a patchwork of dozens of separate German-speaking states. Otto von Bismarck, Prussian chancellor, used nationalism + military force ("blood and iron") + political genius to unite these states into a single German Empire in 1871 — proclaimed in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles, a deliberate humiliation of France. The unified Germany became the most industrially powerful state in Europe.

What was Bismarck's strategy for unifying Germany? How did he use nationalism AND military force together?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Bismarck's approach was "realpolitik" — practical power politics over ideology. He deliberately provoked wars against Denmark (1864), Austria (1866), and France (1870–71), and used each victory to bring more German states under Prussian leadership. The key insight: each military victory against a foreign enemy created a shared sense of "we" among the German states — nothing unifies people like a common enemy. By winning three wars in quick succession, Bismarck demonstrated Prussian military superiority and made joining a German Empire under Prussian leadership attractive rather than threatening to other German states.

The German Empire was proclaimed in January 1871 in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles — a deliberate symbolic humiliation of France, which had just been defeated. The AP significance: unified Germany became the most industrially powerful state in Europe, competing with and eventually surpassing Britain, and its emergence as a major power reshaped European diplomacy in ways that lead directly to World War I (Unit 7).

Nationalism to CHALLENGE: Anti-Colonial and Anti-Imperial Movements

MovementWho / WhereWhat Happened & AP Significance
Propaganda Movement (Philippines)Filipino intellectuals vs. Spanish colonial rule; led by José RizalRizal wrote novels exposing Spanish colonial abuses and advocated for Filipino equality. The Spanish executed him by firing squad in 1896 (shooting him in the back), making him a martyr who ignited the Philippine Revolution. He is the national hero of the Philippines.
Māori Resistance (New Zealand)Māori (Indigenous Polynesian New Zealanders) against British colonizationSigned the Treaty of Waitangi (1840) with Britain, then fought the New Zealand Wars when Britain began aggressively taking land. Freeman-pedia: "throwing a haka right in the British faces." The Māori maintained significant political presence and cultural continuity — a notable example of resistance achieving lasting results.
OttomanismOttoman Empire government-sponsored; attempt to create pan-Ottoman identityOttoman government tried to unify diverse subjects (Greeks, Bulgarians, Armenians, Turks, Arabs) under one "Ottoman" identity. Freeman-pedia: like "fetch" in Mean Girls — "they kept trying to make it happen but it wasn't going to happen." Largely failed — various groups wanted their own nations. Contributes to the Ottoman Empire's eventual collapse (Unit 7).
Italian Unification (Risorgimento)Italian nationalists, including Garibaldi and CavourItaly was divided into separate states (some controlled by Austria and the Papal States). Nationalists used military campaigns and political maneuvering to unify Italy by 1871 — parallel story to German unification.
German unification and the Philippine Propaganda Movement both involved nationalism, but in completely opposite ways. What is the key difference in how each used nationalist ideology?
Section 04

The Industrial Revolution

🏆 Freeman-pedia's MVP of Unit 5: James Watt

Not Bolívar. Not Napoleon. Not Bismarck. James Watt. Without the steam engine, there is no Industrial Revolution. Period. Watt's 1769 improvement made it ten times more powerful while using 75% less fuel. That ten-times improvement powered every factory, locomotive, and steamship of the Industrial Revolution. The unit for measuring electrical power — the watt — is named after him.

Why is James Watt considered the MVP of Unit 5? What specifically did he improve, and why did it matter so much?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
The old Newcomen steam engine was inefficient — it could only push a piston in one direction. Watt's improvement (1769) used a separate condenser to push the piston both ways and converted that motion into rotary power. Result: ten times more energy output using 75% less fuel.

Why this mattered so much: before Watt's engine, manufacturing was limited by human and animal power. After it, mechanical power could replace and exceed human and animal labor in virtually every manufacturing application. This made the factory system possible — not just spinning wheels and looms, but massive machines that could work 24 hours continuously without rest. The steam engine also powered locomotives (railroads) and steamships, transforming transportation. Without Watt's specific improvement, industrialization might have happened eventually but not when and how it did. The AP lesson: sometimes a single technological improvement at the right moment can redirect history.

Why Britain First? The Seven Factors

#FactorWhy It Mattered
1Waterways1,000+ navigable river miles + canal-building program. Rivers and canals were the cheapest way to transport heavy goods (coal, iron) before railroads — Britain had a built-in internal highway system.
2Natural ResourcesAbundant coal deposits AND iron ore. Coal powered the steam engines. Iron built the machines. Having both on the same island was a major advantage.
3Property RightsStrong legal protections for private property, investment, contracts, and patents. Entrepreneurs could invest knowing their innovations and profits were legally protected. The "invisible hand" required secure property rights to function.
4EmpireThe largest empire in history gave Britain access to global raw materials (cotton, rubber) and captive markets for finished goods. The empire both fed and consumed industrial production.
5UrbanizationAs factories grew, cities grew around them. 12 British cities over 100,000 people by 1850. Concentrated labor force near factories; urban workers consumed manufactured goods, stimulating further production.
6Agricultural RevolutionNew farming techniques (crop rotation, enclosure movement) increased food production. More food → more people survived → larger labor force for factories AND more consumers for goods.
7CapitalWorld's first large stock market, developed banking system, insurance for investments. Entrepreneurs could raise money from investors; capital flowed efficiently to productive investments.
From memory, name as many of the seven reasons as you can for why the Industrial Revolution started in Britain. Then explain: why couldn't another country simply copy Britain immediately?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
The seven factors: Waterways (navigable rivers + canals for cheap transport), Natural Resources (coal AND iron on the same island), Property Rights (secure investment, patents, contracts), Empire (global raw materials and markets), Urbanization (concentrated labor + consumer market), Agricultural Revolution (surplus food = surplus labor), Capital (stock market, banks, insurance).

Why couldn't others copy immediately: No other country had all seven factors simultaneously. France had no easy domestic coal + iron. The US had resources but not the capital or urbanization initially. Germany was politically fragmented. The combination of all seven was unique to Britain. Other countries industrialized by: (1) importing British technology and workers (US and Belgium — "Slater the Traitor" moved to Rhode Island in 1793), or (2) state-sponsored industrialization where governments forced modernization (Japan's Meiji Restoration, Russia's Trans-Siberian Railroad). The AP point: industrialization was not automatically replicated — it required specific conditions or deliberate state action.

The Technology of the Industrial Revolution

Three Big Tech Ideas (The College Board's Framework)

1. Power: From Steam to Oil — The First Industrial Revolution (~1760–1870) was powered by coal and Watt's steam engine. Toward the end of this period, the internal combustion engine emerged, running on oil and gasoline. The College Board calls this the fossil fuels revolution — and these are the same fossil fuels whose climate consequences we manage today.

2. The Second Industrial Revolution (~1870–1914) — If the First was about steam and textiles, the Second was about steel, chemicals, and electricity. Key: the Bessemer process made mass steel production cheap (railroads, skyscrapers, bridges); Edison's lightbulb (1879); AC electrical power grids; synthetic chemicals; telegraph and radio. The Second Industrial Revolution made the US and Germany competitive with and eventually superior to Britain.

3. Transportation and Communication — Railroads connected continents. Steamships crossed oceans in days instead of weeks. The transatlantic telegraph cable (1866) allowed near-instant communication between London and New York. For the first time in history, a British official could give orders to India and receive a reply the same day. The world got dramatically smaller.

🌍 The Big Loser: The Non-Industrialized World

As Britain, the US, and Germany captured global industrial production, someone was losing that share. The big losers: Egypt, India, and Southeast Asia — places praised in Units 1 and 2 for sophisticated textile industries. British factory-made cotton cloth was so cheap that Indian handloom weavers couldn't compete. This is the economic context for Unit 6: the newly industrialized West will go to these deindustrialized regions to extract raw materials and sell manufactured goods — the essence of New Imperialism.

What is the main difference between the First and Second Industrial Revolutions?
Section 05

State-Sponsored Industrialization

Not every country industrialized through private enterprise and free markets. Some governments saw Britain's industrial power and recognized: modernize or fall behind militarily and economically. They stepped in to force industrialization from above.

Country / ReformWhat HappenedOutcome
Egypt: Muhammad Ali's ModernizationsNationalized agricultural land, forced farmers to sell crops to the state, modernized the cotton industry, expanded irrigation. The College Board's illustrative example.Cotton production surged. Ali built a proto-industrial state within the Ottoman system. However, state-directed rather than market-driven — Egypt remained dependent on European markets. Key example of a non-European state engaging with industrial capitalism.
Russia: Emancipation of Serfs (1861) + IndustrializationTsar Alexander II freed 23 million serfs after Russia's humiliating defeat in the Crimean War revealed how far it had fallen behind. Russia then built factories and the Trans-Siberian Railroad in the 1890s.Russia industrialized late and unevenly. Economic reforms outpaced political reforms — creating the tensions that exploded in the Russian Revolution of 1905 and 1917 (Unit 7).
China: Self-Strengthening Movement (1860s–1890s)After military defeats (Opium Wars), the Qing launched selective modernization: military upgrades, some industry, limited Western technology. Goal: "Western technology without Western political ideas."Failed. China lost a war to Japan in 1895, demonstrating the limits of selective modernization. You can't adopt the technology without the broader economic and political systems that enable it.
Japan: The Meiji Restoration (1868)After American warships (1853) forced Japan to open, the Tokugawa Shogunate was overthrown and Emperor Meiji restored. Japan launched comprehensive modernization: sent scholars worldwide to study foreign systems, built Western-style military, industry, banking, and legal systems.The most successful state-sponsored industrialization in history. Japan went from feudal isolation to industrial power in one generation. Defeated China in 1895, Russia in 1905. Freeman-pedia: "they don't lose a war again until 1945."
Compare Japan's Meiji Restoration to China's Self-Strengthening Movement. Both were responses to Western pressure — why did Japan succeed where China failed?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Japan (Meiji Restoration, 1868): Japan went ALL IN. The new Meiji government sent officials around the world to study not just technology but entire systems — the German military structure, the French legal system, the American education system, the British industrial organization. They completely restructured Japan's political, legal, military, economic, and educational systems simultaneously. It was comprehensive and radical. Within one generation, Japan had a Western-style constitution, a conscript army, industrial factories, railroads, and a banking system — then proved it by defeating China (1895) and Russia (1905) in war.

China (Self-Strengthening Movement, 1860s–1890s): The Qing tried selective modernization — "Chinese learning for fundamental principles, Western learning for practical application." They wanted the guns and ships but not the political and economic systems that made them possible. This was fundamentally contradictory: you cannot build a competitive modern military without also building the industrial, financial, and administrative systems that support it. China lost a war to Japan in 1895 — a massive humiliation, since Japan was an Asian state that had also been "backward" just 30 years earlier but had committed fully to modernization while China had not.

AP lesson: Successful modernization required comprehensive systemic change, not selective adoption of individual technologies.
Section 06

Capitalism & the New Global Economy

💀 Mercantilism Is Dead

Freeman-pedia: "The book on capitalism wasn't even written until 1776, so capitalism is a Unit 5 thing. Mercantilism is buried six feet under by Adam Smith." Adam Smith's The Wealth of Nations (1776) is mercantilism's funeral — and capitalism's birth certificate.

FeatureMercantilism (Units 3–4)Capitalism (Unit 5 onward)
GoalMaximize STATE wealth; accumulate gold and silverMaximize PRIVATE profit; generate economic growth
Government roleHeavy government control; monopolies; regulated tradeMinimal interference; laissez-faire; let markets work
Trade regulationColonies only trade with mother country; foreign ships bannedFree markets; goods flow where demand takes them
Key mechanismGovernment-chartered monopolies (joint-stock companies)"Invisible hand" of supply and demand; private enterprise
Famous textNo single text — just government policyAdam Smith's The Wealth of Nations (1776)
🌐 Transnational Businesses — The New Economic Powerhouses

With free markets came the rise of transnational businesses — companies operating across national borders, following profit wherever it leads. The College Board specifically mentions the Hong Kong and Shanghai Banking Corporation (HSBC, est. 1865) as an illustrative example. Other transnational companies operating by 1900: Singer (sewing machines), Standard Oil (petroleum), Lever Brothers (soap), United Fruit Company (bananas in Central America).

These companies operated in multiple countries simultaneously, extracted resources from colonies, manufactured in industrial centers, and sold globally. This is the skeleton of the modern global economy.

What is the difference between mercantilism and capitalism? Give at least three specific differences and explain why Adam Smith is the turning point between them.
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Key differences: (1) GOAL — mercantilism aims to maximize state wealth (gold/silver); capitalism aims to maximize private profit and generate economic growth. (2) GOVERNMENT ROLE — mercantilism requires heavy government control, monopolies, and restricted trade; capitalism calls for minimal government interference (laissez-faire) and free markets. (3) TRADE — under mercantilism, colonies can only trade with the mother country; under capitalism, goods flow freely to wherever demand is highest. (4) MECHANISM — mercantilism used government-chartered joint-stock companies with monopoly rights; capitalism uses private enterprise competing in free markets, guided by the "invisible hand."

Why Adam Smith is the turning point: His Wealth of Nations (1776) was the first systematic intellectual argument for why free markets produce better economic outcomes than government-directed mercantilism. Published the same year as the Declaration of Independence, it argued that when individuals pursue their own self-interest in free markets, the "invisible hand" guides resources to their most productive uses — benefiting society without any central direction. This provided the intellectual justification for dismantling mercantile restrictions, which industrial capitalists were also pushing for economically. Theory (Smith) + economic interest (factory owners) = capitalism replaces mercantilism.
Section 07

Reactions to Industrial Capitalism

The Industrial Revolution created enormous wealth AND enormous misery simultaneously. Factory workers worked 14–16-hour days, six days a week, in dangerous conditions, for extremely low wages. Children as young as 5 or 6 worked in factories and mines. Cities were choked with pollution. The College Board identifies four main types of reactions. Know all four.

Reaction 1: Government Reform

📋 The Sadler Report (1832) — The Evidence That Changed Policy

Michael Sadler was a British Member of Parliament who in 1832 interviewed hundreds of current and former child laborers. Children described 14–16-hour workdays, physical punishment, injuries from machinery, stunted physical development, denial of education. The report shocked British public opinion and catalyzed the Factory Acts — laws limiting working hours of children, then women, then adults.

AP significance: The Sadler Report is the key example of government reform in response to industrial abuses. It shows that even within a capitalist system, government intervention occurred when conditions became sufficiently outrageous. Britain's factory reform legislation set a model for other industrializing nations.

What was the Sadler Report? What did it document, and what was its historical consequence?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
The Sadler Report (1832) was a British parliamentary investigation led by Michael Sadler that documented the brutal conditions faced by child laborers in factories. Children as young as 5 or 6 described working 14–16 hours per day, being physically beaten by overseers for slowing down, being injured or maimed by machinery, having no time for school or play, and experiencing stunted physical growth from carrying heavy loads. The testimony was specific, detailed, and devastating.

Historical consequence: The report shocked British public opinion — even middle-class people who had accepted child labor as normal found the specific testimony impossible to ignore. It directly catalyzed the Factory Acts (1833 onward), which limited children's working hours and eventually adults' hours as well. AP significance: The Sadler Report is the AP's go-to example of government reform in response to industrial abuses. It demonstrates that government intervention in a capitalist economy — usually resisted by laissez-faire advocates — could and did occur when public outrage reached a sufficient level.

Reaction 2: Labor Unions

A single worker has no power against an employer. But if all workers in a factory act together, they can force negotiation. Through collective bargaining, labor unions pushed for and eventually won: higher wages, shorter hours, safer conditions, the right not to be fired arbitrarily, and paid time off.

📜 The Chartist Movement (Britain, 1838–1857)

The Chartists were a working-class political movement demanding democratic reforms: universal male suffrage, secret ballot, payment for Members of Parliament (so working-class men could afford to serve), and equal electoral districts. They presented massive petitions — one with over 3 million signatures. Parliament rejected all of them. But most of their demands were eventually implemented over the following decades.

Reaction 3: Marxism / Communism

Karl Marx's Core Argument

Marx analyzed capitalism and identified its fundamental contradiction: the bourgeoisie (factory owners) controlled the means of production. The proletariat (workers) had nothing to sell but their labor. The bourgeoisie paid workers the minimum they could survive on and kept the rest as profit (what Marx called "surplus value") — a form of exploitation as systematic as feudalism or slavery.

Marx's prediction (from the Communist Manifesto, 1848): The proletariat would grow increasingly miserable → eventually rise up and seize the means of production → a communist society would emerge where private property was abolished and resources distributed according to need.

⚠️ Freeman-pedia's Critical Warning

Marx's economic analysis of capitalism's contradictions was insightful. But the actual implementation of Communism — always imposed top-down by authoritarian governments, never arising organically from below as Marx predicted — produced some of the deadliest regimes in history. Stalin killed roughly twice as many people as Hitler. Mao killed approximately three times as many. Know Marxism as an intellectual reaction to industrial capitalism. Also know that its 20th-century implementation (Units 7–8) was catastrophic.

Explain Marx's critique of capitalism in your own words. What did he see as capitalism's fundamental contradiction? What did he predict would happen?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Marx's critique in three parts:

1. The problem (exploitation): In capitalism, the bourgeoisie (factory owners) own all the means of production — factories, machinery, raw materials. The proletariat (workers) own nothing except their labor, which they must sell to survive. The bourgeoisie pay workers just enough to stay alive and work, and keep the remaining value the workers create — what Marx called "surplus value." This is exploitation: workers create all the value, but owners capture most of it.

2. The prediction (revolution): Marx believed capitalism's contradictions would inevitably produce revolution. As capitalism developed, the bourgeoisie would grow smaller (fewer, larger companies) while the proletariat grew larger and increasingly miserable. Eventually, the proletariat would develop "class consciousness" (awareness of their shared condition and interests), organize, and overthrow the bourgeoisie. "Workers of the world, unite! You have nothing to lose but your chains."

3. The vision (communism): After the revolution, the proletariat would control the means of production collectively through the state. Eventually, private property would be abolished entirely and resources distributed according to need: "from each according to his ability, to each according to his needs." A classless society.

AP key: Marx's analysis described real problems. His predictions were wrong (the revolution happened in Russia, not the most industrialized countries). His implementation was catastrophic. Know the theory clearly; note the historical failure briefly.

Reaction 4: Government-Sponsored Modernization

🏛️ Tanzimat Reforms (Ottoman Empire, 1839–1876)

"Tanzimat" means reorganization. These Ottoman modernizing reforms aimed to strengthen the empire against European pressure: guaranteed rights for all Ottoman subjects regardless of religion; modernized the military along European lines; established secular courts and Western-style legal codes; promoted "Ottomanism" — a new national identity for all subjects regardless of ethnicity.

Results: partial success in military modernization and legal reform. But Ottomanism failed — various groups (Greeks, Bulgarians, Armenians) wanted their own nations, not an Ottoman identity. And the Sultan could simply abolish reforms he didn't like. True modernization of the Ottoman political system didn't arrive until after World War I (Unit 7).

Which of the four reactions to industrial capitalism called for abolishing capitalism entirely rather than reforming it?
Section 08

Society in the Industrial Age

The New Industrial Class Structure

ClassWho They WereAP Significance
Ruling ClassAnyone with wealth and power who runs political and economic systems; no longer exclusively hereditary nobility — industrial magnates now includedKey shift: birth no longer automatically determines the top. Industrial wealth could buy political influence. Both progress AND a new form of oligarchy.
Bourgeoisie (Middle Class)Factory owners, managers, professionals, merchants — people who control capital or manage those who do labor, but are subordinate to the ruling classMarx's term for the capitalist class. They invested in factories, drove innovation, pushed for laissez-faire. Also: the engine of capitalism.
Working Class / ProletariatWage laborers who sell their physical labor but own no means of production. Factory workers, miners, mill workers.The largest class and the most affected. Long hours, dangerous conditions, child labor, minimal pay. Target of union organizing and Marxist revolutionary theory.

Women and the Industrial Revolution — Two Very Different Stories

Working-Class Women

Working-class women and children worked in factories alongside men — often doing the same work for lower pay. The factory system separated the family unit (previously working together on a farm or cottage industry) as each member became an individual wage laborer. Labor commodified in a way that family agriculture had not.

Middle-Class Women — The Cult of Domesticity

Middle-class families could afford for women not to work — so middle-class women were expected NOT to work. Four "virtues": Piety, Purity, Submission, Domesticity. By 1890, only 5% of married middle-class women worked outside the home. Simultaneously a marker of prosperity AND a mechanism of social control. The Seneca Falls Declaration (1848) directly challenged these constraints.

What was the Cult of Domesticity? What four "virtues" did it demand of middle-class women? How did the experience of working-class women differ?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
The Cult of Domesticity was a 19th-century middle-class ideology that defined the ideal woman's role as devoted wife, mother, and homemaker. The four "virtues" were: Piety (Christian moral values), Purity (sexual and moral purity before and during marriage), Submission (deference to husband's authority), and Domesticity (the home as the woman's proper sphere — managing the household and raising children).

Why it emerged: As middle-class families became prosperous enough that women didn't need to work for wages, NOT working became a status symbol. A woman at home signaled that her husband could afford to support the family alone. This made the Cult of Domesticity simultaneously a marker of middle-class prosperity AND a powerful mechanism for confining women to the domestic sphere regardless of their ambitions.

Working-class women's experience: The complete opposite. Working-class women worked in factories alongside men, often for lower pay, in the same dangerous conditions. The factory system had disrupted the pre-industrial family economy (where family members worked together at home). Now individual family members were separated as individual wage laborers.

AP key: The Seneca Falls Declaration (1848) — modeled on the Declaration of Independence, declaring "all men and women are created equal" — was a direct challenge to the Cult of Domesticity and its restriction of women's political, economic, and social rights.

Social Challenges of Industrialization

  • Industrial pollution: Factory smoke turned city skies grey. Rivers became industrial sewers. London's "Great Stink" of 1858 (the Thames as open sewer) forced Parliament to fund a modern sewage system.
  • Urban poverty and overcrowding: Factory workers lived in tenements — dense, poorly constructed housing, multiple families sharing tiny spaces, often without running water or sanitation.
  • Disease: Cholera, typhus, and tuberculosis spread rapidly in overcrowded urban conditions. The 19th century saw multiple cholera pandemics sweep through industrial cities.
  • Child labor: Children as young as 5 or 6 worked in factories and mines, crawling through narrow shafts or cleaning machinery while still running. The Sadler Report documented this in devastating detail.
  • Infrastructure inadequacy: Cities grew faster than roads, water systems, police forces, schools, and hospitals could be built.
Section 09

Revolution Comparison Table

This table is built for essay writing. Any comparison LEQ or DBQ about the revolutions can be structured using it.

CategoryAmerican (1776)French (1789)Haitian (1791)Latin American (1810s)
Who LedColonial elites (mostly wealthy property owners)Third Estate, then Jacobins; eventually NapoleonEnslaved people and free people of color; Toussaint L'OuvertureCreole elites (Bolívar, San Martín, Hidalgo)
Enlightenment BasisLocke (natural rights, consent of governed)Rousseau (social contract), Voltaire (freedom)Declaration of Rights of Man applied literally to slaveryBolívar's Jamaica Letter; Locke; Rousseau
Key DocumentDeclaration of Independence (1776)Declaration of Rights of Man (1789)Haitian Constitution (1805)Jamaica Letter (1815)
Social ChangeMinimal — existing social order preserved; slavery continuedSignificant — abolished nobility's privileges; later abolished slavery (briefly; reimposed by Napoleon)Maximum — abolished slavery; first Black republicLimited — casta system largely preserved; Creole elites still dominated
OutcomeConstitutional republic; US Constitution (1787)Turbulent (Terror → Napoleon); Napoleonic Code spreads ideasIndependent Haiti (1804); first Black republic; terror in slaveholding societiesIndependent nations throughout Latin America; continued inequality
LimitsWomen, enslaved people excluded from rightsWomen excluded; slavery reinstated by NapoleonHaiti punished economically for generations; French reparations demanded until 1950sSocial hierarchy persisted; Indigenous peoples and enslaved Africans excluded
For each of the four revolutions, write one sentence identifying: who led it AND what it did NOT change. This is the AP's favorite pattern to test.
MODEL ANSWERS
American: Led by wealthy colonial property-owning elites; did NOT change the social order — slavery continued, women were denied political rights, and the existing class hierarchy was preserved.

French: Led by the Third Estate (eventually Jacobin radicals, then Napoleon); did NOT achieve lasting stability — the revolution consumed itself in the Reign of Terror, Napoleon reimposed some pre-revolutionary structures, and women were still denied political rights.

Haitian: Led by enslaved people and free people of color under Toussaint L'Ouverture; did NOT escape economic punishment — France demanded enormous reparations for "lost property" (including enslaved people) that Haiti paid until the 1950s, fundamentally stunting its development.

Latin American: Led by Creole elites (Bolívar, San Martín, Hidalgo); did NOT change the social hierarchy — the casta system remained largely intact, Indigenous peoples and enslaved Africans were excluded from meaningful rights, and the same elite class that had dominated under Spain continued to dominate the new republics.
Section 10

Glossary of Key Terms

Test yourself on each term before revealing. Mark how well you know it.

Section 11

Timeline of Unit 5 (c. 1750–1900)

c. 1760

Industrial Revolution Begins in Britain

Steam power + textile machinery + coal + iron = the factory system. The most consequential economic transformation in human history begins.

1762

Rousseau: The Social Contract

"Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains." Banned by Louis XV. Becomes the philosophical foundation of the French Revolution 27 years later.

1769

James Watt Improves the Steam Engine

Ten times more powerful than the Newcomen engine, using 75% less fuel. The literal and figurative engine of the Industrial Revolution.

1776

Wealth of Nations + Declaration of Independence

Adam Smith buries mercantilism; capitalism is born. The US declares independence using Locke's framework. Two documents that reshape the world.

1789

French Revolution Begins; Declaration of Rights of Man

The Third Estate seizes power; Declaration of Rights of Man synthesizes all Enlightenment ideas. The most influential revolution in world history begins.

1791

Haitian Revolution Begins

Enslaved people of Saint-Domingue rise up. The most radical revolution: the only successful large-scale enslaved revolt in history.

1804

Haiti Declares Independence

First Black republic; second republic in the Western Hemisphere. Napoleon sells Louisiana Territory after Haitian defeat. A world-changing moment.

1810s–1825

Latin American Independence Movements

Bolívar, San Martín, Hidalgo lead independence across Latin America. Spain and Portugal lose their American empires. New nations with persistent inequality.

1815

Jamaica Letter Written by Bolívar

Written from exile in Jamaica after military defeat. Articulates the philosophical case for Latin American independence. One of the three required AP documents.

1832

Sadler Report (Britain)

Parliamentary investigation of child labor. Shocks British public opinion. Leads to Factory Acts. Key example of government reform in response to industrial abuses.

1848

Communist Manifesto + European Revolutions + Seneca Falls

Marx and Engels publish their call to workers. A wave of revolutions sweeps France, Germany, Austria-Hungary, Italy. Seneca Falls Declaration demands women's rights. A year of global upheaval.

1861

Russia Emancipates Serfs; US Civil War Begins

Russia frees 23 million serfs after defeat in Crimean War. US Civil War begins — the Haitian Revolution's long shadow. Both signal the global decline of coerced labor.

1868

Meiji Restoration (Japan)

Japan dumps the shogunate, restores the Emperor Meiji, and begins comprehensive modernization. The most successful state-sponsored industrialization in history.

1871

German and Italian Unification

Bismarck unites Germany; Italian Risorgimento unites Italy. Two new nationalist states join the European great powers. Balance of power shifts.

c. 1870–1914

Second Industrial Revolution

Steel, chemicals, electricity, telegraph, radio. US and Germany challenge British industrial dominance. The world economy integrates more deeply.

Fill in: James Watt's 1769 improvement to the steam engine made it how many times more powerful than the previous Newcomen engine?
Section 12

Historical Thinking Skills

1. Comparison — The Primary Skill for Revolutions

Weak vs. Strong Comparison

WEAK: "The American and Haitian Revolutions both used Enlightenment ideas."

STRONG: "Both the American and Haitian Revolutions drew on Enlightenment ideals of natural rights and popular sovereignty, but differed fundamentally in the social scope of those ideals. The American Revolution, led by colonial elites, used Enlightenment language to justify political independence while preserving chattel slavery. The Haitian Revolution, led by enslaved people, applied those same ideals to their most logical conclusion, abolishing slavery and establishing a republic where the formerly enslaved became citizens. The same philosophical tradition produced a revolution that reinforced racial hierarchy in one case and demolished it in the other."

2. Continuity and Change Over Time

CONTINUITIES (1750–1900)
  • Social hierarchies and inequality persist: Latin American revolutions preserve casta system; women denied political rights; working class exploited
  • Coerced labor continues: US slavery until 1865; Russian serfdom until 1861
  • Religion remains central to social organization
  • European power continues to expand globally (building on Unit 4)
CHANGES (new in Unit 5)
  • Constitutional republics replace absolute monarchies as the dominant model of legitimate government
  • Capitalism replaces mercantilism; free markets and wage labor become global economic norms
  • Nationalism emerges as a new political force, challenging dynastic empires and creating new nation-states
  • The working class becomes a recognized political actor: labor unions, Marxism, government reform
  • The Industrial Revolution creates the factory system — a fundamentally new way of organizing production with no historical precedent
Practice: Write a one-sentence thesis for this LEQ prompt: "Compare the goals and outcomes of the American and Haitian Revolutions."
SAMPLE THESIS
"While the American and Haitian Revolutions both drew on Enlightenment principles of natural rights and popular sovereignty as their ideological foundation, they differed fundamentally in the social breadth of their application: the American Revolution, led by colonial elites, used Enlightenment ideas to justify political independence while preserving the existing social order — including slavery — whereas the Haitian Revolution, led by enslaved people, applied those same ideas to their most radical conclusion, abolishing slavery and establishing the world's first Black republic; the contrast in outcomes reveals how the same ideology can produce dramatically different results depending on who wields it and for what purpose."
Section 13

Common Command Terms for Unit 5

TermWhat It MeansUnit 5 Example
IdentifyName or point out one specific thing. Minimal explanation."Identify one cause of the Haitian Revolution." → The Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789) proclaimed that men are born free and equal, making slavery in French colonies philosophically contradictory and providing enslaved people with a powerful ideological argument for revolt.
ExplainShow HOW or WHY. Go beyond stating the fact."Explain one effect of the Industrial Revolution on social classes." → Don't just say "it created the proletariat." Explain: The factory system concentrated masses of wage laborers in urban areas, creating a new class whose shared poverty and working conditions created the basis for collective political action — labor unions, Chartism, and eventually Marxism.
CompareSimilarities AND differences. Both must be addressed."Compare the goals of the American and French Revolutions." Both: Enlightenment-based; natural rights; popular sovereignty. Different: American = political independence preserving social order; French = social restructuring abolishing nobility's privileges. Why did the same ideas produce different scopes?
Evaluate the extent to whichMake a judgment about HOW MUCH. Needs both evidence AND counterevidence."Evaluate the extent to which the Industrial Revolution improved living standards." Yes: long-term standards rose; consumer goods cheaper; life expectancy eventually increased. But: short-term child labor, 16-hour days, cholera, urban poverty. Improvement was real but deeply uneven by class, gender, and timing.
ContextualizeBroader historical context that explains WHY something happened."Contextualize the Haitian Revolution." Connect to: French Revolution's rhetoric incompatible with slavery; Saint-Domingue's unique demographic (enslaved vastly outnumbered whites); tradition of Maroon resistance (Unit 4); American Revolution's model of colonial independence.
Section 14

Common Pitfalls & How to Avoid Them

❌

Pitfall 1: Treating All Revolutions as the Same

The Problem: All four revolutions used Enlightenment language, but had very different participants, goals, scopes, and outcomes. Calling them all "revolutions for freedom" and treating them identically kills every comparison point.

✅ The Fix: For each revolution, know: WHO led it, WHAT they wanted, and WHAT ACTUALLY CHANGED afterward — specifically which groups gained rights and which didn't.

❌

Pitfall 2: Ignoring Who Was Left Out

The Problem: Every revolution claimed to be about freedom and equality. Every revolution also excluded someone. The American Revolution excluded enslaved people and women. The French reimposed slavery under Napoleon. Latin American revolutions preserved the casta system.

✅ The Fix: Always address the limits of revolutionary change. Who gained rights? Who didn't? Why? This nuance earns complexity points.

❌

Pitfall 3: Monocausal Explanations for Revolutions

The Problem: Students write that a revolution happened because of Enlightenment ideas, OR economic hardship, OR social inequality. In reality, every revolution required multiple causes to coincide.

✅ The Fix: For any revolution, always identify at least three causes: an ideological cause (Enlightenment), an economic cause (financial crisis, taxation), and a social cause (inequality, discrimination). Show how they reinforced each other.

❌

Pitfall 4: "Liberal" Means Modern Progressive

The Problem: In Unit 5, "liberal" and "liberalism" are used in their classical sense: favoring individual rights, constitutional government, free markets, and limited government power. This is NOTHING like the modern American political usage.

✅ The Fix: Every time you see "liberal" in a Unit 5 context, mentally substitute "classical liberal" — an Enlightenment-based advocate for political freedom and constitutional government.

❌

Pitfall 5: Forgetting the Industrial Revolution's Non-European Dimension

The Problem: Students focus on Britain and Europe in the Industrial Revolution and forget that its rise had massive consequences for non-European regions — India's handloom weavers destroyed, Egypt's cotton economy reshaped, Japan forced to modernize, China's selective modernization failed.

✅ The Fix: For every Industrial Revolution essay, include at least one non-European example: a state that industrialized (Japan/Meiji), tried and failed (China/Self-Strengthening), or was deindustrialized by European competition (India, Egypt).

❌

Pitfall 6: Confusing Communist Theory with Practice

The Problem: Marx's economic critique of capitalism is intellectually coherent and historically significant. Don't confuse the theory (workers collectively controlling production) with the actual implementation (Stalin, Mao — catastrophic authoritarian regimes).

✅ The Fix: Know Marxism as a 19th-century intellectual reaction to industrial capitalism's genuine problems. Note briefly that its 20th-century implementation was catastrophic. Don't equate the theory with the practice.

A student writes: "The Haitian and American Revolutions were very similar because both used Enlightenment ideals to fight for freedom." What is the main problem with this statement?
Section 15

Sample DBQ — With Full Breakdown

📊 DBQ Basics

DBQ = 25% of AP exam. 7 documents, 60 minutes (15 min reading). Thesis + contextualization + document analysis + outside evidence + sourcing + complexity.

"Evaluate the extent to which the Industrial Revolution changed the lives of the working class in Europe between 1750 and 1900."
1
Decode

Command: "Evaluate the extent to which... changed" — HOW MUCH did it change? Need both evidence of change AND limits/continuities.
Focus: Working-class lives (not the bourgeoisie)
Region: Europe (not globally)
Time: 1750–1900 (full arc of industrialization)

✅ Sample Thesis

"The Industrial Revolution dramatically transformed working-class life in Europe by creating new forms of concentrated poverty and exploitation — long hours, dangerous conditions, child labor, and urban squalor — while simultaneously generating the conditions for collective resistance that gradually improved those conditions; however, the extent of improvement varied enormously by time period, gender, and industry, and the most significant improvements (the eight-hour day, child labor laws, meaningful wages) came only in the latter half of the period and only through sustained struggle."

📝 Sample Contextualization

"Before the Industrial Revolution, most European workers were agricultural laborers or artisans in family-based cottage industries. Work was organized around seasonal rhythms and family life. The factory system that emerged in Britain after roughly 1760 transformed this entirely: labor was reorganized around the machine's schedule, production concentrated in urban factories rather than dispersed across the countryside, and workers became anonymous wage laborers rather than skilled artisans with a stake in the land. This fundamental shift in the nature of work — not just harder work, but qualitatively different work organized by entirely new principles — is the context within which all changes in working-class life during this period must be understood."

2
Essay Outline
  • Body ¶1 — NEGATIVE CHANGES: Immediate impact — 14–16-hour days, dangerous machinery, child labor (Sadler Report: children from age 5–6), urban poverty, tenements, cholera. Engels's "Condition of the Working Class in England" — documentation of Manchester slums.
  • Body ¶2 — RESPONSES AND IMPROVEMENTS: Labor unions + collective bargaining → shorter hours, higher wages, weekends. Chartist movement: political demands (universal male suffrage). Government reform (Factory Acts): Sadler Report → child labor restrictions.
  • Body ¶3 — COMPLEXITY: Uneven change by gender (working-class women still lower pay), time (Second Industrial Revolution post-1870: real wages rising), and industry (skilled vs. unskilled workers). Connect globally: as European workers improved conditions, workers in colonized territories often faced WORSENING conditions in the same period.

DBQ Rubric — Maximum 7 Points

1pt
ThesisDirectly answers "to what extent" (dramatically changed, but unevenly and gradually); identifies the mechanism (exploitation → resistance → improvement); establishes line of reasoning.
1pt
ContextualizationDescribes pre-industrial working life in enough detail to show what changed and explains WHY the factory system was qualitatively different.
2pts
Evidence: Document ContentDocuments might include factory inspection reports, Sadler Report excerpts, Chartist petitions, labor union records, government statistics on wages/hours. Accurately use at least 3 (1 pt) and at least 6 (2nd pt).
1pt
Evidence: Beyond DocumentsOutside evidence: Sadler Report, Engels's Condition of the Working Class, specific Factory Acts, Chartist petition with 3 million signatures, labor union victories, Second Industrial Revolution wage data.
1pt
Analysis: SourcingFor 3 documents: explain historical situation, purpose, point of view, or audience. Example: "A factory owner's testimony defending working conditions reflects his financial interest in avoiding regulation, likely leading him to minimize suffering documented in workers' own accounts."
1pt
Analysis: ComplexityShow uneven change by gender, time, industry. OR connect to broader global pattern: as European workers improved conditions, workers in colonized territories under European control faced worsening conditions in the same period.
Section 16

Sample LEQ — With Full Breakdown

📊 LEQ Basics

LEQ = 15% of AP exam. 40 minutes. From memory — no documents. Choose from three prompts.

"Compare the goals and outcomes of the American and Haitian Revolutions."
1
Decode

Command: "Compare" — BOTH similarities AND differences required
Topic: Goals (what they wanted) AND outcomes (what happened) — cover both
Two revolutions: American AND Haitian — both must be substantial

✅ Sample Thesis

"While the American and Haitian Revolutions both drew on Enlightenment principles of natural rights and popular sovereignty as their ideological foundation, they differed fundamentally in the social breadth of their application: the American Revolution, led by colonial elites, used Enlightenment ideas to justify political independence while preserving the existing social order — including slavery — whereas the Haitian Revolution, led by enslaved people, applied those same ideas to their most radical conclusion, abolishing slavery and establishing the world's first Black republic; the contrast in outcomes reveals how the same ideology can produce dramatically different results depending on who wields it and for what purpose."

2
Essay Outline
  • Contextualization: The Atlantic revolutionary moment — Enlightenment ideas circulating through books and pamphlets; French Revolution (1789) radicalizing the environment; American success (1776) demonstrating colonial revolution was possible
  • Body ¶1 — SIMILARITIES: Both drew on natural rights and popular sovereignty; both challenged existing political authority; both produced enduring political institutions (US Constitution, Haitian Constitution of 1805)
  • Body ¶2 — DIFFERENCES: American: elite Creole leadership, slavery preserved, women excluded, political change without social restructuring. Haitian: enslaved people's leadership, slavery abolished, first Black republic, radical social restructuring by necessity
  • Body ¶3 — COMPLEXITY: The Haitian Revolution exposed the American Revolution's hypocrisy — the same document ("all men are created equal") written by enslaved people's enslavers. The Haitian Revolution haunted American political debates about slavery until 1865.

LEQ Rubric — Maximum 6 Points

1pt
ThesisMakes a specific, defensible claim about the comparison (same ideological base, different social scope and outcome) and establishes a line of reasoning.
1pt
ContextualizationFull paragraph explaining the Atlantic revolutionary context — Enlightenment circulation, French Revolution's radicalization, American model — and connecting it to WHY both revolutions happened when they did.
2pts
EvidenceAmerican: Declaration of Independence, Locke's influence, preservation of slavery, US Constitution. Haitian: Toussaint L'Ouverture, Saint-Domingue demographics (500K enslaved vs. 30K whites), Declaration of Rights of Man applied to slavery, Haitian Constitution abolishing slavery.
2pts
Analysis & Reasoning: ComparisonEarn both by: (1) making explicit, direct comparisons (not just describing each separately) and (2) explaining WHY the differences matter — what does the comparison reveal about the relationship between ideology, social position, and revolutionary outcomes?
Section 17

Unit Summary — The Big Picture

💡 ENLIGHTENMENT THINKERS
Name at least four Enlightenment thinkers and explain the core idea each contributed. What is classical liberalism in the Unit 5 context — and why must you NOT confuse it with modern American political usage?
ANSWER
Locke: Natural rights — life, liberty, property. Government exists to protect these; right to overthrow it if it fails.
Rousseau: Social contract, popular sovereignty. "Man is born free and everywhere he is in chains."
Voltaire: Free speech, press, religion; critique of religious authority.
Montesquieu: Separation of powers into branches with checks and balances.
Adam Smith: Free market capitalism; laissez-faire; the "invisible hand" regulates markets without government.

Classical liberalism: In Unit 5, "liberal" = believing in individual rights, constitutional government, free markets, and limited government. NOT the modern left-wing political usage — that's a 20th-century shift. Unit 5 liberals are basically what we'd call libertarians today.
Which Enlightenment thinker is most directly associated with the idea of separation of powers into distinct branches of government?
Montesquieu (C) argued in The Spirit of the Laws that political power should be divided among separate branches (executive, legislative, judicial) with checks and balances to prevent tyranny. This directly influenced the U.S. Constitution. Locke is natural rights, Rousseau is social contract, Smith is free markets.
📜 THREE AP DOCUMENTS
What are the three documents specifically named in the AP World History curriculum for Unit 5? For each: who wrote it, when, and what Enlightenment argument does it make?
ANSWER
Declaration of Independence (1776): Jefferson. Locke-based — natural rights (life, liberty, pursuit of happiness), government by consent, right to overthrow. First successful colonial independence.

Declaration of Rights of Man and Citizen (1789): French Revolution; Jefferson helped draft it. Synthesizes all Enlightenment ideas — men born free and equal, freedom of speech/assembly/religion, due process, popular sovereignty. Freeman-pedia: "the culmination of Enlightenment thought in one document."

Jamaica Letter (1815): Simón Bolívar, written from exile in Jamaica. Makes the Enlightenment case for Latin American independence. Key quote: "The hatred that the peninsula has inspired in us is greater than the ocean between us."
🇭🇹 HAITIAN REVOLUTION
Who was Toussaint L'Ouverture? What makes the Haitian Revolution uniquely radical compared to the American or Latin American revolutions?
ANSWER
Toussaint L'Ouverture: An enslaved man who became the military/political leader of the Haitian Revolution. Self-educated in Enlightenment philosophy. Led forces that defeated Spanish, British, and French armies. Captured through French treachery; died in prison 1803. But the revolution continued — Haiti declared independence January 1, 1804.

Why uniquely radical: Only successful large-scale enslaved revolt in history that created a permanent independent nation. First Black republic. Second republic in the Western Hemisphere. The formerly enslaved became the citizens — a social revolution, not just a political one. Compare: American Revolution = political independence, social hierarchy unchanged; Latin American = Creole elites replace Spanish elites, casta system stays.
Which of the following best explains what made the Haitian Revolution uniquely radical in world history?
The Haitian Revolution (C) was uniquely radical because it was the only successful large-scale enslaved revolt that created a permanent independent nation — the first Black republic in the world. A was wrong because the U.S. was first (1776). D is wrong — Napoleon actually tried to re-enslave Haiti and was defeated. The formerly enslaved becoming the governing citizens is what makes it the most radical of all the Unit 5 revolutions.
🌎 LATIN AMERICAN REVOLUTIONS
Who led the Latin American independence movements? What social class did they belong to, and what did they not change after independence?
ANSWER
Leaders: Creoles (American-born Spanish elite) — Simón Bolívar (Venezuela/Colombia/Ecuador/Peru/Bolivia), José de San Martín (Argentina/Chile), Father Miguel Hidalgo (Mexico).

What they did NOT change: The casta social hierarchy remained largely intact. Indigenous peoples and enslaved Africans were excluded from meaningful political rights. Creoles replaced Peninsulares at the top but the inequality below didn't change. These were political revolutions — not social ones.
⚙️ INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION
Who is Freeman-pedia's "MVP of Unit 5" and why? Name at least five of the seven reasons the Industrial Revolution began in Britain specifically. What is the difference between the First and Second Industrial Revolution?
ANSWER
MVP: James Watt — his steam engine improvement (1769) was 10× more powerful than the Newcomen engine, using 75% less fuel. Without it, no Industrial Revolution. The watt (unit of power) is named after him.

Seven British advantages (W-N-P-E-U-A-C): Waterways (navigable rivers + canals) · Natural Resources (coal + iron in the same country) · Property Rights (investors protected) · Empire (raw materials + markets) · Urbanization (labor force already mobile) · Agricultural Revolution (freed up labor) · Capital (merchant class with money to invest).

First IR (~1760–1870): Coal and steam, textiles, iron, primarily British.
Second IR (~1870–1914): Steel (Bessemer process), chemicals, electricity (Edison), telegraph/radio, US and Germany challenge British dominance.
Which of the following was a feature of the Second Industrial Revolution, distinguishing it from the First?
The Second Industrial Revolution (C, ~1870–1914) is characterized by steel (Bessemer process), chemicals, electricity (Edison), and telecommunications (telegraph, radio), with the US and Germany emerging to challenge British industrial dominance. A and B describe the First Industrial Revolution (~1760–1870). D predates both — Smith published in 1776.
⚡ REACTIONS TO INDUSTRIAL CAPITALISM
What were the four main reactions to industrial capitalism? Give a specific historical example for each reaction.
ANSWER
1. Government reform: Sadler Report documented child labor abuses → Factory Acts limited working hours and age. Parliament responding to humanitarian pressure.

2. Labor unions: Chartism (Britain) — working-class movement demanding universal male suffrage. Workers organizing for political power rather than just wages.

3. Marxism/Communism: Communist Manifesto (1848, Marx & Engels) — abolish private property, proletarian revolution overthrows bourgeoisie. "Workers of the world, unite!"

4. Government-sponsored modernization: Tanzimat Reforms (Ottoman Empire) and Meiji Restoration (Japan) — states rapidly industrializing from the top down to compete with Western powers.
👩 CULT OF DOMESTICITY & WOMEN
What is the Cult of Domesticity? How did it differ for middle-class women vs. working-class women? What event directly challenged it?
ANSWER
Cult of Domesticity: Middle-class ideology defining women's proper role as devoted wife and homemaker. Four virtues: Piety, Purity, Submission, Domesticity. Women kept out of public/professional life.

Working-class women: Could NOT stay home — they worked in factories for lower wages than men. The Cult applied only to those who could afford it.

Challenge: Seneca Falls Convention (1848) and Declaration of Sentiments — explicitly modeled on the Declaration of Independence, demanding women's rights including suffrage. First women's rights convention in American history.
🗾 MEIJI RESTORATION & STATE-SPONSORED INDUSTRIALIZATION
What was the Meiji Restoration (1868)? What did Japan build in the following decades? How does it compare to China's Self-Strengthening Movement?
ANSWER
Meiji Restoration: Japan overthrew the Tokugawa Shogunate in 1868, restored Emperor Meiji, and launched comprehensive Western-style modernization — military, industry, banking, legal system, education. The most successful state-sponsored industrialization in history. Japan defeated China (1895) and Russia (1905). Freeman-pedia: "They don't lose a war again until 1945."

vs. China's Self-Strengthening Movement: China attempted selective modernization — adopt Western weapons but preserve Chinese culture and institutions. Failed because it was too partial and resisted by conservative officials. China lost the First Sino-Japanese War (1895) to the Japan it had assumed was inferior.
Japan's Meiji Restoration succeeded as a model of state-sponsored industrialization while China's Self-Strengthening Movement failed. Which of the following best explains the key difference?
The key difference (B) was the depth of reform. Japan comprehensively overhauled its military, government, legal system, banking, and education all at once. China's Self-Strengthening Movement tried to adopt Western weapons and technology while keeping Confucian institutions intact — a contradictory half-measure that failed. The result: Japan defeated China in 1895, proving which approach worked.
📝 One-Paragraph Summary of Unit 5

Unit 5 (1750–1900 CE) is the midpoint of AP World History and contains the biggest changes in the course. Two revolutions — one political, one economic — reshape everything. The political revolutions begin with the Enlightenment, producing three key documents (Declaration of Independence, Declaration of Rights of Man, Jamaica Letter) and three AP-specific revolutions: the American (preserves social hierarchy while gaining political independence), the Haitian (the most radical — abolishes slavery, creates the first Black republic, led by the enslaved), and the Latin American (led by Creole elites, achieves independence but preserves inequality). The Industrial Revolution, beginning in Britain around 1760 and powered by James Watt's steam engine, transforms how goods are made, where people live, and how wealth is distributed — creating new social classes (bourgeoisie and proletariat), new miseries (child labor, urban poverty, disease), and new ideologies (capitalism replacing mercantilism, Marxism, labor unions, Chartism). States that industrialize become powerful; those that don't become targets — as Unit 6's New Imperialism will show. Some governments force industrialization: the Meiji Restoration succeeds brilliantly; China's Self-Strengthening Movement fails catastrophically. By 1900, the world has entered a new industrial, politically transformed era — and the stage is set for the scramble for empire in Unit 6.

ResourceHow to Use It
Freeman-pedia (YouTube)Unit 5 is a multi-video marathon. Watch all the Industrial Revolution videos — they build on each other. His MVP video on James Watt is essential.
AP Classroom (Unit 5 Progress Check)Questions often use economic data charts and primary source excerpts from Enlightenment thinkers or revolutionary documents. Practice reading graphs alongside text sources.
Communist Manifesto (excerpts)Even five pages gives you the tone and argument. Know the "workers of the world, unite" call and the bourgeoisie/proletariat framework. AP DBQs sometimes use excerpts.
Declaration of Rights of Man (full text)Short enough to read completely. AP frequently uses it in DBQs. Knowing specific articles is more useful than just knowing it exists.
Jamaica Letter by Simón Bolívar (excerpts)The third required document. Read the key excerpts Freeman-pedia quotes. Passionate and accessible. AP sometimes uses it in DBQs about Latin American independence.
AP Central — Released FRQsSearch for Unit 5 DBQs and LEQs on the Industrial Revolution, Enlightenment, and revolutions. Timed practice with official rubrics is the highest-value exam prep.
Check-In

Do You Think You Can Move On?

Five units down, four to go. Do a quick honest check before jumping to Unit 6.

On a scale from 1–10, how confident do you feel about Unit 5? What feels solid? What still feels fuzzy? Be specific.
WHAT "READY" MEANS FOR UNIT 5
You are ready to move on to Unit 6 if you can:

✅ Name and explain all five Enlightenment thinkers and connect each to a specific revolution
✅ Name all three required AP documents — who wrote each, what they argued, and which Enlightenment ideas they drew on
✅ Explain what made the Haitian Revolution uniquely radical compared to the American
✅ Name Freeman-pedia's MVP of Unit 5 and explain why (James Watt + steam engine)
✅ Name at least five of the seven reasons Britain industrialized first
✅ Explain the difference between the First and Second Industrial Revolutions
✅ Name all four reactions to industrial capitalism and give one example of each
✅ Explain the Cult of Domesticity and who it applied to
✅ Compare Japan's Meiji Restoration to China's Self-Strengthening Movement
✅ Write a one-sentence thesis for a comparison essay about two of the Unit 5 revolutions

Fuzzy on any of those? Spend 10 focused minutes there. A solid 7 who keeps moving beats a perfectionist who never gets to Unit 6.

Unit 6 is New Imperialism — the industrialized West goes out to take the rest of the world. Everything you learned in Unit 5 explains why it happens.

Five units down. Unit 6 brings New Imperialism — the industrialized West goes out to take the rest of the world.

Unit 06 · c. 1750–1900 CE · 12–15% of AP Exam

Consequences of Industrialization

0%
🌍 Consequences of Industrialization — It's okay not to remember every detail. Work through it, stay curious, and be proud of what you're building.
Sections
00Introduction
01Rationales
02State Expansion
03Indigenous Resistance
04Global Economy
05Economic Imperialism
06Global Migrations
07Glossary
08Causes & Effects
09Timeline
10Thinking Skills
11Command Terms
12Pitfalls
13Sample DBQ
14Sample LEQ
15Unit Summary
★Ready to Move On?
AP World History Modern · Unit 6

Consequences of Industrialization  |  c. 1750–1900 CE

⚖️ Exam Weight

12–15% of the AP Exam. Freeman-pedia's honest take: "Unit 6 is the worst. But historically it's fascinating." Same time period as Unit 5 (1750–1900). Unit 5 = internal changes (revolutions, industrialization). Unit 6 = external consequences (what happens when industrial empires go global).

Unit 6 is the payoff for everything in Unit 5. The Industrial Revolution gave Europe (and eventually the US and Japan) an unprecedented military and economic advantage. Unit 6 is about what they did with that advantage: they used it to build empires, conquer most of the globe, and reshape the world's population through forced and voluntary migration.

⚠️ A Necessary Warning

Freeman-pedia introduces this unit with a warning: "Y'all are about to see some racism. Not like, oh my god that's racist, but systematic racism that is the predominant thought at the time." Unit 6 requires you to understand racist ideologies of the 19th century — not to agree with them, but to explain how they were used to justify imperial conquest. Understanding the history is not the same as endorsing it.

The Big Picture of Unit 6

  • The RATIONALES: The excuses the West used to justify imperialism (economic, religious, racist)
  • STATE EXPANSION: Where and how the West (and Japan) expanded their empires
  • INDIGENOUS RESISTANCE: How colonized peoples fought back
  • GLOBAL ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT: The M&Ms — Markets and Materials the West was after
  • ECONOMIC IMPERIALISM: Control without conquest — creating economic dependency
  • GLOBAL MIGRATIONS: Push and pull factors driving the movement of millions of people
What is the key difference between European imperialism in Unit 4 (c. 1450–1750) and New Imperialism in Unit 6 (c. 1870–1914)?
Section 01

Rationales for Imperialism — The Why

💡 Real Reasons vs. Stated Reasons
