Freeman-pedia calls the rationales "excuses" — because that's largely what they were. The real reason for New Imperialism was economic. Everything else was a justification layered on top. But understanding these justifications is essential because they shaped how colonizers saw themselves and how they implemented colonial policies.

Rationale 1: The Economic Motivation — The REAL Reason

🍫 M&Ms: Markets and Materials
  • Raw Materials: Industrial factories need inputs — cotton, rubber, coal, iron, petroleum, tin, copper. Africa, Asia, and Latin America had them in abundance. Controlling those territories meant controlling the supply chain.
  • Markets: Industrial factories produce goods faster than any domestic market can absorb. Colonies provided captive markets where European manufactured goods could be sold, often with tariffs blocking local competition.
  • Strategic Position: Control of key territories (Suez Canal, Cape of Good Hope, Pacific islands) gave military and commercial advantages over rival empires.
  • Investment Outlets: European capitalists had accumulated enormous capital that needed somewhere to go. Colonies offered investment opportunities in railroads, mines, and plantations.
What does "M&Ms: Markets and Materials" mean? Explain in your own words why these two economic drivers were the REAL reason for New Imperialism.
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
M&Ms = Markets and Materials. This is the economic core of New Imperialism:

Materials (Raw Materials): Industrial factories require enormous quantities of raw inputs — cotton for textiles, rubber for electrical insulation and eventually tires, iron ore for steel, petroleum for the internal combustion engine, tin for canning food, copper for wiring. Europe's own territories couldn't supply enough. Africa, Asia, and Latin America had these resources in abundance. Controlling those territories meant controlling the supply chain at the source.

Markets: Industrial factories can produce goods far faster than any domestic market can absorb them. You need somewhere to sell them. Colonies provided captive markets — you could impose tariffs that blocked local competition and kept colonial consumers buying European manufactured goods. A colony that had to buy British cloth rather than weave its own was a profit center.

AP key: All the other justifications (Christianity, Social Darwinism, White Man's Burden) are layered on top of this economic foundation. They made the exploitation sound noble. But strip away the ideology and you find factories needing inputs and markets — that's what drove the Scramble for Africa.

Rationale 2: Social Darwinism — Racism Dressed as Science

⚠️ What You're About to Read Is Racist. It's Here Because You Need to Understand It.

Charles Darwin's theory of evolution (1859) introduced "survival of the fittest" — organisms better adapted to their environment survive. This was a scientific theory about biology. It said nothing about human societies, cultures, or races.

Western imperialists took Darwin's biological theory and applied it to human cultures and races — something Darwin himself never intended or endorsed. This misapplication was called Social Darwinism:

  • Western cultures are powerful because they are evolutionarily "fit"
  • Non-Western cultures are less powerful because they are evolutionarily "unfit"
  • Therefore, just as species go extinct, non-Western cultures would "naturally" die out in competition with superior Western civilization
  • Imperial conquest was therefore not just justified but inevitable

This is intellectually dishonest and morally horrifying. Social Darwinism weaponized a legitimate scientific theory to justify exploitation and genocide. The AP specifically names it as an imperial ideology you must understand.

Rationale 3: The White Man's Burden — The Most Cynical Justification

📜 Rudyard Kipling's "The White Man's Burden" (1899)

British author Kipling wrote this poem in 1899 in response to the American annexation of the Philippines. The argument:

  • The West is not in your country because they want your resources (even though they do)
  • The West is in your country because it is their DUTY — their BURDEN — to civilize the world's less advanced peoples
  • It is actually hard work and sacrifice for the West to civilize the world. They're doing you a favor.
  • The colonized are "half devil and half child" who need to be guided toward civilization

The French had a similar concept: the mission civilisatrice ("civilizing mission"). Both performed the same function: reframe brutal economic exploitation as humanitarian charity.

Freeman-pedia's verdict: "I feel gross even having to cover this racist garbage. But those were the prevailing rationales."

What is Social Darwinism? What did Darwin's actual theory say, and how did imperialists misuse it? Why is the distinction between the real theory and the misapplication important for the AP?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Darwin's actual theory (1859): Evolution by natural selection. Organisms that are better adapted to their specific environment survive and reproduce. "Survival of the fittest" means "best suited to this environment" — not "strongest" or "best." It was a theory about biological organisms, not human societies, cultures, or races. Darwin himself never argued that any human culture or race was superior.

How imperialists misused it: Social Darwinism took this biological theory and applied it to human societies, arguing that Western cultures were "evolutionarily superior" and non-Western cultures were "unfit" and would naturally die out. This provided pseudo-scientific legitimacy for racism and imperial conquest — making exploitation seem like natural law rather than a moral choice.

Why the distinction matters for AP: Always note explicitly that Social Darwinism was a misapplication of Darwin's theory. Calling it "pseudo-science" or a "misappropriation" shows analytical sophistication that AP graders reward. A student who just says "Europeans believed in Social Darwinism" misses the point; a student who explains it was a deliberate distortion of legitimate science for ideological purposes earns higher marks.
Which rationale for imperialism is best described as "reframing brutal economic exploitation as humanitarian charity"?
Section 02

State Expansion — New Imperialism in Action

📊 The Numbers Tell the Story

In 1800: Europeans controlled about 35% of the Earth's land surface.
By 1914: Europeans controlled about 84% of the Earth's land surface.
Only two African states remained fully independent in 1914: Ethiopia and Liberia.

Methods of Imperial Expansion

MethodWhat It WasKey Example
Military ConquestDirect military force — armies and naval forces conquer and occupy territory. The Maxim gun (world's first machine gun, 1884, 500+ rounds per minute) gave European armies an overwhelming advantage.Opium Wars: Britain forced China to open ports and accept humiliating trade terms through military force (1839–42, 1856–60).
DiplomacyNegotiating treaties that gave imperial powers access or control without deploying armies — often with force in the background.Berlin Conference (1884–85): 14 European powers divided Africa among themselves. No Africans invited. Lines drawn on a map.
Settler ColonialismThe mother country sends large numbers of citizens to permanently settle in a colony, gradually displacing indigenous population. Goal is replacement, not just extraction.New Zealand, Australia, South Africa, North America — British settlers displaced Māori, Aboriginal, and Indigenous peoples.
Economic ControlControlling an economy through investment, debt, and trade dependency without formal political conquest.Britain in Argentina; US fruit companies in Central America ("banana republics").
Private CompaniesJoint-stock companies or individual rulers held colonial territory as private property before governments eventually took over.British East India Company controlled most of India until the Sepoy Mutiny (1857); King Leopold II personally owned the Congo until 1908.
What was the Berlin Conference? Why does it matter that no Africans were invited?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
The Berlin Conference (1884–85) was convened by German Chancellor Otto von Bismarck to formally regulate the "Scramble for Africa" and prevent European wars over colonial territory. Fourteen European nations attended and negotiated rules for claiming African territory: they had to establish effective occupation (actually control it, not just claim it) and agreed to the eventual abolition of slavery and free navigation of major rivers.

Why no Africans were invited matters: The entire continent of Africa was divided among European powers without a single African voice in the discussion. The powers drew boundary lines across the continent with complete disregard for existing African kingdoms, ethnic groups, language communities, trade networks, and political boundaries. Some ethnic groups were split across two or three different European colonies. Some longtime rival groups were placed in the same colony and forced to be "one nation." These completely arbitrary borders are a direct cause of most of Africa's post-independence political conflicts, civil wars, and instability that continue to this day. Every time you see a news story about conflict in Africa along seemingly strange boundaries, that is often the Berlin Conference's legacy.

India Under British Rule — The British Raj

India is the most important single colonial territory in Unit 6 for the AP exam. British involvement began with the East India Company (Unit 4) and evolved into the crown jewel of the British Empire.

⚔️ The Sepoy Mutiny (Indian Rebellion) of 1857

Sepoys were Indian soldiers employed by the British East India Company. In 1857, a rumor spread that new rifle cartridges — which soldiers had to bite before loading — were greased with beef fat (offensive to Hindus) and pork fat (offensive to Muslims). Whether true or not, this triggered a massive uprising across the subcontinent. The rebellion was suppressed brutally.

The Result: The British Raj (1858–1947): The British government abolished the East India Company and took direct Crown control of India. Queen Victoria was proclaimed Empress of India in 1877. India's rail network, telegraph system, legal system, and educational institutions were built during this period — primarily to serve British imperial needs, but with lasting developmental consequences.

The Sepoy Mutiny of 1857 led directly to which major change in how Britain governed India?
Section 03

Indigenous Resistance — The People Strike Back

"Whatever happens, we have got the Maxim gun, and they have not."
— Hilaire Belloc, summarizing the power imbalance of the imperial era

The colonized peoples fought back. They fought brilliantly, often heroically, sometimes sacrificing everything. In this period, they almost always lost — not because they were less courageous or less intelligent, but because the Maxim gun represents all the technological and industrial advantages Western armies carried: modern firearms, railroads for rapid troop deployment, telegraphs for coordination, and industrial economies that could sustain prolonged wars indefinitely.

The College Board gives 11 illustrative examples. Freeman-pedia narrows it to three key examples, one per category. Know all three well.

Category 1: Direct Armed Resistance

⚔️ War of the Golden Stool — Ashanti vs. Britain (1900, modern Ghana)

The Ashanti Kingdom had been fighting the British on and off for 70 years. The final confrontation was triggered by British Governor Hodgson demanding that the Ashanti hand over their golden stool for Queen Victoria to sit on. The golden stool was not a throne — it was a sacred symbol, a physical representation of the soul and spirit of the Ashanti nation, embodying the dead, the living, and those yet to be born. The demand was not arrogance — it was sacrilege.

Yaa Asantewaa's Speech — Freeman-pedia calls this "probably the best speech in the entire course":

"If you the men of Ashanti will not go forward, then we will. We, the women, will. I shall call upon my fellow women. We will fight the white men. We will fight to the last of us falls in the battlefields."

Outcome: Yaa Asantewaa was captured and exiled. The Ashanti remained under British control. But the British never got the golden stool. They officially stopped looking in 1921. The sacred symbol outlasted the empire that tried to take it.

📦 Boxer Rebellion (China, 1899–1901)

Chinese nationalists ("Boxers") rose up against foreign influence in China, besieging foreign embassies in Beijing. An international military force suppressed the rebellion. China was forced to pay enormous indemnities — further humiliating the Qing Dynasty.

💀 Herero and Nama Genocide (German Southwest Africa, 1904–1908)

The Herero and Nama peoples of modern Namibia rose up against German colonial rule after land theft and forced labor. Germany's response: a campaign of extermination — driving people into the desert, poisoning water holes, establishing concentration camps. Approximately 80% of the Herero people and 50% of the Nama were killed. This is now recognized as the 20th century's first genocide.

What was the War of the Golden Stool? What did Yaa Asantewaa's speech demonstrate about resistance to imperialism? What was the most significant outcome — and why?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
The War of the Golden Stool (1900) was triggered when British Governor Hodgson demanded the Ashanti people hand over their golden stool — not understanding (or not caring) that it was not a throne but a sacred object representing the soul of the Ashanti nation. Demanding it was not just arrogant — it was a direct attack on Ashanti identity and spirituality.

Yaa Asantewaa's speech demonstrates: (1) that women played leading roles in anti-colonial resistance; (2) that resistance could be motivated by cultural and spiritual values, not just political or economic ones; (3) that indigenous peoples were active agents who chose to fight rather than passive victims.

Most significant outcome: The Ashanti were militarily defeated and Yaa Asantewaa was captured and exiled. BUT — the British never got the golden stool. They officially stopped searching for it in 1921. This is the AP's perfect example of how resistance could succeed culturally and spiritually even when it failed militarily. The sacred symbol outlasted the empire that tried to take it — and Ghana gained independence in 1957.

Category 2: New State Formation — The Zulu Kingdom

🛡️ Zulu Kingdom and the Battle of Isandlwana (January 22, 1879)

The Zulu Kingdom was founded by Shaka Zulu in the early 19th century in what is now KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa. Under Shaka, the Zulu developed highly effective military tactics and created a powerful centralized state.

At the Battle of Isandlwana (1879), a Zulu force of roughly 20,000 warriors attacked and annihilated a British column of 1,800 men. Over 1,300 British soldiers were killed in a single day. This was the single largest defeat in the entire history of the British Empire at the hands of any indigenous people. Freeman-pedia: "The Brits won't have a day this bad again until World War One."

Outcome: Whatever happens, we have got the Maxim gun, and they have not. The British regrouped, deployed their full industrial military capacity, and eventually defeated the Zulu Kingdom. But Isandlwana showed that indigenous forces, fighting on their home terrain with tactical brilliance, could defeat modern European armies even without equivalent technology.

🦁 Ethiopia — The One That Got Away

Ethiopia under Emperor Menelik II is the most important example of a non-European state successfully resisting colonization. At the Battle of Adwa (1896), Ethiopian forces defeated the Italian army — the first decisive African military victory over a European colonial army. Ethiopia remained independent throughout the entire colonial era. Menelik II was a brilliant diplomat who acquired European firearms, built a modern army, and played European powers against each other.

Category 3: Rebellion and Other Forms of Resistance

🐄 The Xhosa Cattle-Killing Movement (South Africa, 1856–57)

Freeman-pedia's warning: "This is super tragic." The Xhosa people of South Africa were under enormous pressure from British colonization — land seizures, military defeats, and cattle lung disease (spread by European cattle) devastating their traditional way of life. In this desperate context, a prophecy spread: if the Xhosa killed all their cattle and destroyed all their crops, their ancestral spirits would rise, drive the British into the sea, and restore their prosperity.

Between 1856–1857, the Xhosa killed approximately 400,000 cattle and destroyed their grain stores. The famine that followed killed approximately 75% of the Xhosa people in the affected area. The British took the remaining, weakened Xhosa land with almost no resistance. The cattle-killing movement represents the desperation that colonial oppression could drive people to — and the catastrophic consequences of that desperation.

The three resistance examples — Ashanti (War of the Golden Stool), Zulu (Battle of Isandlwana), and Xhosa (cattle-killing) — all end in loss for the colonized people. But what does each reveal about different forms of resistance and different dimensions of the colonial experience?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Ashanti/Yaa Asantewaa: Shows that resistance could be about cultural and spiritual values (the golden stool), not just political power. Shows women's leadership in anti-colonial movements. Shows that symbolic resistance can outlast military defeat — the British never got the stool.

Zulu/Isandlwana: Shows that indigenous peoples could achieve significant military victories even against technologically superior forces, when fighting on home terrain with tactical brilliance. Also shows the ultimate limits of such victories — the British absorbed the defeat, regrouped, and deployed overwhelming force.

Xhosa cattle-killing: Shows the psychological and cultural devastation that colonial oppression could produce — pushing people to desperate measures that inadvertently served colonial interests. Shows that resistance doesn't always take military or political form; it can be spiritual, even when tragically misguided. Also shows how colonial disruption (cattle disease from European cattle) could undermine traditional economic systems in ways that destabilized entire societies.

Together: These three examples show that resistance was varied, constant, creative, and ultimately overwhelmed by the Maxim gun and industrial military power — but that the colonized were never passive.
Which African nation was the ONLY one to successfully maintain its independence throughout the entire colonial era by defeating a European army?
Section 04

Global Economic Development — What the West Was After

Key Raw Materials Driving Imperial Expansion

ResourceWhere It Came FromWhy It Mattered
RubberAmazon (Brazil) originally; transplanted to Congo, Malaya, and other tropical regionsEssential for industrial wiring insulation and eventually tires. King Leopold II exploited the Congo for rubber with genocidal brutality. The rubber trade drove some of the worst human rights abuses of the colonial era.
CottonIndia, Egypt, American South (US)Primary raw material of the textile industry — the heart of the First Industrial Revolution. Britain's demand made India and Egypt economically dependent on British textile markets.
Coal and IronBritain, Germany, Pennsylvania (US), parts of AfricaFuel and material of the Industrial Revolution. Countries with abundant coal and iron had a massive industrial advantage.
Petroleum / OilPennsylvania (US) first; later Middle East, Southeast AsiaThe Second Industrial Revolution ran on oil. Would become the 20th century's most strategically important resource.
Guano (Bird/Bat Poop)Pacific islands, caves, coastal islandsFreeman-pedia's most unusual resource. Until synthetic fertilizers, bird/bat excrement was the best available fertilizer. Also used in gunpowder. The 1856 US Guano Islands Act allowed Americans to claim any unclaimed island with guano deposits.

Freeman-pedia's MVP of Unit 6: King Leopold II

⭐ MVP of Unit 6 — The Face of Imperial Atrocity
King Leopold II of Belgium — Personal Owner of the Congo

At the Berlin Conference in 1884, Leopold — not Belgium, not a Belgian company, but Leopold personally — took control of the Congo Free State as his own private property, promising to end the Arab slave trade and bring civilization to the Congolese.

What Leopold actually did:

  • Every Congolese village was assigned a rubber quota that had to be met regardless of conditions
  • If a village failed to meet its quota, Leopold's agents would cut off the hands of the villagers as punishment — including children
  • Soldiers were required to present severed hands as evidence that they had enforced the quotas, creating a horrific economy of mutilation
  • Missionaries (including Alice Seeley Harris) photographed the atrocities; Joseph Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899) was an early literary account; Mark Twain wrote King Leopold's Soliloquy (1905) as devastating satire
  • International pressure eventually forced Leopold to hand the Congo to the Belgian state in 1908
  • Estimated death toll: 10–20 million Congolese people

Leopold II is Freeman-pedia's MVP of Unit 6 not because he was the greatest person but because he best represents the true nature of imperial exploitation stripped of all civilizing rhetoric. His statues remained standing in Belgium until Black Lives Matter protests in 2020 renewed demands for their removal. Many remain.

Why is King Leopold II Freeman-pedia's MVP of Unit 6? What did he do in the Congo, and why does his case matter for understanding New Imperialism broadly?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
King Leopold II personally owned the Congo Free State (1885–1908) as his private property — not Belgium's colony, but his personal estate. He promised at the Berlin Conference to end the Arab slave trade and bring civilization; what he actually ran was a rubber extraction operation enforced through systematic terror: rubber quotas for every village, and severed hands as the punishment for failure to meet those quotas. Leopold's agents were required to produce severed hands as proof they had enforced the quotas — leading to an economy of mutilation that killed approximately 10–20 million Congolese people through violence, starvation, and disease.

Why this matters for understanding imperialism broadly: Leopold's Congo shows what imperialism looks like when the civilizing rhetoric is stripped away. Other imperial powers (Britain, France) at least operated through formal governmental structures with some accountability. Leopold operated through personal terror with zero accountability. His case makes visible the exploitative logic that underlay all imperialism — it just happened more nakedly in the Congo than elsewhere. Missionaries and journalists who exposed Leopold's crimes helped create the first international human rights movement, showing that colonial subjects had allies even in the colonizing countries.
Section 05

Economic Imperialism — Control Without Conquest

💰 How Economic Imperialism Works

Not all imperialism looks like the Berlin Conference. Sometimes the most effective form of control is economic — making a country's economy so dependent on yours that you effectively control its government without ever officially colonizing it.

  1. Identify a country with resources you need or a market you want
  2. Invest heavily — buy mines, build railroads, fund plantations, establish banks
  3. Make the country dependent on your capital, technology, and markets for economic survival
  4. Once the economy depends on you, the government must follow policies that protect your interests — without you conquering anything

Result: Effective control of a country without the administrative costs or diplomatic complications of formal colonization. Also called neo-colonialism.

CountryWho Had Economic ControlHow It Worked
ChinaBritain (and other Western powers)Opium Wars forced China to open ports and accept unequal treaties: extraterritoriality, forced opium imports, favorable trade terms. Britain made China economically dependent while extracting goods.
ArgentinaBritainBritain invested enormous capital in Argentine railroads, banks, and beef export industry. By the late 19th century, British investors had more influence over Argentine economic policy than the Argentine government itself.
EgyptBritainEgypt's cotton economy became dependent on British textile mills. When Egypt went into debt (partly building the Suez Canal), Britain took control of Egyptian finances — then occupied Egypt militarily in 1882.
Central AmericaUnited Fruit Company (US)American companies owned vast plantations, railroads, and port facilities. More economic/political power than the governments themselves. The derisive term banana republic comes directly from this pattern.
What is economic imperialism (neo-colonialism)? How is it different from formal colonialism, and why might it actually be MORE effective in some ways?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Economic imperialism (neo-colonialism): a foreign power controls a country's economy and political decisions through heavy investment, trade dependency, and debt — without formally colonizing it. The controlled country appears independent (has its own government, flag, laws) but in practice must follow policies that protect the dominant power's economic interests.

How it differs from formal colonialism: In formal colonialism, a foreign government directly rules the territory (like the British Raj in India). In economic imperialism, the controlled country keeps its own government and sovereignty on paper. The British didn't need to declare Argentina a colony to control it — their investments and trade relationships gave them that control at lower cost.

Why it can be more effective: (1) Lower cost — no need to deploy armies, administer a colonial government, or deal with constant armed resistance; (2) More legitimate-looking — the controlled country appears independent, making resistance and international criticism harder; (3) Self-sustaining — once a country's economy depends on your capital and markets, its own elites often voluntarily maintain the dependency because their wealth depends on it; (4) Flexible — economic relationships can be maintained even as formal political arrangements change.

AP examples: Britain in Argentina (more British investment than governmental), US in Central America ("banana republics"), China in many African countries today (the same pattern, applied in the 21st century).
Section 06

Global Migrations — The World on the Move

The industrial economy and imperial expansion set tens of millions of people in motion. Freeman-pedia: "There's a good chance that your people got to where they are now during this time period."

📌 Essential Terms for AP Essays

Push Factors: Bad things in your home country that force you to leave — famine, war, poverty, political persecution, disease, lack of economic opportunity. You are being pushed out.

Pull Factors: Good things in a destination country that attract you — jobs, higher wages, available land, political freedom, family connections already there. You are being pulled in.

Most migrations involve both. Always identify both when discussing migration in AP essays.

Who MigratedFrom → ToPush / PullSignificance
Irish to USIreland → New York, BostonPUSH: Great Famine (1845–52) — potato failure killed 1 million; PULL: jobs in American industrial cities1 million died; 1+ million migrated. Ireland's population fell 25%. Irish immigrants faced severe discrimination but became a major political and cultural force.
Chinese to US and AustraliaChina → California, Australia, CanadaPUSH: Opium Wars humiliation, Taiping Rebellion (~20 million dead), poverty; PULL: gold rushes, railroad construction jobsChinese immigrants built the US transcontinental railroad. Both US (Chinese Exclusion Act, 1882) and Australia (White Australia Policy) responded with explicitly racist immigration restrictions.
Italians to ArgentinaItaly → Argentina, Brazil, USPUSH: Italian unification wars, rural poverty; PULL: Argentina's 1853 constitution welcomed immigrants; booming agricultural economy2 million Italians moved to Argentina 1888–1920. Today 63% of Argentinians have Italian ancestry. Pope Francis and Lionel Messi are both Argentine-born of Italian descent.
Indians to British ColoniesIndia → Caribbean, South Africa, East Africa, FijiPUSH: British colonial policies impoverished Indian peasants; PULL: labor contracts in British sugar colonies after slavery abolishedThe indentured labor system replaced slavery in British colonies. Technically free (workers had contracts), but conditions were often barely distinguishable from slavery.
Europeans to AmericasBritain, Germany, Scandinavia, Eastern Europe → US, Canada, Argentina, BrazilPUSH: Industrial poverty, overpopulation, religious persecution (especially Jews from Russia after pogroms); PULL: available land, high wages, political freedomRoughly 55–60 million Europeans migrated to the Americas between 1820 and 1930. Transformed the demographics of North and South America permanently.

Effects of Migration: Enclaves and Xenophobia

Ethnic Enclaves

When migrants arrive, they naturally gravitate toward people who share their language, food, and culture. Result: ethnic enclaves — Chinatown, Little Italy, Polish Hill, Indian communities in South Africa. Provide economic and social support networks but can also reinforce cultural separation and invite hostility.

Xenophobia and Discriminatory Laws

Fear or dislike of foreigners is a consistent response to large-scale immigration. When large numbers of people with different languages or appearances arrive, some portion of the receiving society reacts with hostility, blaming newcomers for economic competition, crime, or cultural change.

Discriminatory PolicyCountry / DateWhat It Did
Chinese Exclusion ActUnited States, 1882The only US law ever to restrict immigration based on a specific nationality. Prohibited Chinese laborers from entering the US. Not repealed until 1943. Directly responded to anti-Chinese racism among white workers who resented Chinese labor competition.
White Australia PolicyAustralia, 1901–1973A series of laws effectively banning non-European immigration to Australia. Explicitly designed to maintain Australia as a predominantly white, British-descended society. Freeman-pedia describes an 1888 Australian cartoon that blamed Chinese immigrants for cheap labor, robbery, opium, smallpox, and immorality.
Russian PogromsRussian Empire, 1880s–1900sState-tolerated or organized attacks on Jewish communities. Killed thousands and drove millions of Jews to emigrate, primarily to the United States. A major push factor for Eastern European Jewish immigration to America.
What are push factors and pull factors? Give a specific push AND pull factor for at least TWO of the migration examples in this section.
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Irish to the US:
PUSH: The Great Famine (1845–52) — a potato blight wiped out Ireland's primary food source, killing approximately 1 million people and leaving 1+ million more facing starvation. British policies that continued to export food from Ireland during the famine made conditions worse.
PULL: Jobs in American industrial cities (Boston, New York) and construction (canals, railroads); lower food prices; political freedom from British rule.

Chinese to the US/Australia:
PUSH: "Shove factors" — the Opium Wars humiliation left China economically devastated; the Taiping Rebellion (largest civil war in history, ~20 million dead) created massive instability; poverty in southern China was extreme.
PULL: California Gold Rush (1848), railroad construction jobs (transcontinental railroad built largely by Chinese workers), Australian gold rushes; higher wages than available in China.

Eastern European Jews to the US:
PUSH: Russian pogroms — state-tolerated massacres of Jewish communities; legal restrictions on where Jews could live and what occupations they could hold; extreme poverty in the Pale of Settlement.
PULL: Religious freedom in the United States; economic opportunities in American industrial cities; family connections already there.
Section 07

Glossary of Key Terms

Test yourself on each term before revealing. Mark how well you know it — your marks are saved.

Section 08

Causes, Consequences & Significance

Why Did New Imperialism Happen When It Did?

📌 Causes
  • Industrial demand: Factories needed raw materials and markets — M&Ms
  • Military technology: Maxim gun, breech-loading rifles, and steamships made conquest faster and cheaper than ever
  • Transportation: Railroads and steamships made projecting power to distant territories feasible
  • European competition: As Germany and Italy unified (Unit 5) and the US expanded, established powers competed aggressively to lock down territories before rivals
  • Weakness of target states: Qing Dynasty devastated by Taiping Rebellion; Mughal Empire effectively ended; Ottoman Empire declining
⚡ Consequences
  • Africa and Asia largely colonized by 1914; traditional political structures destroyed or subordinated
  • Local economies restructured around colonial needs: growing cash crops, extracting minerals
  • Imperial rivalries (especially Britain vs. Germany) contributed directly to World War I (Unit 7)
  • Seeds of anti-colonial nationalism planted: Indian National Congress (1885), Pan-African consciousness — all responses that eventually produce independence movements (Unit 8)
  • Massive demographic migrations as colonial labor demands moved millions of people
🔭 Significance — Why Unit 6 Matters Today

New Imperialism explains why the world looks the way it does today. Colonial-era borders still define most African and Asian states. Colonial-era economic relationships (extracting raw materials, selling manufactured goods) still shape North-South global inequality. Colonial-era racial hierarchies left psychological and social legacies that shape politics and society in both colonizer and colonized nations. Understanding Unit 6 is essential for understanding the 20th and 21st centuries.

Section 09

Timeline of Unit 6 (c. 1750–1900)

1807

Britain Abolishes the Slave Trade

First major power to abolish the transatlantic slave trade; uses its navy to enforce the ban. Slavery in British colonies abolished in 1833.

1839–42

First Opium War

Britain forces China to open ports and accept the Treaty of Nanjing. China cedes Hong Kong. Beginning of China's "Century of Humiliation."

1845–52

Irish Great Famine

Potato blight kills 1 million; 1+ million more emigrate. Ireland's population falls 25%. Major push factor for Irish immigration to the US.

1857

Sepoy Mutiny / Indian Rebellion

Massive Indian uprising against the East India Company. Suppressed brutally. Leads to establishment of the British Raj under Crown rule.

1858

British Raj Established

British Crown takes direct control of India. Queen Victoria becomes Empress of India in 1877. India is the "jewel in the crown" of the British Empire.

1879

Battle of Isandlwana

Zulu forces kill 1,300 British soldiers — the worst defeat in the entire history of the British Empire at indigenous hands. British regrouped and defeated the Zulus by year's end.

1882

Chinese Exclusion Act (US); Britain Occupies Egypt

First US law banning immigration from a specific nationality. Britain occupies Egypt to protect financial interests and control the Suez Canal.

1884–85

Berlin Conference; Maxim Gun Invented

14 European powers divide Africa with no African representatives. Hiram Maxim invents the first machine gun (500+ rounds/minute), fundamentally shifting imperial power.

1885

Indian National Congress Founded

Educated Indians form a political organization to demand greater participation in governance. The institutional seed of the eventual independence movement.

1895–96

Japan Defeats China; Ethiopia Defeats Italy at Adwa

Japan wins First Sino-Japanese War, acquires Taiwan. Ethiopia defeats Italy at Adwa — the only African nation to defeat a European colonial army. Two opposite outcomes for non-Western nations.

1898

Spanish-American War; US Acquires Philippines and Cuba

Spain loses its remaining empire to the US. America becomes an imperial power in the Pacific and Caribbean.

1899–1901

Boxer Rebellion; War of the Golden Stool

China: nationalist uprising against foreign influence suppressed by international forces. Ghana: Yaa Asantewaa leads Ashanti resistance against British attempt to seize the golden stool.

1904–08

Herero and Nama Genocide; Leopold's Congo Exposed

Germany exterminates 80% of the Herero people in SW Africa (20th century's first genocide). Missionaries and journalists expose Leopold's Congo atrocities.

Fill in: The Battle of _____ (1896) was the first time a major African nation decisively defeated a European colonial army, allowing Ethiopia to remain independent throughout the colonial era.
Section 10

Historical Thinking Skills

1. Multi-Factor Causation — Why Did Imperialism Happen?

WEAK: "Imperialism happened because Europeans were greedy."

STRONG: "New Imperialism in the late 19th century resulted from the convergence of economic necessity (industrial factories required raw materials from Africa and Asia and markets for manufactured goods), military technological advantage (the Maxim gun, steamships, and breech-loading rifles gave European armies insurmountable advantages over indigenous forces), European nationalist competition (newly unified Germany and Italy competed with established Britain and France for colonial territory), and the weakness of potential target states (the Qing Dynasty had been devastated by the Taiping Rebellion; the Mughal Empire had collapsed; the Ottoman Empire was declining). No single factor explains imperialism — it required all of these conditions to coincide."

2. CCOT — Continuity and Change

CONTINUITIES
  • European powers continue to expand globally (building on Unit 4 trading post foundations)
  • Economic exploitation of colonial territories continues: raw materials extracted; manufactured goods sold back
  • Indigenous resistance to foreign domination continues throughout human history
  • Migration continues as a fundamental human activity
  • Racial and cultural hierarchies in colonial relationships continue from Unit 4
CHANGES
  • Scale shifts from coastal trading posts (Unit 4) to conquest of vast interior territories
  • Berlin Conference formalizes a new type of imperial order: Africa explicitly divided through diplomatic negotiation
  • Maxim gun creates an irreversible technological power imbalance
  • Social Darwinism gives racism a pseudo-scientific veneer — new and more systematic than before
  • Xenophobic legislation (Chinese Exclusion Act, White Australia Policy) is new as a government response to migration
Practice: Write a two-sentence thesis for this prompt: "Evaluate the extent to which economic motivations drove European imperialism in Africa between 1870 and 1900."
SAMPLE THESIS
"While economic motivations — particularly the industrial demand for African raw materials and the need to secure markets for European manufactured goods — were the primary drivers of European imperialism in Africa between 1870 and 1900, a complete explanation requires acknowledging the significant roles of strategic competition between European powers (especially the newly unified Germany), Social Darwinist and missionary ideologies that provided genuine (if self-serving) justifications beyond pure profit, and the specific strategic value of African geographic positions like the Suez Canal and Cape of Good Hope that transcended immediate economic calculation."
Section 11

Common Command Terms for Unit 6

TermWhat It MeansUnit 6 Example
IdentifyName one specific thing. Minimal explanation."Identify one effect of British rule on Indian society." → The construction of a railroad network across India, which initially served British military and commercial purposes but also created infrastructure that facilitated Indian economic integration and eventually independence movements.
ExplainMake clear HOW or WHY. Show the connection."Explain one cause of the Scramble for Africa." → Connect industrial demand to colonial expansion: European factories required raw materials that Africa possessed, and industrial military technology (the Maxim gun, steamships) made rapid conquest feasible, creating both the incentive and the capability for rapid African colonization simultaneously.
Evaluate the extent to whichJudge HOW MUCH. Evidence on both sides."Evaluate the extent to which economic factors explain New Imperialism." → Yes: factories needed M&Ms; investment capital needed outlets; competition for resources drove the scramble. But: nationalist prestige, strategic competition, religious motivations, and Social Darwinist ideology all played independent roles. A purely economic explanation is incomplete.
CompareSimilarities AND differences. Both must be substantial."Compare British rule in India and King Leopold's Congo." → Both: exploited local labor, extracted resources, used violence to maintain control. Different: Britain created a formal administrative structure (Raj) with some legal framework; Leopold used personal terror with no accountability and murdered millions.
ContextualizeBroader historical context explaining WHY something happened."Contextualize indigenous resistance to European imperialism." → Connect to: the long history of resistance throughout Units 1–5; the specific circumstances making 19th-century resistance more difficult (Maxim gun, railroads, telegraph); and the seeds of nationalist consciousness planted by imperial education that eventually produced independence movements.
Section 12

Common Pitfalls & How to Avoid Them

❌

Pitfall 1: Ignoring Non-European Empires (Especially Japan and the US)

The Problem: Students focus exclusively on British, French, and Belgian imperialism and forget that the US (Manifest Destiny, Spanish-American War, Philippines) and Japan (Taiwan, Korea, China) were also imperial powers in this period.

✅ The Fix: Japan is the most important non-European imperial power to know. Its defeat of China (1895) and Russia (1905) demonstrated that non-Western nations could master industrial power and imperial methods, directly challenging Social Darwinist assumptions about Western superiority.

❌

Pitfall 2: Romanticizing Empire — The "Civilizing Mission" Was Real

The Problem: Writing about imperialism as if the civilizing mission was genuine and beneficial — that Europeans really brought progress to Africa and Asia. This ignores the fundamental exploitative nature: extraction of resources, destruction of local industries, disruption of political systems, and atrocities like Leopold's Congo.

✅ The Fix: Always note the costs to colonized peoples alongside any genuine developmental benefits. Ask: who built India's railroads, and who primarily benefited? Who grew Egypt's cotton, and who profited? Colonial infrastructure typically served imperial needs first.

❌

Pitfall 3: Writing About Colonized Peoples as Passive

The Problem: Writing that colonized peoples passively accepted European domination ignores the evidence and will cost you points on any essay about resistance.

✅ The Fix: Know at least three specific resistance examples: Yaa Asantewaa and the Ashanti (direct armed resistance), Menelik II and Ethiopia (successful military resistance), and the Indian National Congress (political resistance). Use them as specific evidence.

❌

Pitfall 4: One-Way Analysis — Effects on the Colonized Only

The Problem: Imperialism changed Europe too. Imperial rivalries (especially Germany vs. Britain and France) contributed to World War I. Imperial wealth funded European economic growth. Social Darwinism, developed to justify empire, also infected domestic European politics.

✅ The Fix: For any essay about imperialism's effects, include at least one effect on the imperial powers themselves. The most important: imperial rivalries as a cause of World War I is the bridge between Unit 6 and Unit 7.

❌

Pitfall 5: Treating Social Darwinism as Legitimate Science

The Problem: Writing about Social Darwinism as if it were a scientific theory rather than a racist ideology. Darwin's actual theory was about biological organisms; it said nothing about cultural or racial superiority.

✅ The Fix: Always note explicitly that Social Darwinism was a misapplication of Darwin's biological theory — Darwin himself never argued that some human cultures were evolutionarily superior. Calling it "pseudo-science" or a "misappropriation" shows the sophistication AP graders reward.

A student writes: "European imperialism in Africa brought civilization and infrastructure, which was beneficial for African people in the long run." What are the two main problems with this statement?
Section 13

Sample DBQ — With Full Breakdown

📊 DBQ Basics

DBQ = 25% of AP exam. 7 documents, 60 minutes (15 min reading). Thesis + contextualization + document analysis + outside evidence + sourcing + complexity.

"Evaluate the extent to which economic motivations drove European imperialism in Africa between 1870 and 1900."
1
Decode

Command: "Evaluate the extent to which economic motivations" — HOW MUCH did economics drive imperialism? Need evidence FOR economic causation AND evidence of other factors.
Focus: Africa specifically (not India or China)
Time: 1870–1900 (height of the Scramble for Africa)

✅ Sample Thesis

"While economic motivations — particularly the industrial demand for African raw materials and the need to secure markets for European manufactured goods — were the primary drivers of European imperialism in Africa between 1870 and 1900, a complete explanation requires acknowledging the significant roles of strategic competition between European powers (especially the newly unified Germany), Social Darwinist and missionary ideologies that provided genuine (if self-serving) justifications beyond pure profit, and the specific strategic value of African geographic positions like the Suez Canal and Cape of Good Hope that transcended immediate economic calculation."

📝 Sample Contextualization

"The rapid European colonization of Africa in the 1870s–1900s did not occur in a historical vacuum. It was the direct consequence of the Industrial Revolution that had been transforming European economies since the late 18th century. As Britain, France, Germany, and other powers completed their industrialization, their factories required ever-growing quantities of raw materials — cotton, rubber, metals, petroleum — that Europe's own resources could not supply. At the same time, European nationalism, intensified by the unification of Germany (1871) and Italy (1871), had created a new atmosphere of competitive rivalry in which colonial possession was seen as a measure of national greatness. The technological developments of this same industrial era — the Maxim gun, steamships, the telegraph — gave European armies such overwhelming advantages over African forces that rapid, relatively inexpensive conquest had become feasible for the first time. It was the convergence of these industrial, nationalist, and technological changes that made the Scramble for Africa possible and, in the logic of European great power competition, almost inevitable."

2
Essay Outline
  • Body ¶1 — ECONOMIC MOTIVATIONS (Primary): Raw materials — rubber (Congo), cotton (Egypt, Sudan), gold and diamonds (South Africa). Markets — African colonies provided captive markets for manufactured goods. Investment — European capital invested in African mines, railroads, plantations. Outside evidence: Leopold II's Congo rubber quotas; British gold and diamond mining in South Africa.
  • Body ¶2 — NON-ECONOMIC MOTIVATIONS: Strategic competition — Germany's late entry forced Britain and France to accelerate colonization; nationalism drove the speed. Social Darwinism and missionary ideology — genuine (if self-serving) beliefs. Strategic geography — Suez Canal (Egypt) and Cape of Good Hope (South Africa) had military/naval value beyond immediate economic return.
  • Body ¶3 — COMPLEXITY: Economic and non-economic factors were intertwined — the Berlin Conference shows both economic rules (effective occupation) AND diplomatic competition. Resistance complicates the economic story: the War of the Golden Stool and the Herero and Nama Genocide show that the costs of maintaining colonial control sometimes exceeded the immediate economic benefits.

DBQ Rubric — Maximum 7 Points

1pt
ThesisDirectly addresses "to what extent" (primarily economic, but with significant non-economic contributions), names specific mechanisms, AND acknowledges other factors.
1pt
ContextualizationConnects African colonization to the Industrial Revolution, European nationalism, and technological developments and explains WHY all three were necessary preconditions.
2pts
Evidence: Document ContentDocuments might include: reports on rubber extraction, Berlin Conference proceedings, economic data on resource exports, letters from missionaries, nationalist speeches. Use at least 3 (1pt) and 6 (2nd pt).
1pt
Evidence: Beyond DocumentsOutside evidence: Leopold's Congo rubber quotas and hand-cutting punishments; Battle of Adwa; Yaa Asantewaa's speech; Indian National Congress; De Beers diamond mining; Chinese Exclusion Act as parallel xenophobic response to imperial-driven migration.
1pt
Analysis: SourcingFor 3 documents: explain how historical situation, purpose, POV, or audience affects meaning. Example: "A report by a British colonial administrator justifying African conquest on economic grounds reflects the author's institutional interest in securing funding and political support, which would lead him to emphasize economic benefits while minimizing costs to Africans."
1pt
Analysis: ComplexityShow the intertwining of economic and non-economic factors at the Berlin Conference. Or: the same imperialism that enriched Europe devastated African societies — demonstrating that a single historical process can have radically different consequences depending on your position.
Section 14

Sample LEQ — With Full Breakdown

📊 LEQ Basics

LEQ = 15% of AP exam. 40 minutes. From memory — no documents. Choose from three prompts.

"Compare the methods of imperial control used by Britain in India and France in Algeria between 1830 and 1900."
1
Decode

Command: "Compare" — Both similarities AND differences. Both must be substantial.
Topic: Methods of control (governance, economics, culture, law) — not the history of conquest
Two cases: Britain in India AND France in Algeria — both substantial

✅ Sample Thesis

"Both Britain in India and France in Algeria used military force, economic exploitation, and legal restructuring to maintain imperial control, but they differed fundamentally in their approach to governance and cultural policy: Britain relied primarily on indirect rule through existing local institutions and a pragmatic tolerance for Indian cultural and religious practices, whereas France imposed direct rule and an aggressive assimilation policy aimed at making Algeria culturally French — a difference reflecting deeper divergences in each empire's theory of what colonial control meant and what it was ultimately for."

2
Essay Outline
  • Contextualization: Both came in the wake of Industrial Revolution; both were extractive; both justified by civilizing mission rhetoric; both preceded by military conquest
  • Body ¶1 — SIMILARITIES: Both used military force to establish and maintain control; both built railroad and telegraph infrastructure primarily for imperial economic and military needs; both imposed legal systems that disadvantaged indigenous populations; both extracted local resources for metropolitan benefit
  • Body ¶2 — DIFFERENCE 1 (Governance): Britain: indirect rule in India — maintained hundreds of princely states as nominal rulers under British "paramountcy"; used East India Company until 1858, then Viceroy; leveraged existing power structures. France: direct rule — Algeria formally annexed as French territory in 1848; treated as three French departments; large settler population (pieds-noirs) moved in
  • Body ¶3 — DIFFERENCE 2 (Cultural Policy): Britain in India: English-language education for Indian elite (Macaulay's Minute); but generally tolerated Hinduism, Islam, and Indian cultural practices. France in Algeria: aggressive cultural assimilation; French language mandatory; mission civilisatrice actively attempted to replace Algerian culture with French culture

LEQ Rubric — Maximum 6 Points

1pt
ThesisMakes a specific comparison (both used X, but differed in Y) and establishes a line of reasoning about what the difference reflects.
1pt
ContextualizationFull paragraph connecting both colonial systems to the Industrial Revolution's demand for raw materials and the competitive nationalist context. Must explain WHY relevant.
2pts
EvidenceBritain in India: East India Company, Sepoy Mutiny, British Raj, Macaulay's Minute, Indian National Congress, Indian railroad network. France in Algeria: 1830 conquest, 1848 annexation, pieds-noirs settlers, mission civilisatrice, French legal code applied.
2pts
Analysis & Reasoning: ComparisonEarn both by: (1) making explicit comparisons (not describing each separately) AND (2) explaining WHY the difference matters — what does the contrast between indirect British rule and direct French assimilation reveal about different theories of what empire was for?
Section 15

Unit Summary — The Big Picture

🧪 JUSTIFICATIONS FOR IMPERIALISM
What is Social Darwinism? What scientific theory does it misappropriate, and why is the misapplication racist? What is the White Man's Burden — who wrote it, what does it argue, and what function did it serve?
ANSWER
Social Darwinism: The misapplication of Darwin's biological theory of evolution to human cultures — arguing Western cultures were "evolutionarily superior" and non-Western cultures would "naturally" die out. Provided pseudo-scientific legitimacy for racism. Darwin himself never made these arguments — his theory was about biological organisms, not human societies.

White Man's Burden: Rudyard Kipling's 1899 poem arguing the West had a moral duty to "civilize" less advanced peoples — reframing brutal economic exploitation as charitable sacrifice. Similar to the French mission civilisatrice. Function: provided moral cover for conquest, making it feel like generous service rather than theft.
Which of the following best describes the function of Social Darwinism as an imperial ideology?
Social Darwinism (B) took Darwin's actual biological theory of natural selection and illegitimately applied it to human cultures and races. Darwin's theory explained how organisms evolve; Social Darwinism twisted this into a claim that Western societies were "superior" by nature. Darwin himself never endorsed this application. D is directly wrong — Darwin's theory was biological, not political.
🗺️ BERLIN CONFERENCE & SEPOY MUTINY
What happened at the Berlin Conference (1884–85)? Why does it matter that no Africans were present? What were the long-term consequences of its borders? What was the Sepoy Mutiny (1857) — what triggered it and what did it lead to?
ANSWER
Berlin Conference: 14 European powers divided Africa among themselves with no African representatives present. Drew arbitrary borders across the continent with no regard for existing kingdoms, ethnic groups, or trade networks. Direct cause of most post-independence African political instability — borders split ethnic groups or forced rival groups together.

Sepoy Mutiny (1857): Triggered by rumor that rifle cartridges were greased with beef/pork fat (offensive to Hindus and Muslims). Led to massive uprising across India. Brutally suppressed. Result: abolition of the East India Company → establishment of the British Raj under Crown rule. Queen Victoria became Empress of India (1877).
🔫 MAXIM GUN & AFRICAN RESISTANCE
What is the Maxim gun and why is it central to understanding Unit 6? Describe all three resistance examples — Yaa Asantewaa/War of the Golden Stool, Zulu Kingdom/Battle of Isandlwana, and Xhosa cattle-killing. What made each unique?
ANSWER
Maxim gun: World's first fully automatic machine gun (1884), 500+ rounds per minute. Gave European armies an overwhelming advantage. Freeman-pedia mantra: "Whatever happens, we have got the Maxim gun, and they have not." Explains why indigenous resistance almost always failed militarily.

Yaa Asantewaa / War of the Golden Stool (1900, Ghana): British demanded the Ashanti's sacred golden stool (symbol of Ashanti sovereignty). Yaa Asantewaa (queen mother) rallied resistance: "We will fight the white men." Ashanti were militarily defeated — but the British never got the golden stool. Symbolic victory in defeat.

Zulu / Battle of Isandlwana (1879): 20,000 Zulu warriors killed 1,300 British soldiers — the worst defeat in British Empire history at indigenous hands. The British recovered and ultimately defeated the Zulu Kingdom, but Isandlwana proved indigenous forces could defeat European armies.

Xhosa cattle-killing (1856–57): Facing British pressure, a Xhosa prophet said killing cattle would cause the dead to rise and expel Europeans. Xhosa killed 400,000 cattle. The resulting famine killed ~75% of affected Xhosa. British then took their land with almost no resistance — devastating self-destruction from desperation.
The Battle of Isandlwana (1879) is historically significant primarily because it demonstrated that:
Isandlwana (B) proved that determined, well-organized indigenous forces could defeat European armies even without firearms. The Zulu warriors overwhelmed 1,300 British soldiers — the worst indigenous defeat of the British Empire. However, Britain recovered and eventually defeated the Zulu Kingdom, so A is wrong — Zululand was ultimately colonized. The battle matters as evidence of agency and capacity, not as a story of permanent success.
👑 KING LEOPOLD II & ECONOMIC IMPERIALISM
Who was King Leopold II and what did he do in the Congo? What is economic imperialism (neo-colonialism) — give two specific examples of countries that experienced it without formal colonization.
ANSWER
King Leopold II: Belgian king who personally owned the Congo Free State — not Belgium's colony, but his private property. Forced Congolese villages to meet rubber quotas; soldiers severed the hands of those who failed (presented as proof of enforcement). Estimated 10–20 million Congolese died. Freeman-pedia's MVP of Unit 6: the embodiment of imperial exploitation stripped of all "civilizing" rhetoric.

Economic imperialism: Controlling a country's economy and political decisions through investment, debt, and trade dependency — without formal colonization. Examples:
• Britain in Argentina — controlled Argentine economy through railroad/banking investments without governing it.
• United Fruit Company in Central America — US fruit companies dominated Honduras, Guatemala, Costa Rica ("banana republics"), bribing governments and controlling workers.
🚢 MIGRATION, PUSH/PULL & XENOPHOBIA
Define push factors and pull factors. Give specific examples for two different migration patterns (Irish and Chinese migration). Name two discriminatory immigration laws and explain who they targeted and why they were created.
ANSWER
Push factors: Conditions driving people to leave — famine, war, economic collapse, persecution.
Pull factors: Conditions attracting people to a destination — economic opportunity, political freedom, land.

Irish: PUSH = Great Famine (1845–52, potato blight killed 1 million, displaced millions more). PULL = industrial jobs in Boston, New York, and other US cities.

Chinese: PUSH = Opium Wars humiliation, Taiping Rebellion devastation. PULL = California Gold Rush (1848), transcontinental railroad construction (1860s).

Discriminatory laws:
• Chinese Exclusion Act (US, 1882) — only US law ever banning immigration from a specific nationality. Not repealed until 1943.
• White Australia Policy (1901–1973) — banned non-European immigration to maintain Australia's racial composition. Explicitly xenophobic.
🦁 BATTLE OF ADWA — SUCCESSFUL RESISTANCE
Who won the Battle of Adwa (1896) and who lost? Why is it historically significant for African history and for understanding the limits of imperialism?
ANSWER
Winner: Ethiopia, under Emperor Menelik II. Loser: Italy's colonial army.

Significance: The first decisive African military victory over a European colonial army in the imperial era. Ethiopia was the only African nation to maintain full independence throughout the entire colonial period (along with Liberia). The AP exam's go-to example of successful African resistance. Adwa demonstrates that African agency was real — the outcome of imperialism was not inevitable or biologically determined.
Which African nation successfully defeated a European colonial army at the Battle of Adwa (1896), remaining independent throughout the entire colonial era?
Ethiopia (C) under Emperor Menelik II defeated Italy's colonial army at the Battle of Adwa in 1896 — the first decisive African military victory over a European colonial army. Ethiopia and Liberia were the only two African nations to remain fully independent throughout the colonial era. The Zulu Kingdom (A) and Ashanti Empire (B) both ultimately fell to British colonization, despite their impressive military victories at Isandlwana and their resistance over the Golden Stool.
📝 One-Paragraph Summary of Unit 6

Unit 6 (1750–1900 CE) is the story of what happens when the Industrial Revolution meets a world divided between the industrialized and the non-industrialized. The West uses its military and economic advantages to conquer or dominate most of Africa, Asia, and Latin America in the frantic competitive expansion called New Imperialism. The motivations are real (M&Ms: raw materials and markets for factories) and the justifications are constructed (Social Darwinism, the White Man's Burden, the missionary impulse). The methods range from outright military conquest (Berlin Conference carving up Africa; the British Raj in India) to economic control without formal conquest (Britain in Argentina, US fruit companies in Central America). Colonized peoples fought back — Yaa Asantewaa and the Ashanti, the Zulu at Isandlwana, Ethiopia at Adwa, the Boxers in China — almost always facing the verdict: "Whatever happens, we have got the Maxim gun, and they have not." The most grotesque example of imperial exploitation was King Leopold II's personal Congo — a rubber plantation running on terror, mutilation, and murder that killed 10–20 million people. Alongside the imperial story runs the migration story: tens of millions of people moving across the globe, pushed by famine and violence, pulled by economic opportunity, creating ethnic enclaves and provoking xenophobic legislation. By 1900, the world has been restructured by industrial power — and the accumulated tensions of imperial rivalry are loading the gun that will fire in 1914.

ResourceHow to Use It
Freeman-pedia (YouTube)Watch the Unit 6 video series. The MVP video on King Leopold II is essential. His maps on global economic development and resistance movements help visualize what otherwise feels like an overwhelming list of examples.
King Leopold's Ghost (Adam Hochschild)Highly readable, deeply researched account of Leopold's Congo. Even a chapter or two deepens your understanding of imperial exploitation beyond any AP review. Also tells the story of the activists (including Mark Twain) who exposed it.
Rudyard Kipling's "The White Man's Burden" (full text)Short enough to read completely. AP exam frequently uses excerpts. Knowing the poem's arguments deeply helps you both use it as evidence and critically analyze it.
AP Classroom (Unit 6 Progress Check)Progress checks often use maps of colonial empires, economic data on resource extraction, and primary source excerpts. Practice reading colonial maps alongside documents.
AP Central — Released FRQs on imperialismSearch for released DBQs and LEQs on imperialism, Africa, and India. Writing timed practice responses is the highest-value exam preparation activity.
Check-In

Do You Think You Can Move On?

Six units down. Three to go. Do a quick honest check before jumping to Unit 7.

On a scale from 1–10, how confident do you feel about Unit 6? What feels solid? What still feels fuzzy?
WHAT "READY" MEANS FOR UNIT 6
You are ready to move on to Unit 7 if you can:

✅ Explain Social Darwinism — what scientific theory it misused and why the misuse was racist
✅ Explain the White Man's Burden — who wrote it, what it argued, and what function it served
✅ Describe what happened at the Berlin Conference and explain the consequences of excluding Africans
✅ Name the Sepoy Mutiny and explain what it led to (the British Raj)
✅ Recite the Maxim gun mantra and explain why it matters for understanding indigenous resistance
✅ Describe all three resistance examples: Yaa Asantewaa/Golden Stool, Zulu/Isandlwana, Xhosa cattle-killing
✅ Explain economic imperialism with two specific examples
✅ Explain what King Leopold II did in the Congo
✅ Name push and pull factors for at least two migration patterns
✅ Explain the Chinese Exclusion Act and White Australia Policy as examples of xenophobia
✅ Name the Battle of Adwa and explain its significance

Fuzzy on any of those? Spend 10 focused minutes there. You don't need a 10/10. Move forward.

Unit 7 is Global Conflict — the imperial rivalries of Unit 6 are about to explode into the first truly global war. Everything you just learned explains why it happens.

Six units down. Unit 7 brings Global Conflict — the imperial rivalries are about to explode.

Unit 07 · c. 1900–Present · 12–15% of AP Exam

Global Conflict

0%
💥 Global Conflict — It's okay not to remember every detail. Work through it, stay curious, and be proud of what you're building.
Sections
00Introduction
01Four Shifters
02World War I
03Interwar Period
04World War II
05Mass Atrocities
06Glossary
07Causes & Effects
08Timeline
09Thinking Skills
10Command Terms
11Pitfalls
12Sample DBQ
13Sample LEQ
14Unit Summary
★Ready to Move On?
AP World History Modern · Unit 7

Global Conflict  |  c. 1900–Present

⚖️ Exam Weight + College Board Warning

12–15% of the AP Exam. Freeman-pedia's honest note: "The College Board does NOT want you going deep on the world wars. They want the big ideas, the key causes, and the specific technologies." This guide gives you exactly what the AP tests — no more, no less.

🏆 Freeman-pedia's MVP of Unit 7: Gavrilo Princip

A tuberculosis-ridden teenage Bosnian-Serbian nationalist who shot Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914. Freeman-pedia's VORP argument: remove Princip from history and everything disappears with him.

No WWI = no communism, no fascism, no Holocaust, no nuclear weapons, no Cold War, no decolonization. Possibly no Great Depression. Possibly no Civil Rights Movement. The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand is the Big Bang of Unit 7 and arguably of the entire contemporary period.

The Big Topics of Unit 7

  • THE FOUR SHIFTERS: Major power transitions that set up WWI — Mexico, China, Russia, and the Ottoman Empire
  • WORLD WAR I: MAIN causes, total war, new military technology, outcomes and the Versailles Treaty
  • THE INTERWAR PERIOD: The Great Depression, three economic responses, 13 unresolved tensions
  • WORLD WAR II: Causes, total war again, firebombing and atomic bombs, outcomes
  • MASS ATROCITIES: The Holocaust (required) + four illustrative examples (Armenian Genocide, Holodomor, Cambodian Genocide, Rwandan Genocide)
Freeman-pedia argues that Gavrilo Princip is the MVP of Unit 7 through a "VORP" argument. What is that argument?
Section 01

The Four Shifters — Major Power Transitions After 1900

📌 These Are Required — Not Optional Examples

Freeman-pedia is explicit: these four are not illustrative examples — the College Board requires you to know them. Think of them as tectonic shifts that reshape the political landscape before WWI even starts. Each represents a country the course has been tracking for years finally breaking through to a new form.

Shifter 1: Mexico — From 31-Year Dictatorship to Constitutional Republic

Porfirio Díaz had ruled Mexico as a dictator since 1876 — 31 years. He promised free elections in 1910, then arrested his rival Francisco Madero and declared himself winner again. Freeman-pedia: "Díaz is basically Mexican Putin." Madero escaped to the US, returned, overthrew Díaz, then was assassinated. A decade of civil war followed: Emiliano Zapata (peasant revolutionary, land reform) and Pancho Villa (guerrilla in the north).

Key outcome: The Mexican Constitution of 1917 — one of the most progressive constitutions in the world at that time. Guaranteed social rights, secular education, land redistribution, labor rights, democracy, and restricted the power of the Catholic Church.

SHIFT: From 31-year dictatorship to a constitutional, secular republic.

Shifter 2: China — The End of 2,000 Years of Dynastic Rule

We've been watching China's Qing Dynasty weaken throughout Units 5 and 6: Opium Wars, Taiping Rebellion, Sino-Japanese War, Boxer Rebellion. By 1911, the Qing had lost the Mandate of Heaven. Sun Yat-sen led the Chinese United League: expel the Manchus, establish a republic, distribute land. The League overthrew the Qing Dynasty. The last emperor, Puyi, abdicated — he was six years old.

The 2,000-year dynastic cycle in China ended in 1912. The Republic of China was established. The republic never fully stabilized — falling into warlordism and factionalism, then into civil war between Nationalists (GMD) and Communists (CCP), ending with the Communists winning in 1949 (Unit 8).

SHIFT: 2,000-year dynastic cycle ends. China becomes a republic — the first in its entire history.

Shifter 3: Russia — From Tsarist Empire to Communist Soviet Union

Tsar Nicholas II was spectacularly bad at ruling: he organized anti-Semitic pogroms, lost the Russo-Japanese War (1905), ordered soldiers to fire on peaceful protesters (Bloody Sunday, 1905), employed Rasputin as a spiritual advisor, and sent Russia into WWI with catastrophic results — more casualties than Britain, France, and the US combined.

The February Revolution (1917): Protests broke out in Petrograd on International Women's Day. The Tsar tried to return to the capital and was stopped by his own military. He abdicated. A Provisional Government took over but continued the disastrous war.

The October Revolution: Lenin led the Bolsheviks (radical communists) in storming the Winter Palace. Russia became the USSR — the first communist state in history. Lenin immediately took Russia out of WWI (Treaty of Brest-Litovsk, 1918), surrendering enormous territory to Germany.

SHIFT: 300-year Romanov dynasty ends. Russia becomes the world's first communist state.

Shifter 4: The Ottoman Empire — From "Sick Man of Europe" to Turkey

The Ottoman Empire had been declining since Unit 5. They made the fatal mistake of joining WWI on Germany's side (the losing side). The Treaty of Versailles carved up what remained — former Arab territories distributed as League of Nations mandates to Britain (Palestine, Iraq, Jordan) and France (Syria, Lebanon). These mandates were essentially new colonies in diplomatic clothing.

Mustafa Kemal Atatürk led a successful military campaign to expel foreign forces from Anatolia, then established the Republic of Turkey in 1923. His modernization: secular constitution, Western calendar, Latin alphabet replacing Arabic script, women's right to vote, secular education, Western dress codes. Freeman-pedia: "Think of this as the true continuation of the Tanzimat reforms."

SHIFT: 600-year Ottoman Empire ends. Turkey emerges as a secular, modernizing republic.

Name all four shifters. For each, write what it shifted FROM and what it shifted TO. Then explain: why does Freeman-pedia say these are required, not optional examples?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Mexico: From 31-year Díaz dictatorship → secular constitutional republic (1917 Constitution). Key figures: Madero, Zapata, Pancho Villa.

China: From 2,000-year dynastic cycle (Qing Dynasty, last emperor Puyi age 6) → Republic of China (1912). Key figure: Sun Yat-sen. Never stabilized; civil war between GMD and CCP (Mao wins 1949, Unit 8).

Russia: From 300-year Romanov dynasty (incompetent Tsar Nicholas II) → world's first communist state (USSR). Key figure: Lenin. Bolshevik takeover October 1917. Treaty of Brest-Litovsk takes Russia out of WWI.

Ottoman Empire / Turkey: From 600-year Ottoman Empire (joined wrong side in WWI) → secular Republic of Turkey (1923). Key figure: Atatürk. Arab territories became League of Nations mandates (Britain and France). Turkey got Latin alphabet, women's suffrage, secular law.

Why required, not optional: These four shifts explain why the world that enters WWI looks different from the world that left Unit 6. They are not background — they are the conditions that make WWI's consequences so explosive. A Russia without the Romanovs = a communist superpower. A China without the Qing = a century of instability. These shifts set up everything that follows in Unit 7.
Section 02

World War I (1914–1918) — The War That Broke the World

💬 Bismarck's Prediction (1898)

"Europe today is a powder keg, and the leaders are like men smoking in an arsenal. A single spark will set off an explosion that will consume us all. I cannot tell you when that explosion will occur, but I can tell you where: some damn foolish thing in the Balkans will set it off."

He was right. Sixteen years later, that damn foolish thing happened: Gavrilo Princip shot Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo, Bosnia. But that assassination was only the spark. The powder keg was built by four causes.

The MAIN Causes of WWI

M
Militarism

Industrial militaries had grown to unprecedented scale. Compare Napoleon's muskets (1815) to WWI's artillery: the German Paris Gun could fire from 81 miles away. Armies had grown from hundreds of thousands to millions. Military spending had doubled and tripled across Europe in the decades before 1914. Every major power believed their military was strong enough to win a quick war. They were all catastrophically wrong.

A
Alliances

The old country-vs-country model was replaced by alliance networks. Fight one country, fight their whole crew. Triple Entente: Russia + France + Britain (assembled 1894–1907). Triple Alliance: Germany + Austria-Hungary + Italy (1882). Any conflict would instantly drag multiple great powers in — turning a local incident into a world war.

I
Imperialism

The competing imperial powers had been fighting over African and Asian territories for 50 years (Unit 6). Britain and Germany competed for colonies. France and Germany nearly went to war over Morocco — twice. When these rivals faced each other on European borders, that stored resentment made peaceful resolution nearly impossible.

N
Nationalism

Two kinds: (1) Jingoistic nationalism — each great power believed its military was invincible and any war would be over by Christmas. This made leaders reckless. (2) Ethnic nationalism — nations without states wanted independence. Serbia wanted a united Slavic state carved out of the Austro-Hungarian Empire. Gavrilo Princip was a Bosnian Serb nationalist acting on this impulse when he shot the Archduke.

MAIN: Explain all four causes of WWI. For each, give a specific example and explain how it contributed to the war starting. Then explain: why was the assassination the TRIGGER but NOT the cause?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Militarism: European nations had built massive, industrialized armies over decades. The German Paris Gun fired 81 miles. Armies numbered in the millions. Military leaders on all sides believed their forces could win a quick, decisive war — none of them were right. The massive military buildup created both the capability for catastrophic war and the confidence to start one.

Alliances: Triple Entente (Russia-France-Britain) + Triple Alliance (Germany-Austria-Hungary-Italy). When Austria-Hungary declared war on Serbia, Russia mobilized for its ally Serbia. Germany declared war on Russia. France was pulled in as Russia's ally. Britain was pulled in when Germany violated Belgian neutrality (the Schlieffen Plan). One assassination became a world war in five weeks.

Imperialism: Britain and Germany had competed fiercely for African colonies (Unit 6). France and Germany had nearly fought over Morocco (1905, 1911). When these same rivals faced each other on European borders with decades of accumulated resentment, peaceful compromise was nearly impossible.

Nationalism: Serbian ethnic nationalism wanted to unite South Slavs (in the Austro-Hungarian Empire) into a unified state. Gavrilo Princip was acting on this impulse. Jingoistic nationalism made every major power believe it was superior and would win quickly — contributing to reckless decision-making.

Why assassination = trigger, not cause: Without MAIN — without decades of military buildup, alliance networks ready to drag everyone in, imperial resentments, and nationalist tensions — the assassination would have been a diplomatic incident, not a world war. Leaders could have defused it. The reason they couldn't is because the powder keg (MAIN) was already primed. The assassination was just the match.

WWI as Total War

WWI was the first total war — meaning the entire nation's resources were mobilized for the war effort, not just the army:

  • Civilians buying war bonds to fund the military
  • Women entering factories to produce weapons and supplies — challenging the Cult of Domesticity from Unit 5
  • Rationing food, metal, and other goods so more could go to the front
  • Propaganda campaigns mobilizing public opinion and encouraging enlistment
  • Colonial subjects mobilized: Indian, African, Australian, Canadian, New Zealand, and other colonial troops fought for Britain. Britain couldn't have fought the war without its empire's manpower.

WWI Military Technology — The Seven Killers

TechnologyWhat It WasScale / Impact
ArtilleryLarge guns firing explosive shells packed with shrapnel60% of ALL WWI casualties were caused by artillery. On the first day of the Battle of Verdun, the Germans fired 1 million shells — and the battle lasted 140 more days.
Poison GasChemical weapons: chlorine gas, phosgene, and mustard gas, fired in artillery shellsFirst war with chemical weapons. Germany (80% of world's chemical production) led development. Caused horrific injuries. Eventually reduced in effectiveness by improved gas masks.
Machine GunRapid-fire automatic weapons set up in concrete pillboxes along trench linesCreated "No Man's Land." Anyone crossing open ground faced thousands of rounds per minute. Made frontal assaults catastrophically lethal — explaining the trench stalemate.
AirplanesInitially for reconnaissance; then armed with machine guns (interrupter gear allowed firing through propellers)First war with aerial combat. By war's end, bombing from the air had begun — prefiguring WWII's air campaigns.
