TanksArmored vehicles on caterpillar tracks, designed to cross No Man's LandNotoriously unreliable in WWI but proved the concept. First used by British at Battle of the Somme (1916). Would revolutionize WWII warfare as the primary offensive weapon.
DreadnoughtsMassive steel battleships with enormous long-range guns — floating artillery platformsDominated naval warfare. The massive naval arms race between Britain and Germany building competing fleets was itself a cause of WWI tensions (the M in MAIN).
Submarines (U-boats)German underwater vessels; unrestricted submarine warfare against all shipping supplying BritainGermany's 360 U-boats sank more ships than the entire rest of the German navy. Their unrestricted attacks on US ships directly brought the US into the war in 1917.

WWI Outcomes — Four Collapsed Empires and the Seeds of WWII

Four Empires Ended
  • German Empire → Weimar Republic
  • Austro-Hungarian Empire → Austria, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia
  • Russian Empire → USSR (after the Bolshevik Revolution)
  • Ottoman Empire → Turkey + League of Nations mandates
Treaty of Versailles Key Provisions
  • War Guilt Clause (Article 231): Germany forced to accept full blame for WWI
  • Reparations: Equivalent to ~$400 billion today
  • Military restrictions: Army limited to 100,000 troops; no air force; no submarines
  • Territorial losses: Lost 13% of territory and 10% of population
🚨 The WWII Thesis

"The Treaty of Versailles made WWII inevitable by humiliating Germany without truly defeating German nationalism." The treaty created economic conditions for hyperinflation and Great Depression devastation, and fueled a nationalist rage that Hitler exploited. Humiliated without destroyed = the most dangerous possible outcome.

The War Guilt Clause (Article 231 of the Treaty of Versailles) is historically significant primarily because it:
Section 03

The Interwar Period (1919–1939) — The 20 Years Between Disasters

The Great Depression (1929)

The US came out of WWI economically strong. America's first celebrity economist declared in August 1929 that "stock prices have reached a prominently high plateau." The stock market crashed the following month (Black Tuesday, October 29, 1929). The US banking system collapsed. Because Europe was deeply in debt to American banks, the collapse spread immediately worldwide.

What made it worse for Germany specifically: already dealing with WWI reparations and hyperinflation from the early 1920s (when Germany printed money to pay reparations, destroying the currency's value), the Depression added 6 million unemployed by 1932. Economic desperation made extreme political ideologies appealing: if capitalism failed you, maybe fascism or communism was the answer.

Three Economic Responses to the Great Depression

FeatureNew Deal (US)Fascist Corporatism (Italy/Germany)Five-Year Plans (USSR)
LeaderFDR (elected 1932)Mussolini (Italy, 1922); Hitler (Germany, 1933)Stalin (consolidated power late 1920s)
Economic TheoryKeynesian: government spends to stimulate demand and create jobsFascist corporatism: dictator controls economy for state goals; 75% of Italian economy state-controlledCentral planning: state sets 5-year production targets; private property abolished
Political SystemConstitutional democracy: FDR had elections, Congress, courtsTotalitarian dictatorship: all opposition suppressed; no elections; all life controlledCommunist oligarchy: party controls state; Stalin's purges eliminated all opposition
ResultMixed recovery. Real recovery came with WWII spending. US democracy survived and thrived.Short-term economic boost through military spending. Led directly to WWII. Both fascist states destroyed.Industrial growth from devastated baseline. 12 million dead from collectivization. USSR survived WWII at catastrophic human cost.
Still Around?YES — the US is still hereNO — both fascist states were destroyed in WWIINO — USSR collapsed in 1991
What is fascism? What does the name come from? What were its core beliefs? Why was it appealing to desperate people during the Great Depression?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Fascism gets its name from the fasces — an old Roman symbol of a bundle of rods (sticks). One stick can be broken easily; a bundle together is unbreakable. The fascist message: individual weakness becomes collective strength when everyone thinks alike, acts alike, and subordinates themselves to the national collective.

Core beliefs: (1) Authoritarian leadership — one supreme leader (Duce/Führer) whose will IS the state's will; (2) extreme nationalism — the nation above everything; national pride as a religion; (3) anti-communism — fierce opposition to Marxism and class warfare, which appealed to business owners and middle class who feared communist revolution; (4) suppression of all opposition — no free press, no opposition parties, no independent courts; (5) in Nazi Germany specifically: racial hierarchy with antisemitism at its core.

Why appealing during the Depression: When capitalism had failed and democracy seemed unable to fix it, fascism offered a simple narrative: "It's not capitalism's fault — it's the fault of traitors (Jews, communists, foreigners). Follow our strong leader and we'll restore national greatness." Desperate people in desperate times found this narrative compelling. The Nazis won 2.6% of the German vote in 1928 (good economy); 37% in 1932 (Depression peak). The Great Depression didn't create fascism — it gave fascism its mass audience.

The 13 Unresolved Tensions of the Interwar Period

Freeman-pedia maps out 13 questions left unanswered by WWI that made WWII almost inevitable. These aren't just historical curiosities — they're the fault lines along which WWII and the Cold War would fracture.

#TensionAnswer
6What happens to the weak Weimar Republic in Germany?Hyperinflation + Great Depression + Versailles humiliation = Hitler. The Weimar Republic's structural weaknesses let a minority party take control legally.
7Is Mussolini's fascism in Italy catching on?Catastrophically yes — he's the blueprint for Hitler. Fascism spreads to Germany, Spain, and beyond.
8Is the world actually safer under the League of Nations?No. Its false sense of security made things worse. Japan invades Manchuria (1931); League condemns it. Italy invades Ethiopia (1935); League condemns it. Both aggressors simply leave the League. Nobody stops them.
9What about Palestine? (Britain's Balfour Declaration promised a Jewish homeland)Still unresolved. The creation of Israel (1948) as a response to the Holocaust creates a conflict that continues today.
11India fought for Britain in WWI. Will Britain grant home rule?They delay. Gandhi's nonviolent resistance begins. Independence comes in 1947 (Unit 8).
12China is in civil war between Nationalists (GMD) and Communists (CCP). Who wins?Mao's CCP wins in 1949 (Unit 8). The GMD retreats to Taiwan. Still relevant today.
13How do you stop Japan's Greater East Asian Co-Prosperity Sphere from expanding?You eventually don't — until Japan attacks Pearl Harbor and the US enters WWII.
Freeman-pedia's Conclusion

"The interwar period is madness. What's the answer to most of these questions? World War II."

Why did the League of Nations fail to prevent WWII, and what was it replaced by?
Section 04

World War II (1939–1945) — The Biggest Deal

📌 Freeman-pedia's Framing

"Until the oceans rise and we're all forced to live in some post-apocalyptic aquatic society, World War II will remain the biggest deal." 70–85 million dead. The most destructive conflict in human history. The College Board focuses on specific elements — here is exactly what they require.

Causes of WWII

Cause 1: The Unsustainable Peace

The Treaty of Versailles humiliated Germany without destroying German nationalism. The War Guilt Clause + reparations + territorial losses + military restrictions created economic desperation and national humiliation. When the Depression hit Germany especially hard (already suffering from hyperinflation), Hitler offered national restoration to a desperate public.

The League of Nations made things worse by giving people a false sense of security while proving unable to stop determined aggressors.

Cause 2: Aggressive Totalitarian Militarism

Fascist Germany, Italy, Spain, plus militarist Japan pursued aggressive territorial expansion: Germany into the Rhineland (1936), Austria (1938), Czechoslovakia (1938–39), Poland (1939); Italy into Ethiopia (1935); Japan into Manchuria (1931) and China (1937).

European democracies tried appeasement — most famously at Munich (1938) where Britain and France allowed Hitler to take the Sudetenland in exchange for his promise of no further expansion. He broke it immediately.

What is appeasement? What happened at Munich in 1938? Why is it now used as a cautionary historical example?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Appeasement was the diplomatic strategy of making concessions to an aggressive power to avoid war. The logic: better to give them what they want than risk another devastating war. In 1938, at the Munich Conference, British Prime Minister Neville Chamberlain and French Prime Minister Daladier allowed Hitler to annex the Sudetenland (a German-speaking part of Czechoslovakia) in exchange for Hitler's promise of "no further territorial demands." Chamberlain returned home declaring "peace for our time."

Hitler invaded the rest of Czechoslovakia six months later, then invaded Poland in September 1939, starting WWII in Europe.

Why it's now a cautionary example: Munich demonstrated that yielding to aggression signals weakness and encourages further aggression. By trying to avoid war at Munich, the democracies actually made WWII more likely — they demonstrated that Hitler could take what he wanted without serious opposition. "Munich" and "appeasement" have since become synonyms for dangerous naivety when facing authoritarian aggressors. Any politician today who proposes negotiations with an aggressor will be accused of "appeasement" — the lesson of 1938 still shapes foreign policy thinking.

WWII's New Military Technology: Two Critical Additions

🔥 Don't Let the Atomic Bombs Overshadow Firebombing

Firebombing: Bombs dropped from high altitude created literal firestorms so intense they generated their own wind systems. The firebombing of Tokyo (March 1945) killed approximately 80,000–100,000 people in a single night — more than either atomic bomb individually. Dresden, Hamburg, and dozens of German cities were firebombed into rubble. More people died in WWII firebombing campaigns than in both atomic bombings combined. Both Germany and Japan lobbied after the war for firebombing to be classified as a war crime.

☢️ Atomic Bombs — The Weapon That Changed Everything

Brand new to July 1945. The Manhattan Project (US, British, Canadian scientists, with major contributions from European refugee scientists fleeing Hitler) developed and tested the first nuclear weapon (Trinity test, New Mexico, July 16, 1945). Less than a month later:

  • August 6: "Little Boy" dropped on Hiroshima — approximately 70,000–140,000 dead
  • August 9: "Fat Man" dropped on Nagasaki — approximately 40,000–80,000 dead
  • August 15: Japan surrendered. This ended a war Japan had started in 1937 — eight years before.

Freeman-pedia's point: the atomic age began in July 1945 and has not ended. The fear of nuclear weapons shapes every geopolitical conflict from 1945 to today.

Why does Freeman-pedia say firebombing gets overlooked? Compare firebombing and atomic bombs in terms of: what they were, who was targeted, and the death tolls involved.
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Firebombing gets overlooked because the atomic bombs were so revolutionary and dramatic that they captured historical attention. But here's the comparison:

Firebombing: Incendiary bombs dropped on urban areas to create massive firestorms. Used by Allied forces against German cities (Dresden, Hamburg, Cologne) and Japanese cities throughout 1944–45. The firebombing of Tokyo (March 9–10, 1945) created a firestorm that killed approximately 80,000–100,000 people in a single night. Firebombing campaigns against Japanese cities killed more people overall than both atomic bombs combined. Targeted civilians entirely — the goal was to destroy urban industrial capacity and break civilian morale.

Atomic bombs: Two nuclear weapons dropped on Hiroshima (August 6) and Nagasaki (August 9), 1945. Hiroshima: ~70,000–140,000 dead (immediate + radiation deaths). Nagasaki: ~40,000–80,000 dead. Total: ~110,000–220,000 dead from both bombs combined.

Why firebombing matters for the AP: (1) The death tolls are comparable and in aggregate larger; (2) Both represent the WWII reality that total war now meant the deliberate, industrial-scale killing of civilians; (3) The AP College Board specifically calls out firebombing as a new weapon in WWII alongside atomic weapons. Do not write about WWII new weapons and only mention atomic bombs.

WWII Outcomes — The Modern World Is Born

OutcomeWhat It Meant
70–85 million deadThe deadliest conflict in human history. About 3% of the world's entire population. Majority were civilians — a fundamental shift from earlier wars.
United Nations (1945)Replaced the League of Nations with actual enforcement mechanisms (Security Council with veto powers for US, USSR, UK, France, China). Also produced the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) — a direct response to the Holocaust.
Cold War beginsThe US and USSR emerged as the two global superpowers with incompatible ideologies. The wartime alliance immediately fractured. Cold War competition would define global politics for 45 years (Unit 8).
Decolonization acceleratedColonial peoples who fought for European powers now demanded independence. India gained independence in 1947; dozens more followed (Unit 8). WWII proved European powers were not invincible.
Nuclear age beginsThe atomic bomb made global civilization's destruction possible in hours. This reality defines international relations from 1945 to today — and limits how "hot" Cold War conflicts could get.
Section 05

Mass Atrocities After 1900 — Genocide

📌 The Pattern Across All Genocides
  • A politically vulnerable group is scapegoated for society's problems
  • The state (or a state-sponsored group) uses modern organizational and military technology to systematically kill them
  • International response is typically inadequate or nonexistent until after the worst has occurred
  • The perpetrators often deny it afterward — sometimes for decades or permanently

The Holocaust — The Required Genocide

What It Was

The systematic, state-sponsored persecution and murder of six million Jews and millions of others (Roma, disabled people, LGBTQ+ individuals, political opponents, Soviet POWs) by the Nazi German government between 1933 and 1945. Approximately two-thirds of all European Jews were killed.

1
Legal Discrimination (1933–38)

Nuremberg Laws stripped Jews of citizenship; Jews barred from professions, schools, and public life. The legal framework of discrimination set the stage for physical violence.

2
Persecution and Violence (1938–41)

Kristallnacht ("Night of Broken Glass," 1938) — organized pogrom destroying Jewish property; forced emigration; ghettos established in occupied Poland.

3
The "Final Solution" (1941–45)

Nazi leadership at the Wannsee Conference (1942) formalized the plan for systematic extermination. Death camps (Auschwitz, Treblinka, Sobibor) used industrial methods: gas chambers, crematoria. This was murder as industrial process.

Aftermath
  • Nuremberg Trials (1945–46): Nazi leaders tried for war crimes and crimes against humanity. Established the legal principle that "following orders" was not a defense for genocide. Precedent for international criminal law.
  • UN Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948): A direct response to the Holocaust, establishing universal human rights that no state could legally violate.
  • State of Israel (1948): The Holocaust made the case for a Jewish homeland irrefutable to much of the world, leading to UN recognition — in the territory of the British mandate in Palestine (Interwar Tension #9).
What were the three stages of the Holocaust? What was the Nuremberg Trials' most important legal precedent? What did the Universal Declaration of Human Rights establish — and why was it a direct response to the Holocaust?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Three stages:
1. Legal discrimination (1933–38): Nuremberg Laws (1935) stripped Jews of German citizenship, barred them from professions and schools, and created a legal framework of second-class status.
2. Persecution and violence (1938–41): Kristallnacht (1938) — organized pogrom destroying Jewish businesses and synagogues, killing ~100 Jews, arresting 30,000 more; ghettos established in occupied Poland; forced emigration.
3. "Final Solution" (1941–45): Wannsee Conference (1942) formalized the plan for systematic extermination. Death camps at Auschwitz, Treblinka, Sobibor, and others used gas chambers and crematoria to murder people at industrial scale. Six million Jews killed by 1945.

Nuremberg Trials' most important precedent: "Following orders" is not a legal defense for genocide or crimes against humanity. Individual soldiers, officers, and government officials can be held legally responsible for atrocities even if they were ordered to commit them by superiors. This established the foundation for modern international criminal law (the International Criminal Court uses this precedent today).

Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948): Established that there are fundamental human rights — to life, liberty, security, freedom from torture, right to education, etc. — that belong to every human being by virtue of being human, and that no state can legally strip from its citizens. It was a direct response to the Holocaust because the Holocaust showed what happened when a state treated its own citizens as having no rights at all. The UDHR was the international community's attempt to create a legal floor below which no government could go.

The Four Illustrative Genocides

Armenian Genocide
1915–1923 · Ottoman Empire · ~1 million dead

The Ottomans targeted Armenian Christians, suspecting them of supporting Christian Russia during WWI. Death marches through the Syrian desert; forced conversion to Islam. Still officially denied by Turkey today. Hitler explicitly cited it as a precedent: "Who, after all, speaks today of the annihilation of the Armenians?" The first major genocide of the 20th century.

Holodomor (Ukraine)
1932–33 · Soviet Union · ~4 million dead

Stalin collectivized Ukrainian farms, shipped crops to industrial workers elsewhere, restricted movement, and refused outside aid — deliberately weaponizing famine against the Ukrainian people. "Holodomor" means "hunger plague." Stalin's quote: "If only one man dies of hunger, that is a tragedy. If millions die, that's only statistics." Freeman-pedia says he hates Stalin the most — and this is a major reason why.

Cambodian Genocide
1975–79 · Cambodia · ~2 million dead

Under Pol Pot and the communist Khmer Rouge, 25% of Cambodia's entire population was killed — the highest per-capita death toll of any genocide. City residents were forcibly relocated to rural labor camps. Intellectuals, professionals, and minorities were murdered. Pol Pot modeled his approach partly on Mao's Cultural Revolution. Ended by Vietnam's invasion in 1979.

Rwandan Genocide
1994 · Rwanda · ~800,000 dead in 100 days

Roots in Belgian colonial-era exploitation of Tutsi vs. Hutu divisions (Unit 6 legacy). In 100 days, Hutu militias killed approximately 800,000 Tutsis and moderate Hutus. The international community (including the US and UN) failed to intervene despite clear warning signs. A devastating example of genocide as organized mass violence within a community — and of international failure to act.

Name all five genocides in this section. For each, give: when, where, approximate death toll, and the one key detail that distinguishes it from the others.
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Holocaust: 1933–1945, Nazi Germany. 6 million Jews + millions of others. Key distinguishing detail: the industrialization of murder — gas chambers and crematoria used as a systematic assembly-line process. Led to the Nuremberg Trials and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

Armenian Genocide: 1915–1923, Ottoman Empire. ~1 million dead. Key distinguishing detail: the FIRST major genocide of the 20th century; Hitler cited it as a precedent; still officially denied by Turkey today.

Holodomor: 1932–33, Soviet Union (Ukraine). ~4 million dead. Key distinguishing detail: weaponized famine — Stalin used deliberate starvation as the instrument of genocide; he collectivized farms and blocked international food aid.

Cambodian Genocide: 1975–79, Cambodia. ~2 million dead (~25% of the entire population). Key distinguishing detail: the HIGHEST PER-CAPITA death toll of any genocide. A quarter of the country's people were killed. Led by Pol Pot and the communist Khmer Rouge; ended by Vietnam's invasion.

Rwandan Genocide: 1994, Rwanda. ~800,000 dead in 100 days. Key distinguishing detail: the SPEED — 800,000 people killed in approximately 100 days while the international community watched and did nothing. Roots in Belgian colonial exploitation of Hutu/Tutsi divisions.
Section 06

Glossary of Key Terms

Test yourself on each term before revealing. Mark how well you know it.

Section 07

Causes, Consequences & Significance

The Chain of Causation Across Both Wars

WWI Causes (MAIN)
  • Decades of military buildup (Militarism)
  • Alliance networks that turned a local crisis into a world war (Alliances)
  • Imperial competition building resentment (Imperialism)
  • Ethnic and jingoistic nationalism (Nationalism)
  • Assassination of Franz Ferdinand = the spark
WWII Causes
  • Versailles humiliation without destroying German nationalism
  • Great Depression → economic desperation → extremism
  • League of Nations' failure to enforce collective security
  • Appeasement enabling further aggression
  • Totalitarian fascism (Hitler, Mussolini) and Japanese militarism pursuing deliberate expansion
🔗 The Causal Connection Between the Wars

WWI's consequences (Versailles + communist Russia + four collapsed empires + 13 unresolved tensions) directly created WWII's preconditions. The two wars are not separate events — they are chapters of the same story, with the interwar period as the terrible bridge between them. Freeman-pedia's framing: WWI is the Big Bang; WWII is what happened when the debris didn't settle properly.

CCOT — What Changed and What Continued

CONTINUITIES
  • Nationalism continues to drive political conflicts throughout the period
  • Empires continue to exist (though shakier); decolonization only really begins after WWII
  • Antisemitism and ethnic/racial prejudice continue from earlier periods into the genocides
  • Economic inequality continues; great power competition continues
CHANGES
  • Scale of destruction — WWI and WWII kill tens of millions in years; previous wars killed thousands over decades
  • Technology makes civilians as vulnerable as soldiers — artillery, gas, firebombing, atomic bombs
  • Totalitarian governments achieve complete control of information and daily life — new in 20th century
  • Women permanently enter the public workforce during total war
  • US emerges as superpower; European great powers permanently weakened
Section 08

Timeline of Unit 7 (c. 1900–1945)

1905

Russian Revolution of 1905; Russo-Japanese War Lost

First sign of Romanov weakness. Russia's defeat by Japan shocked the world — the first time an Asian nation defeated a European power in modern times (Meiji Restoration payoff from Unit 5).

1910–17

Mexican Revolution

Overthrow of Díaz dictatorship; decade of civil war; 1917 Constitution establishes Mexico as a secular constitutional republic. One of the most progressive constitutions in the world at that time.

1911–12

Chinese Revolution; Qing Dynasty Falls

Sun Yat-sen leads the overthrow of the Qing Dynasty. The 2,000-year dynastic cycle ends. Republic of China established. Six-year-old Emperor Puyi abdicates.

1914

WWI Begins — Assassination of Franz Ferdinand

Gavrilo Princip shoots the Archduke in Sarajevo. Alliance system activates. The Big Bang of the contemporary period. Five weeks after the assassination, Europe is at war.

1915

Armenian Genocide Begins

Ottoman Turkey begins systematic killing and deportation of Armenian Christians. Up to 1 million killed. First major genocide of the 20th century. Still denied by Turkey.

1917

Russian Revolution (February and October)

February: Tsar Nicholas II abdicates. October: Lenin's Bolsheviks seize power. Russia becomes the world's first communist state.

1918

WWI Armistice (November 11)

"The war to end all wars" ends. 17 million dead. Four empires dissolved. Seeds of WWII planted.

1919

Treaty of Versailles; League of Nations Founded

Germany forced to accept blame, pay reparations, cede territory, limit military. Woodrow Wilson's League of Nations established — without US membership.

1922

Mussolini Takes Power in Italy; USSR Formally Established

Mussolini becomes Italy's prime minister through political intimidation (the "March on Rome"). The USSR is formally established — first communist state in world history.

1929

US Stock Market Crash; Great Depression Begins

Black Tuesday, October 29. Global economic collapse. The Depression kills the Weimar Republic and gives Hitler his path to power.

1932–33

Holodomor (Ukraine Famine)

Stalin's collectivization policies create a deliberate famine killing ~4 million Ukrainians. "If millions die, that's only statistics."

1933

Hitler Becomes German Chancellor

Appointed chancellor in January. Nazis won 37% of the vote in 1932 at the Depression's worst. Democracy ends in Germany. The Nazi state begins.

1937

Japan Invades China (Second Sino-Japanese War)

Freeman-pedia argues this is the true start of WWII in Asia. The Nanjing Massacre: Japanese forces kill an estimated 200,000 Chinese civilians.

1938

Munich Agreement (Appeasement); Kristallnacht

Britain and France give Hitler the Sudetenland. Kristallnacht: organized Nazi pogrom against Jews. The Holocaust begins its escalation.

1939

WWII Begins in Europe (Germany Invades Poland)

September 1: Germany invades Poland. Britain and France declare war. WWII's European theater begins.

1941

Germany Invades USSR; Japan Attacks Pearl Harbor

Operation Barbarossa (June): the largest military invasion in history. Pearl Harbor (December 7): US enters WWII. The war becomes truly global.

1945

WWII Ends; UN Founded; Cold War Begins; Nuclear Age Begins

May: Germany surrenders. August 6 & 9: atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. August 15: Japan surrenders. October: United Nations founded. Nuclear age and Cold War begin simultaneously.

Fill in: The atomic bomb was dropped on Hiroshima on August _____, 1945, killing approximately _____ people, and on Nagasaki three days later.
Section 09

Historical Thinking Skills

1. Causation — Connecting the Wars

WEAK: "The assassination of the Archduke caused WWI, and then Hitler caused WWII."

STRONG: "WWI was caused by decades of militarism, alliance networks, imperial competition, and nationalist movements that had been building since the 1880s (MAIN). The assassination was the spark, not the cause. The manner in which WWI ended — Versailles' humiliation of Germany without truly defeating German nationalism, combined with the global economic collapse of the Great Depression (1929) and the League of Nations' inability to enforce collective security — created the conditions that made WWII not just possible but likely. Hitler's rise was the proximate cause of WWII; the systemic causes were WWI's unresolved consequences."

Practice: Write a two-sentence thesis for this prompt: "Evaluate the extent to which the Treaty of Versailles caused World War II."
SAMPLE THESIS
"While the Treaty of Versailles significantly contributed to World War II by creating the economic devastation and nationalist humiliation that made Hitler's message politically viable, it was not the sole cause of WWII; the Great Depression's global economic collapse, the League of Nations' failure to enforce collective security, European democracies' appeasement policies, and Japanese militarism in Asia combined with the Versailles legacy to produce a war that was the product of multiple reinforcing failures rather than a single flawed treaty."
Section 10

Common Command Terms for Unit 7

TermWhat It MeansUnit 7 Example
IdentifyName one specific thing. Minimal explanation."Identify one cause of World War I." → Militarism — the massive expansion of European armies and weapons technology in the decades before 1914 created military machines capable of catastrophic destruction and gave military leaders confidence (misplaced) that a quick, decisive war was possible.
ExplainMake clear HOW or WHY. Show the connection."Explain one effect of the Treaty of Versailles on Germany." → The War Guilt Clause combined with enormous reparations devastated the German economy (contributing to hyperinflation), while the forced military restrictions humiliated German nationalist pride — creating precisely the resentment and economic desperation that made Hitler's promises of national restoration politically appealing during the Great Depression.
CompareSimilarities AND differences. Both must be addressed."Compare the causes of WWI and WWII." → Both: unresolved nationalist tensions, failure of international institutions, arms buildup. But WWI was caused by alliance miscalculation in a crisis; WWII was caused by deliberate, ideologically-driven aggression by totalitarian states that exploited the failures of WWI's peace settlement.
AnalyzeExamine in detail — components, causes, effects."Analyze how the Great Depression contributed to the rise of totalitarianism." → Economic desperation made people receptive to scapegoating and extreme solutions; governments that seemed unable to fix the economy lost legitimacy; totalitarian movements offered simple explanations and decisive leadership. Germany was especially vulnerable due to WWI humiliation + Weimar structural weaknesses.
Evaluate the extent to whichJudge HOW MUCH. Needs both supporting AND counter-evidence."Evaluate the extent to which the Treaty of Versailles caused WWII." → Yes: created economic ruin and humiliation that made Nazi promises appealing; War Guilt Clause fueled resentment. But: Great Depression, appeasement, League failure, Japanese militarism in Asia all contributed independently. Versailles was necessary but not sufficient.
DescribeGive key features or characteristics."Describe the military technology of World War I." → Artillery (60% of casualties), poison gas (first chemical weapons), machine guns (created No Man's Land), airplanes (reconnaissance and combat), tanks (first used at Somme 1916), submarines (U-boats brought US into war). Show the range.
Section 11

Common Pitfalls & How to Avoid Them

❌

Pitfall 1: The Archduke's Assassination CAUSED WWI

The Problem: "Gavrilo Princip shot Archduke Franz Ferdinand and that caused WWI." The assassination was the TRIGGER, not the cause. Without the MAIN framework — decades of militarism, alliance networks, imperial competition, and nationalist tensions — the assassination would have been a diplomatic incident, not a world war.

✅ The Fix: Always use the MAIN acronym when explaining WWI causation. The assassination is evidence of the underlying tensions (specifically Serbian nationalism), not the cause itself.

❌

Pitfall 2: Confusing WWI and WWII Events

The Problem: Saying the Holocaust happened in WWI, or that trenches and poison gas were primarily WWII, or that the atomic bomb was used in WWI. These are different wars with different technologies, different causes, and different outcomes.

✅ The Fix: WWI = trenches, poison gas, alliance system, stalemate. WWII = Blitzkrieg, Holocaust, atomic bombs, totalitarian ideology driving the conflict. Keep them separate.

❌

Pitfall 3: WWI and WWII Were Only European Wars

The Problem: Students focus entirely on the Western Front and forget both were genuinely global. In WWI: Indian, Australian, New Zealand, Canadian, African colonial troops all fought; battles occurred in Africa, the Middle East, and the Pacific. In WWII: the Pacific theater was enormous; India's 2.5 million volunteers were the largest volunteer army in history.

✅ The Fix: For any WWI or WWII essay, include at least one reference to a non-European dimension: colonial troops, Pacific theater, effects on colonial independence movements.

❌

Pitfall 4: Oversimplifying WWII Blame to Hitler Alone

The Problem: Hitler was central to the European theater but not alone. Italy under Mussolini, Japan under military leadership, and Stalin's USSR (which aligned with Germany 1939–41) were all responsible. WWII in Asia arguably started in 1937 with Japan's invasion of China — entirely unrelated to Versailles.

✅ The Fix: Always address both theaters: Europe (German and Italian fascism, appeasement, Versailles legacy) AND Asia (Japanese militarism, the Second Sino-Japanese War 1937, Pearl Harbor). Structural causes were shared across both theaters.

❌

Pitfall 5: Treating the Genocides as Just Background Information

The Problem: Students mention the Holocaust in one sentence and move on. But the College Board lists mass atrocities as a distinct section equal in importance to the wars themselves.

✅ The Fix: Know at least two genocides in enough detail to use them as evidence in essays. For the Holocaust: know the three stages, the Nuremberg Trials, and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights as a direct response.

A student writes: "WWI started when Gavrilo Princip assassinated Archduke Franz Ferdinand, which caused all the European nations to go to war." What is the main historical error in this statement?
Section 12

Sample DBQ — With Full Breakdown

📊 DBQ Basics

DBQ = 25% of AP exam. 7 documents, 60 minutes (15 min reading). Thesis + contextualization + document analysis + outside evidence + sourcing + complexity.

"Evaluate the extent to which the Treaty of Versailles (1919) caused World War II."
1
Decode

Command: "Evaluate the extent to which... caused" — HOW MUCH did Versailles cause WWII? Not a yes/no but a judgment: important but not sufficient?
The prompt requires: Evidence supporting Versailles as a cause AND evidence of other causes to show nuance.

✅ Sample Thesis

"While the Treaty of Versailles significantly contributed to World War II by creating the economic devastation and nationalist humiliation that made Hitler's message politically viable, it was not the sole cause of WWII; the Great Depression's global economic collapse, the League of Nations' failure to enforce collective security, European democracies' appeasement policies, and Japanese militarism in Asia combined with the Versailles legacy to produce a war that was the product of multiple reinforcing failures rather than a single flawed treaty."

📝 Sample Contextualization

"The Paris Peace Conference of 1919 was held in the immediate aftermath of the most destructive war in human history up to that point. The representatives faced contradictory pressures: Wilson wanted a 'peace without victory' based on his Fourteen Points (national self-determination, open diplomacy, the League of Nations), while Clemenceau demanded that Germany be punished severely enough to prevent future aggression and compensate France for its enormous losses. The result was a compromise that satisfied no one: it was harsh enough to humiliate Germany without being comprehensive enough to actually dismantle German military and industrial capacity. This context — a peace negotiated between exhausted, traumatized nations with incompatible goals, while excluding the defeated powers — shaped every one of the treaty's provisions."

2
Essay Outline
  • Body ¶1 — HOW VERSAILLES CONTRIBUTED: War Guilt Clause (Article 231) forced Germany to accept full blame — fueled nationalist resentment. Reparations devastated the already weakened German economy; hyperinflation of the 1920s. Territorial losses and military restrictions reinforced the nationalist narrative of unfair treatment.
  • Body ¶2 — OTHER CAUSES (limits Versailles as THE cause): The Great Depression: Nazis won only 2.6% of vote in 1928 (good economy); 37% in 1932 (Depression peak). Versailles alone didn't cause Hitler — the Depression activated it. League of Nations' failure to stop Japan (1931) or Italy (1935) emboldened Hitler to ignore it. Japanese militarism: WWII in Asia started in 1937 entirely unrelated to the European Versailles settlement.
  • Body ¶3 — COMPLEXITY: Versailles and other causes were interconnected: The reparations Versailles demanded required Germany to borrow from American banks — when those banks collapsed in 1929, Germany's economy collapsed fastest. Versailles created the League of Nations; the League's weakness then allowed the very aggression Versailles was supposed to prevent.

DBQ Rubric — Maximum 7 Points

1pt
ThesisDirectly addresses "to what extent" (significant but not sole cause), acknowledges multiple causes, and establishes a line of reasoning (Versailles + Depression + appeasement = WWII).
1pt
ContextualizationExplains the contradictory goals at the Paris Peace Conference and WHY they produced an unsatisfying compromise that neither punished Germany sufficiently to prevent future aggression nor treated it fairly enough to prevent resentment.
2pts
Evidence: Document ContentDocuments might include: excerpts from the Versailles Treaty (War Guilt Clause text), German reactions to the clause, economic data showing German hyperinflation and unemployment, Hitler's speeches exploiting Versailles humiliation, Munich Agreement accounts. Use at least 3 (1pt) and at least 6 (2nd pt).
1pt
Evidence: Beyond DocumentsOutside evidence: 1928 vs. 1932 Nazi election results (2.6% vs. 37%); League of Nations failures at Manchuria (1931) and Ethiopia (1935); Munich Agreement (1938); Japan's invasion of China (1937) as a non-European cause of WWII.
1pt
Analysis: SourcingFor 3 documents: explain how historical situation, purpose, point of view, or audience affects meaning. Example: "Hitler's speech invoking the 'stab-in-the-back' myth reflects his purpose of mobilizing nationalist resentment for political gain; as a propagandist in a democracy under economic stress, he would emphasize Versailles humiliation regardless of its actual causal weight."
1pt
Analysis: ComplexityShow the connection between causes: Versailles created the League of Nations, which then failed to stop WWII; Versailles reparations made Germany dependent on American loans that collapsed in 1929; or connect to Asian theater — WWII began there in 1937 without any relation to Versailles.
Section 13

Sample LEQ — With Full Breakdown

📊 LEQ Basics

LEQ = 15% of AP exam. 40 minutes. From memory — no documents. Choose from three prompts.

"Compare the causes of World War I and World War II."
1
Decode

Command: "Compare" — BOTH similarities AND differences required
Topic: CAUSES (not the events of the wars, not the outcomes — stay focused on causation)
Both wars must be substantial in your response

✅ Sample Thesis

"Both World War I and World War II were driven by unresolved nationalist tensions, military competition, and the failure of international institutions to manage great-power rivalry; however, they differed in the nature of their causation — WWI resulted from the accidental escalation of a regional crisis through automatic alliance obligations, while WWII resulted from deliberate, ideologically-driven aggression by totalitarian states that exploited the specific failures of WWI's peace settlement, the Great Depression's economic desperation, and democratic powers' unwillingness to risk another war."

2
Essay Outline
  • Contextualization: Both wars emerged in the context of industrialized European great-power competition — WWI from the imperial rivalry of the late 19th century (Unit 6); WWII from the unresolved consequences of WWI. The same forces (nationalism, military competition, weak international institutions) were present in both, but operated differently.
  • Body ¶1 — SIMILARITIES: Both caused by nationalist tensions (ethnic and jingoistic); both involved military competition building for years before the war; both were exacerbated by weak or nonexistent international institutions that couldn't manage great-power conflict (pre-WWI: no effective forum; pre-WWII: League of Nations failed to stop determined aggressors)
  • Body ¶2 — KEY DIFFERENCE: WWI = accidental escalation through alliance obligations (no one planned a world war; everyone planned a short local war); WWII = deliberate aggression (Hitler, Mussolini, Japanese military leadership planned territorial expansion and provoked the wars they wanted)
  • Body ¶3 — COMPLEXITY: WWI's causes directly created WWII's causes — the same framework of nationalist tensions and military competition, BUT now weaponized by totalitarian states with coherent ideological programs for using war to achieve political goals. The continuity between the wars is as important as their differences.

LEQ Rubric — Maximum 6 Points

1pt
ThesisIdentifies a similarity (both driven by nationalist tensions and military competition), identifies the key difference (accidental vs. deliberate causation), and establishes a line of reasoning.
1pt
ContextualizationA full paragraph connecting both wars to the broader context of industrialized great-power competition from the 19th century (Unit 6) and WWI's unresolved consequences as background for WWII.
2pts
EvidenceWWI: MAIN causes (specific: alliance network mechanism, assassination as spark not cause, military buildup statistics). WWII: Versailles terms, Hitler's rise, Great Depression vote data (2.6% vs. 37%), Japanese invasion of China 1937.
2pts
Analysis & Reasoning: ComparisonEarn both by: (1) making explicit comparisons (not describing each war separately) AND (2) explaining WHY the differences matter — accidental vs. deliberate causation means the same preconditions produce wars through entirely different mechanisms, with profound implications for how to prevent future wars.
Section 14

Unit Summary — The Big Picture

💥 MAIN CAUSES OF WWI
What does MAIN stand for? For each of the four causes, give a specific historical example. What was the trigger of WWI — and why is the trigger NOT the same as the cause?
ANSWER
M = Militarism: Massive pre-war military buildup (Paris Gun could fire 81 miles). All powers believed they'd win quickly — this overconfidence made war more likely.
A = Alliances: Triple Entente (Russia-France-Britain) + Triple Alliance (Germany-Austria-Hungary) turned a local Balkan conflict into a world war in 5 weeks.
I = Imperialism: 50 years of colonial competition (Unit 6) built deep resentment between the same powers now fighting in Europe.
N = Nationalism: Serbian ethnic nationalism (Princip wanted a unified Slavic state) + jingoistic great-power pride made leaders reckless.

Trigger vs. Cause: Gavrilo Princip's assassination of Franz Ferdinand was the trigger — the spark. The powder keg (MAIN) was already built. Remove the assassination and the war likely still happens eventually; remove MAIN and there's no war.
Which of the following best describes the role of the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in causing WWI?
The assassination (B) was the immediate trigger — the spark that ignited the powder keg of MAIN. Without decades of militarism, entangling alliances, imperial rivalries, and nationalist tensions, the assassination would have remained a regional crisis. The AP exam tests this distinction: the assassination is evidence of nationalism (N in MAIN), not a separate cause.
🌍 FOUR SHIFTERS
What are the four shifters at the beginning of Unit 7? For each, name what shifted, who led it, and the approximate date.
ANSWER
Mexico: Díaz's 31-year dictatorship → secular constitutional republic (1917 Constitution). Mexican Revolution.

China: 2,000-year Qing dynasty → Republic of China (1912). Sun Yat-sen. Last emperor Puyi was 6 years old.

Russia: 300-year Romanov dynasty → world's first communist state (USSR, 1922). Lenin and the Bolsheviks, October Revolution 1917.

Ottoman Empire: 600-year empire → secular Republic of Turkey (1923). Atatürk (Mustafa Kemal) modernized and Westernized Turkey.
⚔️ TOTAL WAR & VERSAILLES
What is total war? Give three specific examples of how WWI was fought as a total war. Then name three provisions of the Treaty of Versailles and explain how each planted the seeds of WWII.
ANSWER
Total war: Entire nation's resources — economic, social, human — mobilized for the war effort. Examples: civilians buying war bonds · women entering factories (challenging the Cult of Domesticity) · rationing food/materials · propaganda campaigns · colonial troops from India, Africa, Australia, and Canada fighting for Britain.

Versailles provisions → WWII seeds:
1. War Guilt Clause (Article 231): Germany forced to accept full blame → fueled nationalist resentment Hitler exploited.
2. Reparations (~$400B today): devastated German economy, caused hyperinflation → economic desperation made Hitler appealing.
3. Military restrictions (100K army, no air force, no submarines): humiliated German military pride → created desire for revenge.
🦅 FASCISM & THE GREAT DEPRESSION
What is fascism? Where does the word come from? Name its four core beliefs. Then name the three economic responses to the Great Depression — which of the three governments is still around today?
ANSWER
Fascism: Name comes from the Roman fasces — a bundle of rods symbolizing strength through unity of the collective. Core beliefs: (1) Authoritarian leadership — one supreme leader; (2) Extreme nationalism; (3) Anti-communism; (4) Suppression of all opposition. Nazi Germany added racial hierarchy and antisemitism.

Three Depression responses:
1. New Deal (US, FDR) — Keynesian deficit spending, government employment. Still around.
2. Fascist Corporatism (Germany/Italy, Hitler/Mussolini) — state-directed economy, rearmament. Both destroyed in WWII.
3. Five-Year Plans (USSR, Stalin) — command economy, forced collectivization. Collapsed 1991.

Only the US democratic approach survives.
Which of the following best explains why fascism appealed to Germans in the early 1930s?
Fascism's appeal (C) came from specific historical conditions: the Great Depression caused massive unemployment and economic desperation, while the Treaty of Versailles had humiliated Germany with the War Guilt Clause, enormous reparations, and military restrictions. Hitler offered both economic recovery (rearmament creating jobs) and nationalist revenge. A is a form of the racist "Germans were always this way" argument — the AP rejects this kind of deterministic thinking.
🕍 THE HOLOCAUST & ACCOUNTABILITY
What were the three stages of the Holocaust? What was the Nuremberg Trials' most important legal precedent? What did the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) establish — and why was it created when it was?
ANSWER
Holocaust — Three Stages:
1. Legal discrimination (1933–38): Nuremberg Laws stripped Jews of citizenship, civil rights, ability to hold jobs.
2. Persecution and violence (1938–41): Kristallnacht (1938), forced ghettos, mass deportations.
3. "Final Solution" (1941–45): Wannsee Conference formalized genocide; death camps (Auschwitz, Treblinka, etc.); 6 million Jews killed, plus millions of Roma, disabled people, LGBTQ+ people, political dissidents.

Nuremberg Trials: Nazi leaders tried for war crimes after WWII. Most important precedent: "following orders" is not a legal defense for genocide. Individuals are responsible for their actions even under authoritarian systems.

UDHR (1948): Established that fundamental human rights belong to all people by virtue of being human — no state can legally strip them. Created directly in response to the Holocaust as a global commitment to "never again."
💣 FIREBOMBING, ATOMIC BOMBS & APPEASEMENT
What is firebombing — and why does it get overlooked compared to the atomic bombs despite killing more people? What is appeasement — what happened at Munich (1938) and why does "Munich" now serve as a historical warning?
ANSWER
Firebombing: Incendiary bombing of civilian cities — designed to start uncontrollable fires. The firebombing of Tokyo (March 1945) killed 80,000–100,000 people in one night — more than either atomic bomb individually. Total firebombing deaths exceeded the combined atomic bomb totals. Gets overlooked because atomic weapons were so technologically revolutionary and dramatic. Both represent total war's reality: industrialized mass civilian killing.

Appeasement: The policy of yielding to an aggressor's demands to avoid conflict. At the Munich Agreement (1938), Britain and France gave Hitler the Sudetenland (Czech territory) in exchange for his promise of no more territorial demands. He invaded the rest of Czechoslovakia 6 months later. Lesson: yielding to authoritarian aggressors encourages more aggression. "Munich" is now the historical shorthand for dangerously naive capitulation to a bad actor.
The League of Nations failed to prevent WWII primarily because:
The League (B) had two fatal flaws: the US Senate rejected Wilson's League (so the world's largest economy never joined), and it had no enforcement army — it could pass resolutions condemning aggression but had no mechanism to stop it. It failed to stop Japan in Manchuria (1931) or Italy in Ethiopia (1935), signaling to Hitler that determined aggressors could act freely. The United Nations (1945) was designed to fix these flaws with the Security Council.
📝 One-Paragraph Summary of Unit 7

Unit 7 (1900–c.1945) is the story of how the modern world was forged in catastrophe. Four major power shifts set the stage: Mexico becomes a constitutional republic; China's 2,000-year dynastic cycle ends with the fall of the Qing; Russia's Romanov dynasty falls and the world's first communist state (the USSR) emerges; and the Ottoman Empire collapses into Turkey under Atatürk. Then Gavrilo Princip assassinates Archduke Franz Ferdinand in 1914, and the powder keg built by decades of militarism, alliance networks, imperial competition, and nationalism (MAIN) explodes into WWI. The "war to end all wars" kills 17 million, dissolves four empires, creates the Soviet Union, and produces the Treaty of Versailles — which humiliates Germany without destroying German nationalism. The interwar period features the Great Depression (fueling fascism as an alternative to capitalism), three competing economic systems (New Deal, fascist corporatism, Five-Year Plans), and 13 unresolved global tensions. All roads lead to WWII: fascist totalitarians and Japanese militarists deliberately start the biggest conflict in history (70–85 million dead). Firebombing and the atomic bomb industrialize mass civilian killing. The Holocaust — six million Jews systematically murdered — represents the darkest expression of 20th-century totalitarian ideology. The war ends with the US and USSR as global superpowers, the UN and Universal Declaration of Human Rights as responses to the Holocaust, and the nuclear age and Cold War beginning simultaneously.

Check-In

Do You Think You Can Move On?

Seven units down. Two to go. Do a quick honest check before jumping to Unit 8.

On a scale from 1–10, how confident do you feel about Unit 7? What feels solid? What still feels fuzzy?
WHAT "READY" MEANS FOR UNIT 7
You are ready to move on to Unit 8 if you can:

✅ Name all four MAIN causes of WWI with a specific example of each
✅ Explain why the assassination was the trigger but NOT the cause
✅ Name all four shifters and what each shifted from and to
✅ Explain three specific provisions of the Treaty of Versailles and how each contributed to WWII
✅ Explain fascism — its symbol, four core beliefs, and why it was appealing during the Depression
✅ Name the three economic responses to the Depression and which government is still around
✅ Describe the three stages of the Holocaust
✅ Explain the Nuremberg Trials' most important legal precedent
✅ Explain what the Universal Declaration of Human Rights established and why
✅ Explain firebombing and atomic bombs — and why firebombing gets overlooked
✅ Write a two-sentence thesis for a comparison of WWI and WWII causes

Fuzzy on any? Spend 10 focused minutes there. You don't need a 10/10.

Unit 8 is the Cold War. The US and USSR square off, decolonization reshapes the map, and the world holds its breath hoping no one pushes the button.

Seven units down. Unit 8 brings the Cold War — the US and USSR square off and the world holds its breath.

Unit 08 · c. 1900–Present · 12–15% of AP Exam

Cold War & Decolonization

0%
🌍 Cold War & Decolonization — It's okay not to remember every detail. Work through it, stay curious, and be proud of what you're building.
Sections
00Introduction
01The Cold War
02Communism Spreads
03Decolonization
04New States
05Global Resistance
06Glossary
07Timeline
08Thinking Skills
09Pitfalls
10Sample DBQ
11Sample LEQ
12Unit Summary
★Ready to Move On?
AP World History Modern · Unit 8

Cold War & Decolonization  |  c. 1900–Present

⚖️ Exam Weight

12–15% of the AP Exam. Freeman-pedia's framework: "Think of it as an extension and deconstruction of the previous two units. Colonies from Unit 6 are getting their independence. Hot wars from Unit 7 are getting smaller and colder."

🏆 Freeman-pedia's MVP of Unit 8: Nelson Mandela

The anti-apartheid leader who spent 27 years in a South African prison before becoming the country's first democratically elected president. Mandela represents the convergence of Unit 8's two major themes: the decolonization of Africa and non-violent resistance to oppressive power structures. He belongs with Gandhi, MLK, and Kwame Nkrumah in the pantheon of non-violence achieving political change.

The Two Big Topics of Unit 8

The Cold War (1947–1991)

The US (capitalist democracy) vs. the USSR (authoritarian communist state), competing for global influence without directly fighting each other — through nuclear deterrence, proxy wars, alliances, propaganda, and economic pressure. Plus the Non-Aligned Movement: countries that refused to pick a side.

Decolonization (primarily 1945–1975)

The rapid dismantling of the European colonial empires built in Unit 6. Some colonies negotiated independence peacefully; others fought for it. What they did with that independence — new borders, economic development, migration — is equally important.

Why does Freeman-pedia call Unit 8 "an extension and deconstruction of the previous two units"?
Section 01

The Cold War — 50 Years of Eyeball-to-Eyeball

📌 Why "Cold"?

The US and USSR never directly fought each other. Nuclear deterrence made direct conflict suicidal — Mutually Assured Destruction (MAD) meant any nuclear first strike would guarantee the attacker's own destruction. So the competition stayed "cold" — fought through proxy wars, economic pressure, intelligence operations, propaganda, and an arms race that terrified the world for 45 years.

The Three Teams — Don't Think A vs. B

A
US + Western Bloc
NATO (1949). Capitalist democracies. Containment of communism. Free markets + democratic elections.
B
USSR + Eastern Bloc
Warsaw Pact (1955). Authoritarian communist states. Planned economy. One-party rule. Spreading revolution globally.
C
Non-Aligned Movement
29 nations at Bandung (1955). India, Egypt, Ghana, Yugoslavia. Refused to pick a side. Had their own agendas.
🌏 Bandung Conference (1955) — The Birth of Team C

29 African and Asian nations met in Bandung, Indonesia, and declared their independence from both Cold War blocs. Key members: India (Nehru), Egypt (Nasser), Ghana (Nkrumah), Yugoslavia (Tito), Indonesia (Sukarno). They had their own agendas — economic development, sovereignty, anti-colonialism — that didn't fit neatly into the US-USSR binary. The Non-Aligned Movement is the AP exam's proof that the Cold War was not simply a two-sided conflict.

What is the Non-Aligned Movement? Why does Freeman-pedia insist on "three teams" instead of two? Give at least three specific countries and their leaders from Team C.
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Team A = US-led Western bloc (NATO, 1949): capitalist democracies (US, UK, France, West Germany, Canada, etc.) committed to containing communism and defending free markets and democratic elections.

Team B = Soviet-led Eastern bloc (Warsaw Pact, 1955): authoritarian communist states (USSR, East Germany, Poland, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, etc.) committed to spreading communist revolution globally.

Team C = Non-Aligned Movement (Bandung Conference, Indonesia, 1955): 29 African and Asian nations that refused to formally align with either superpower. Key leaders: Nehru (India), Nasser (Egypt), Nkrumah (Ghana), Tito (Yugoslavia), Sukarno (Indonesia). They had their own agendas: economic development, national sovereignty, anti-colonialism, and the right to self-determination without being pawns in a Cold War game.

Why three teams matter: Writing the Cold War as simply A vs. B ignores roughly 30 nations with their own foreign policy goals and erases the significance of the Bandung Conference and the entire decolonization process. Many of Unit 8's most important events (Suez Crisis, Vietnam, Angola) cannot be understood without Team C's perspective.

Nuclear Proliferation & MAD

☢️ The Scale Is Hard to Comprehend

Freeman-pedia's comparison: before WWI, Britain had 29 dreadnoughts and Germany had 17 — that was a buildup. By the peak of the Cold War, the US had roughly 31,000 nuclear warheads and the USSR had roughly 45,000. Freeman-pedia: "Each one is a Thanos-level threat, and each side is building thousands."

The Logic of MAD: Build so many nuclear weapons that the other side knows they can never destroy them all in a first strike. Therefore they can never attack without guaranteeing their own destruction. Insane as a doctrine — effective as a practical deterrent.

🚀 The Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962) — The Closest We Got

US spy planes discovered Soviet nuclear missiles being installed in Cuba — 90 miles from Florida. President Kennedy imposed a naval blockade. For 13 days, the world teetered on the edge of nuclear war.

Resolution: The USSR removed its missiles from Cuba; the US quietly removed its missiles from Turkey and promised not to invade Cuba.

Freeman-pedia's key insight (from McNamara): even with 26,000 US nukes vs. 3,000 Soviet nukes, the US was deterred from attacking Cuba because 3,000 nuclear warheads is still 3,000 nuclear warheads. Deterrence works both ways.

What is nuclear deterrence / MAD? What happened during the Cuban Missile Crisis? Why didn't the US attack Cuba even though it had vastly more nuclear weapons than the USSR?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
MAD (Mutually Assured Destruction): Each superpower built so many nuclear weapons that even if the other launched a devastating first strike, enough weapons would survive to retaliate and destroy the attacker. Since both sides would be completely destroyed, neither could rationally start a nuclear war. The key is that you don't need equality — just enough to destroy your attacker. Even with 26,000 US nukes, the US was deterred by 3,000 Soviet nukes because 3,000 nuclear warheads would still devastate the US.

Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962): US spy planes discovered Soviet nuclear missiles being installed in Cuba — 90 miles from Florida, within range of most of the continental US. Kennedy imposed a naval blockade rather than an airstrike (to avoid immediate escalation). For 13 days, the world was on the edge of nuclear war. Resolution: USSR agreed to remove its missiles from Cuba; the US promised not to invade Cuba and quietly removed its missiles from Turkey. Kennedy looked like he won; Khrushchev got something too.

Why didn't the US attack? Because 3,000 Soviet warheads in Cuba could have destroyed most major American cities — far more than enough to deter the US. The numerical imbalance didn't matter; what mattered was that either side could destroy the other completely. That's MAD.

Proxy Wars — The Substitute Fights

A proxy war is a conflict where the two superpowers support opposing sides rather than fighting each other directly. From 1945 to 1991, there were at least 60 proxy wars worldwide. The superpowers funded, armed, trained, and advised their proxies — and often ended up supporting some genuinely terrible people simply because they were "our" terrible people.

ConflictUS SideSoviet SideOutcome & Significance
Korean War (1950–53)South Korea + UN forcesNorth Korea + ChinaStalemate at the 38th parallel. Korea remains divided to this day. First major Cold War proxy war. UN collectively responded to communist aggression.
Vietnam War (US involvement 1965–73)South Vietnam + US forcesNorth Vietnam (Ho Chi Minh) + Viet Cong; backed by USSR and ChinaCommunist North Vietnam unified the country in 1975. The US's costliest Cold War defeat. 58,000 Americans and ~2 million Vietnamese died. Reshaped US domestic politics.
Angola (1975–2002)UNITA rebels; later South AfricaMPLA government; Cuban troops; Soviet weaponsOne of the longest proxy wars. 500,000 dead. Landmines still litter the countryside. Independence immediately turned into a proxy civil war.
Afghanistan (1979–89)Afghan mujahideen (via Pakistan); CIA weaponsSoviet Union directly invading and occupyingThe USSR's "Vietnam." 15,000 Soviet soldiers killed; millions of Afghans dead. Helped bring down the Soviet Union financially. The mujahideen later formed the Taliban and al-Qaeda.
Nicaragua (1979–90)The Contras — US-backed right-wing rebelsSandinista government; Soviet armsFreeman-pedia's featured example. Contras committed widespread human rights abuses. Reagan secretly funded them after Congress banned it (Iran-Contra scandal). Sandinistas won 67% in 1984 elections but lost power in 1990.
What is a proxy war? Why does the pattern of proxy wars embarrass both superpowers? Use the Afghanistan and Nicaragua examples to illustrate.
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
A proxy war is a conflict where the US and USSR support opposing sides rather than fighting each other directly — each side uses a local "proxy" (substitute) to advance its interests without the risk of direct superpower confrontation. From 1945 to 1991, approximately 60 proxy wars occurred worldwide.

Why proxy wars embarrass both superpowers:

Afghanistan: The US armed and funded the Afghan mujahideen (through Pakistan's ISI) to fight the Soviet invasion — one of the CIA's largest covert operations. The mujahideen successfully drove the Soviets out (the USSR's "Vietnam"). But many of those same mujahideen fighters, equipped with US weapons and training, went on to form the Taliban and al-Qaeda. The September 11, 2001 attacks were carried out by al-Qaeda, a direct descendant of the CIA-funded mujahideen. The US helped create the organization that attacked it.

Nicaragua: The Sandinistas won a revolution against the US-backed Somoza dictatorship (1979) and held genuinely free elections — winning 67% of the vote in 1984. The US responded by secretly funding the Contras, right-wing rebels who committed widespread atrocities, in violation of US Congressional law (the Boland Amendment). This became the Iran-Contra scandal when it was revealed that the US had secretly sold weapons to Iran and used the proceeds to fund the Contras. The US was supporting human rights abusers to fight a democratically elected government — because it was socialist.

End of the Cold War (1989–1991)

Why the Cold War Ended
  • Economic exhaustion: The Soviet economy could not sustain massive military spending, propping up satellite states, AND the Afghan war simultaneously. The Five-Year Plans never produced a consumer economy competitive with the West.
  • Gorbachev's reforms: Glasnost (openness — allowing criticism) and perestroika (restructuring — economic liberalization) meant to save the Soviet system instead unleashed forces that destroyed it.
  • Revolutions in Eastern Europe (1989): Poland, Hungary, East Germany, Czechoslovakia, Romania, and Bulgaria all overthrew communist governments. The Berlin Wall fell November 9, 1989 — the symbolic end of the Cold War.
  • Soviet Union dissolved (December 25, 1991): The USSR broke apart into 15 independent republics. The Cold War was over.
Why did the Cold War end? Which of the following best explains the primary cause of the Soviet Union's collapse?
Section 02

The Spread of Communism — China and Redistribution

China: The Communist Victory

China's civil war between the Nationalist Party (GMD/Kuomintang under Chiang Kai-shek) and the Communist Party (CCP under Mao Zedong) had been going since the late 1920s. WWII forced a truce — Japan devastated both sides. After Japan's defeat, the civil war resumed. Why did the Communists win?

  • Mao focused on the peasants — roughly 80% of China's population. He promised land reform that would directly benefit them.
  • The Nationalists were associated with the urban elite, corruption, and failed governance.
  • After Japan's defeat, the Communists pushed the Nationalists to the island of Taiwan (where the Republic of China technically still exists).
  • The People's Republic of China was proclaimed October 1, 1949.

Mao's Three Steps — How the CCP Ruled China

Step 1
Land Reform

Mao had promised land to the peasants. He delivered: landlords were rounded up, publicly humiliated, and executed. Their land was redistributed to the peasants. This was the foundation of communist support in rural China — direct material benefit to the majority of the population.

Step 2
Great Leap Forward (1958–62)

Mao's version of Stalin's Five-Year Plans: collectivize agriculture and force rapid industrialization. Freeman-pedia's comparison: Stalin's collectivization killed approximately 12 million people in the Soviet Union. But China has a much larger population. Mao's Great Leap Forward killed approximately 50 million people — the largest man-made famine in history. Peasants were forced into agricultural communes; food production plummeted; the government hid the famine and continued exporting food abroad.

Let That Sink In

50 million people. That's more than the Mongols killed. That's way more than World War One. That's more than WWI and WWII combined in some estimates. And it happened not through warfare but through catastrophically misguided agricultural policy combined with government denial and cover-up.

Step 3
Cultural Revolution (1966–76)

Mao launched a campaign to destroy the "Four Olds": old customs, old culture, old habits, and old ideas. Red Guards (mostly teenagers mobilized by Mao) attacked traditional culture: they destroyed Confucius's tomb, vandalized temples, purged intellectuals, teachers, and anyone connected to traditional Chinese culture. Urban youth were sent to the countryside to "learn from the peasants." Millions were persecuted; hundreds of thousands killed. This crippled China's educated class for a generation. Mao died in 1976; real reform didn't begin until Deng Xiaoping in the late 1970s.

Name all three steps of Mao's rule. For each: what was the goal, what happened, and what was the consequence? What is the death toll of the Great Leap Forward — and how should you contextualize it?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Step 1: Land Reform (1949–53)
Goal: Fulfill the communist promise to give land to the peasants and eliminate the landlord class.
What happened: Landlords were publicly humiliated, tried at "struggle sessions," and executed. Their land was redistributed to peasants.
Consequence: Created genuine popular support for the CCP among the rural majority; eliminated the traditional landed elite. Effective but brutal — estimated 1–2 million landlords killed.

Step 2: Great Leap Forward (1958–62)
Goal: Rapidly industrialize and collectivize Chinese agriculture simultaneously — modeled on Stalin's Five-Year Plans but even more ambitious.
What happened: Peasants forced into agricultural communes; household cooking pots melted down to meet steel quotas (in "backyard furnaces" that produced useless steel); food production collapsed; the government covered up the famine and continued exporting food; local officials falsified production numbers to avoid punishment.
Death toll: ~50 million people — the largest man-made famine in history. For context: more than the entire population of California today. More than WWII's total military deaths. Stalin's collectivization killed ~12 million in a country with a much smaller population.

Step 3: Cultural Revolution (1966–76)
Goal: Rebuild Chinese culture along pure communist lines by destroying the "Four Olds" (old customs, culture, habits, ideas).
What happened: Red Guards (teenagers) attacked teachers, intellectuals, Confucian temples and sites; sent urban youth to the countryside; purged party officials accused of "revisionism"; Mao used it to eliminate political rivals.
Consequence: Crippled China's educated class for a generation; set back education, science, and culture by decades. China didn't begin recovering until Deng Xiaoping's pragmatic reforms after Mao's death in 1976.

Redistribution — Iran's White Revolution

Why Iran? The College Board's Comparison

Iran is NOT communist. But the College Board uses it as an illustrative example of redistribution — one of the key communist/socialist policy tools — as a contrast to China's approach. Know it for that purpose.

Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi implemented 19 modernizing reforms (1963–79) — a "bloodless revolution" (hence "white"):

  • Land redistribution: The government BOUGHT land from landlords (vs. China's execution-and-seizure approach) and sold it to peasants at 30% below market value. 40% of all Iranians received land.
  • Women's suffrage (1963): Iranian women received the right to vote
  • Literacy Corps: Mandatory literacy education — 2/3 of Iran was illiterate in 1963
  • Dam construction: Tripled irrigation across Iran

Critical context: The Shah was a US-supported authoritarian installed by a CIA-engineered coup (1953). His White Revolution modernized Iran but didn't democratize it. Rising opposition from both the Left and Islamic conservatives culminated in the Iranian Revolution of 1979, which overthrew the Shah and established the Islamic Republic of Iran.

How did Iran's White Revolution approach land redistribution differently than Mao's China? What does this comparison reveal about different paths to the same policy goal?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Same goal: land to peasants.

China (Mao's land reform): Landlords were arrested, publicly humiliated at "struggle sessions," and executed. Their land was seized without compensation and redistributed to peasants. Method: revolutionary violence; complete elimination of the landlord class. Political context: communist revolution, class warfare as state ideology.

Iran (White Revolution): The government purchased land from landlords at 30% below market value and sold it to peasants at the same price. No landlord was executed or imprisoned. 40% of Iranians received land. Method: market-based transaction; landlords were compensated and their class was not eliminated. Political context: US-backed authoritarian modernization; the Shah wanted economic development and political stability, not class warfare.

What the comparison reveals: The same policy outcome (land redistribution to peasants) can be achieved through radically different methods with radically different political implications. Mao's method was faster and more thorough but required mass violence. Iran's method was more moderate but left existing power structures largely intact and created different types of opposition (Islamic conservatives resented the secular modernization; the left wanted more thoroughgoing reform). The College Board uses this comparison to show that redistribution is not exclusively communist — it's a policy tool with many applications.
Section 03

Decolonization — Backwards Imperialism

Why Did Decolonization Happen NOW?
  • WWII exhausted the colonial powers: Britain, France, Netherlands, Belgium — all spent and militarily depleted from the most destructive war in history
  • Colonial subjects returned home radicalized: 1 million Indians fought in WWI; 15 million from British colonies fought in WWII. They came home demanding the self-determination they had fought to defend in Europe
  • The UN Charter (1945) committed its members to "the principle of equal rights and self-determination of peoples" — making colonialism legally indefensible
  • Cold War dynamics: Both the US (ideologically anti-colonial) and USSR (supporting revolutionary movements) pressured European empires to decolonize

Path 1: Negotiated Independence — Ghana (1957)

🌍 Kwame Nkrumah — "Self-Government Now"

Ghana was the first sub-Saharan African state to gain independence (1957) — Freeman-pedia's "negotiated peace" example. Kwame Nkrumah used "positive action" — non-violent resistance, education, and economic pressure — modeled on Gandhi's approach. He was jailed repeatedly, like Gandhi.

Progression: By 1925, Ghanaians had their own legislature. By 1946, Ghanaians held the majority in their own legislature — the first in sub-Saharan Africa. 1957: The Gold Coast became the Republic of Ghana, flying the Pan-African colors (red, yellow, green) with a black star representing African emancipation.

Why Ghana matters: It proved decolonization didn't require armed struggle and set a template for other African independence movements. Nkrumah became a pan-African icon.

Path 2: Armed Struggle — Angola (1961–1975)

⚔️ Angola — Independence Followed by Proxy War

Angola's history with Portugal dates to Unit 4 — Queen Nzinga fought the Portuguese in the 17th century. Angolans weren't even allowed to form political parties under Portuguese colonial rule. 1961: Armed revolt begins and spreads nationwide. Portugal fought three simultaneous colonial wars (Angola, Mozambique, Guinea) — the military strain helped end the Portuguese dictatorship itself. 1974: A coup in Portugal overthrew the dictatorship; the new republic immediately ended all colonial wars. 1975: Angola gains independence — then immediately descends into civil war.

The two sides of Angola's civil war? USSR-backed MPLA vs. US-backed UNITA. It was a proxy war. The civil war lasted until 2002 — 27 years after independence. 500,000 dead, a third of the population displaced, 100,000 children separated from families, landmines still in the soil today.

Why Angola matters: It shows the dark side of Cold War proxy conflicts — a nation wins independence only to have its civil war immediately weaponized by superpower competition.

CountryIndependenceMethodKey Details
India & Pakistan1947Negotiated (with partition)Gandhi's non-violent civil disobedience forced British withdrawal. But partition along religious lines killed over 1 million in communal violence and displaced 15 million — one of history's largest forced migrations.
Ghana1957NegotiatedNkrumah's "positive action." First independent sub-Saharan African nation. Template for peaceful decolonization.
Algeria1962Armed struggleThe FLN fought France for 8 years. France had 1 million European settlers (pieds-noirs) who resisted independence. Over 1 million Algerians died. France's most traumatic decolonization.
Vietnam1954 / 1975Armed struggleHo Chi Minh's Viet Minh defeated the French at Dien Bien Phu (1954). Divided at the 17th parallel (like Korea at the 38th). North-South division became a Cold War proxy conflict killing millions more before reunification in 1975.
Congo1960Nominally negotiatedBelgium rushed independence without preparation. Patrice Lumumba (PM) assassinated with Belgian and US involvement. Civil war followed. Cautionary tale of decolonization without preparation.
Compare Ghana's independence and Algeria's independence. What was similar? What explains the fundamental difference in HOW they achieved independence?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Similarities: Both had organized nationalist movements with clear ideological frameworks. Both were accelerated by WWII (colonial powers exhausted). Both faced international pressure from the UN's self-determination principle and Cold War dynamics. Both gained independence in the same general wave (late 1950s–early 1960s). In both cases, colonial rule had become economically and politically costly for the imperial power.

Why Ghana was peaceful: Britain governed Ghana through a relatively thin colonial administration that depended on African cooperation. Without that cooperation (Nkrumah's "positive action" — strikes, boycotts, mass organizing), governance became impossible. Crucially, there was no significant European settler population in Ghana with strong incentives to resist independence. Nkrumah built incrementally (legislature by 1925, majority by 1946) and exploited Britain's post-WWII weakness.

Why Algeria was violent: France had 1 million European settlers (pieds-noirs) who had lived in Algeria for generations, owned land and businesses, and had every reason to resist independence — they would lose everything. These settlers had enormous political power in France itself. The FLN had no choice but armed struggle because there was no path to negotiated independence while a million settlers and the French military resisted. Eight years of brutal war, over 1 million Algerians killed, finally made Algeria's cost unsustainable for France.

The lesson: The presence or absence of a large European settler population determined whether negotiation was possible. This same factor explains why Kenya (some settlers, Mau Mau rebellion) was more violent than Nigeria (fewer settlers, relatively peaceful transition).
Section 04

Newly Independent States — What Happened After Independence

Getting independence was the beginning, not the end. Newly independent states had to solve enormous problems: colonial borders that made no sense, economies designed to serve imperial powers rather than local populations, and Cold War superpowers immediately trying to pull them into their orbit.

1. New Boundaries — The Cartography Problem

Freeman-pedia: "The European imperialists were terrible cartographers. The borders they drew were trash."

The borders of most post-colonial nations in Africa and the Middle East were drawn by European powers at the Berlin Conference (1884–85) with no regard for ethnic, linguistic, religious, or cultural boundaries. The result:

  • Ethnic groups were split across multiple nations (Kurds across Turkey, Iraq, Iran, Syria; Yoruba across Nigeria and Benin)
  • Multiple rival ethnic groups were forced into single nations (Rwanda: Hutus and Tutsis; Iraq: Sunnis, Shia, and Kurds; Nigeria: over 250 ethnic groups)

Three Key Border Disputes: Israel/Palestine (British Palestine became Israel in 1948, displacing Palestinian Arabs); India/Pakistan Partition 1947 (Radcliffe Line through Punjab and Bengal — 1 million killed, 15 million displaced); Korea (divided at 38th parallel 1945/1953 — still divided today).

2. Economic Development — Building Nations from Colonial Extraction

Leader / CountryWhat They Did & Why It Matters
Gamal Abdel Nasser — EgyptBuilt the Aswan High Dam on the Nile — controlled flooding, irrigated vast new land, generated half of Egypt's electricity.
