When Nasser nationalized the Suez Canal (1956) to fund the dam after the US and Britain withdrew funding, Britain and France invaded (the Suez Crisis). The US forced them to withdraw — demonstrating that European imperial power was over. A landmark event in both decolonization and the Cold War.
Sirimavo Bandaranaike — Sri LankaBecame the world's first non-hereditary female head of government in 1960 (Prime Minister of Ceylon/Sri Lanka). Nationalized private schools to expand public education. Freeman-pedia highlights her as a remarkable historical first that often gets overlooked.
Julius Nyerere — TanzaniaBuilt the TAZARA Railway (Tanzania-Zambia Railway) — the largest sub-Saharan railway. Connected landlocked Zambia to the Tanzanian coast, reducing dependence on apartheid South Africa. Key pan-African figure and advocate for the Free Mandela campaign. Freeman-pedia's running joke: Nyerere had a toothbrush mustache and keeps showing up throughout Unit 8.
Kwame Nkrumah — GhanaBeyond independence, pursued pan-African unity. Built the Akosombo Dam on the Volta River. Advocated for African Economic Community. Eventually overthrown in a military coup (1966) while on a diplomatic trip. Cautionary tale: even successful independence leaders couldn't always maintain democratic rule.

3. Migration Toward the Metropoles

🌍 Why Did Post-Colonial Migrants Go to the Metropole?

A metropole is the mother country that ruled the colony. After independence, migrants from former colonies disproportionately moved to the metropole:

  • Decades of colonial rule created cultural and linguistic familiarity
  • South Asians (Indians, Pakistanis, Bangladeshis) → Britain. North and West Africans → France. Filipinos → United States. Caribbean peoples → their respective metropoles

The result: the populations of former imperial powers became dramatically more diverse in the second half of the 20th century. Britain today has large South Asian and Caribbean communities; France has large North and West African communities. These migrations permanently reshaped the culture and demographics of the metropoles.

What is a metropole? Why do post-colonial migrants disproportionately go to the metropole rather than other destinations? Give two specific examples.
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
A metropole is the "mother country" that ruled a colony. Post-colonial migrants disproportionately move to the metropole for three reasons:

1. Language: Decades of colonial rule created widespread knowledge of the metropole's language. Indians, Pakistanis, and Nigerians speak English because of British colonial education; North Africans speak French for the same reason.

2. Cultural familiarity: Colonial subjects grew up learning about the metropole's institutions, history, and culture. British institutions (common law, parliamentary system, educational degrees) were familiar in a way that, say, German institutions were not.

3. Family and community networks: Colonial rule brought some people to the metropole as students, workers, or soldiers. These early migrants created networks that subsequent migrants could use — finding jobs, housing, and community through existing connections.

Specific examples:
• South Asians (Indians, Pakistanis, Bangladeshis) → Britain: 200 years of British colonial rule in South Asia created English as a common language, British institutions as familiar frameworks, and family connections from colonial-era migration. Today Britain has ~4 million South Asians.
• North and West Africans (Algerians, Moroccans, Senegalese) → France: French colonial rule created French-speaking educated classes in North and West Africa who, after independence, often moved to France seeking economic opportunities. Today France has ~5–6 million North and West Africans.
Section 05

Global Resistance to Established Power Structures

📌 Four Categories the College Board Tests

The College Board organizes this section around people and movements that either resisted or intensified conflict during the Cold War/decolonization era. Know all four categories with at least one specific example each.

Category 1: Intensifiers of Conflict — Pinochet & Chile

In 1970, Salvador Allende was democratically elected as Chile's president — a socialist who won fair elections. In 1973, General Augusto Pinochet led a military coup (with documented US CIA support) that overthrew Allende. Allende died during the coup. Pinochet ruled as a military dictator from 1973 to 1990.

Pinochet was the most prominent figure in Operation Condor — a coordinated program among South American military dictatorships (Argentina, Chile, Uruguay, Paraguay, Bolivia, Brazil) to track down, "disappear," and kill political dissidents across national borders, with US intelligence support.

⚠️ The Cold War's Cynical Logic at Its Extreme

Pinochet's Chile is the clearest example of the US's Cold War logic taken to its most cynical extreme — actively overthrowing a democratically elected government because it was socialist, and supporting a brutal dictatorship because it was anti-communist. The US repeatedly chose anti-communism over democracy during the Cold War, which created enormous resentment in Latin America and beyond.

Category 2: Non-Violent Resistance — Nelson Mandela (MVP)

⭐ MVP of Unit 8 — Nelson Mandela

Apartheid ("apartness" in Afrikaans) was South Africa's system of racial segregation, formalized in law in 1948. Every aspect of life — where you could live, go to school, eat, or love — was subject to racial classification.

Nelson Mandela's Path
  • Trained as a lawyer; used legal advocacy to fight apartheid (like Gandhi before him)
  • Arrested repeatedly; traveled illegally to other African nations to build support
  • Sentenced to life imprisonment in 1964; served 27 years on Robben Island
  • "Free Mandela" became a global cause — concerts, boycotts, international economic sanctions on South Africa
  • 1989: New South African president F.W. de Klerk negotiated Mandela's release (1990) and the end of apartheid
  • 1994: First election in South Africa open to all races — Mandela won in a landslide. Shared the Nobel Peace Prize with de Klerk.
The Non-Violence Tradition — Four Giants

Freeman-pedia groups these four together: Gandhi (India/South Africa), Martin Luther King Jr. (US Civil Rights), Kwame Nkrumah (Ghana independence), and Nelson Mandela (South African anti-apartheid). All four used non-violence to achieve structural political change against overwhelmingly powerful opponents. All four were imprisoned for their activism.

Why is Nelson Mandela Freeman-pedia's MVP of Unit 8? What is apartheid? What did Mandela do before prison, what happened during his 27 years in prison, and what did he accomplish after his release?
WHAT THE AP EXPECTS YOU TO KNOW
Apartheid: Afrikaans for "apartness." South Africa's system of institutionalized racial segregation formalized in 1948 by the National Party government. Classified all people into racial categories and used the legal system to restrict where non-white people could live, work, go to school, receive medical care, or even love. A comprehensive system of racial oppression rooted in Dutch colonial rule and intensified after South African independence.

Mandela before prison: Trained as South Africa's first Black lawyer. Used legal advocacy and, increasingly, organized resistance through the ANC (African National Congress). After the Sharpeville Massacre (1960) when police killed 69 peaceful protesters, Mandela helped found the ANC's armed wing. He was arrested, tried for treason, and sentenced to life imprisonment in 1964.

During his 27 years on Robben Island (1964–1990): "Free Mandela" became a global cause. Musicians held benefit concerts. Countries imposed economic sanctions on South Africa. Julius Nyerere's Tanzania was a key African advocate. International pressure on the apartheid government grew steadily throughout the 1970s and 1980s. Mandela refused offers of conditional release and became a symbol of principled resistance.

After his release (1990): Negotiated the end of apartheid with President F.W. de Klerk — an extraordinary act of political leadership and reconciliation. In 1994, South Africa held its first election open to all races. Mandela won with ~62% of the vote. He served one term as president, modeling peaceful democratic transition. Shared the Nobel Peace Prize with de Klerk. Retired in 1999. Died 2013. Freeman-pedia's MVP because he represents the convergence of Unit 8's two themes — decolonization and non-violent resistance — and because his triumph was one of the most remarkable political achievements of the 20th century.

Category 3: Destabilizing Dictators — Idi Amin (Uganda)

Full title: "His Excellency, President for Life, Field Marshal Alhaji Dr. Idi Amin Dada, VC, DSO, MC, Conqueror of the British Empire, Lord of All the Beasts of the Earth and Fishes of the Seas, and Conqueror of the British Empire in Africa in General and Uganda in Particular." Freeman-pedia notes he was "legitimately crazy."

Amin led a coup in 1971 and ruled as a brutal military dictator. He expelled Uganda's entire Asian population (70,000 people, mostly Indian-origin Ugandans). He killed approximately 500,000 Ugandans. His regime ended when he invaded Tanzania — whose president Julius Nyerere (the toothbrush mustache guy) counterattacked and drove him out in 1979.

Why he matters: Amin is a prime example of how newly independent states in Africa were destabilized by military coups and brutal dictatorships — often in the vacuum left by colonial rule without the institutions, traditions, or economic stability needed for stable governance. He also shows that Cold War dynamics are not the only cause of post-independence instability.

Category 4: Violence Against Civilians — Al-Qaeda

Arabic: "the base." Founded by veterans of the Soviet-Afghan War (1979–89) — many of whom had been armed and trained by the CIA as mujahideen fighting the Soviets. Al-Qaeda seeks the establishment of a global Islamic caliphate governed by Sharia law.

Most known for: the September 11, 2001 attacks — the deadliest terrorist attack in history (nearly 3,000 killed). Stated reasons: US support for Israel; US military presence in the Middle East; US support for governments that have acted against Muslim interests.

⚠️ Historical Irony

The US helped create the mujahideen who became al-Qaeda by arming them against the Soviets in Afghanistan — the same proxy war logic that backfired catastrophically. The CIA funded, armed, and trained fighters who later attacked the US. This is the most consequential unintended consequence of Cold War proxy war logic.

What is the historical connection between the Soviet-Afghan War (1979–89) and the September 11, 2001 attacks?
Section 06

Glossary of Key Terms

Test yourself on each term before revealing. Mark how well you know it.

Section 07

Timeline of Unit 8 (1945–1994)

1945

UN Founded; Cold War Begins

United Nations replaces the League of Nations. The wartime US-Soviet alliance immediately fractures. Nuclear age and Cold War begin simultaneously.

1947

Truman Doctrine; India/Pakistan Independence

US commits to containing communism globally. Britain hastily partitions India; partition violence kills over a million and displaces 15 million.

1948

Israel Established; Apartheid Formally Begins

UN recognizes Israel; first Arab-Israeli War begins. South Africa's National Party formally codifies apartheid.

1949

NATO Formed; PRC Established; USSR Tests Nuclear Bomb

Western military alliance. Mao's Communists win China's civil war; Nationalists flee to Taiwan. The USSR now has nukes — the arms race accelerates.

1950–53

Korean War

First major Cold War proxy war. Stalemate at the 38th parallel. Korea remains divided. UN collectively defended South Korea.

1953

CIA Coup in Iran; Korean Armistice

CIA-backed coup overthrows democratic PM Mossadegh, restores the Shah — sowing seeds of the 1979 Islamic Revolution. Korea armistice signed.

1954

Battle of Dien Bien Phu; French Leave Indochina

Ho Chi Minh's forces defeat the French. Vietnam divided at 17th parallel. Sets up the Vietnam War.

1955

Bandung Conference; Warsaw Pact

29 African and Asian nations found the Non-Aligned Movement. Soviet military alliance with Eastern Europe formalizes the Cold War's bipolar structure.

1956

Suez Crisis

Nasser nationalizes Suez Canal; Britain and France invade; US forces their withdrawal. Signals the end of British and French imperial power.

1957

Ghana Independence; Sputnik

First independent sub-Saharan African nation (Nkrumah). USSR launches the first Earth-orbiting satellite — the Space Race begins.

1958–62

Great Leap Forward (China)

Mao's collectivization and industrialization campaign. Result: ~50 million dead in the largest man-made famine in history.

1960

"Year of Africa" — 17 Nations Independent

Seventeen African nations gained independence in a single year. The age of European empire effectively ended. Sirimavo Bandaranaike becomes world's first female head of government in Sri Lanka.

1962

Cuban Missile Crisis

The closest humanity has come to nuclear war. 13 days; USSR backs down and removes missiles from Cuba. Demonstrates both MAD and the limits of superpower brinksmanship.

1966–76

Cultural Revolution (China)

Mao purges "Four Olds"; Red Guards attack traditional culture, intellectuals, teachers. Hundreds of thousands killed; millions persecuted. China set back a generation.

1973

Pinochet Coup in Chile

US-backed military coup overthrows democratically elected socialist Allende. Operation Condor coordinates South American dictatorships to kill leftists.

1975

Angola Independence; Vietnam Reunification

Angola gains independence but immediately descends into proxy civil war. North Vietnam reunifies the country under communist rule.

1979

Iranian Revolution; Soviet Invasion of Afghanistan

Shah overthrown; Islamic Republic of Iran established. USSR invades Afghanistan — their "Vietnam"; the mujahideen it fights will become al-Qaeda.

1989

Berlin Wall Falls; Tiananmen Square

East Germany opens the wall (November 9). Communist governments fall across Eastern Europe. In China, the government massacres pro-democracy protesters at Tiananmen Square.

1991

USSR Dissolves; Cold War Ends

The Soviet Union breaks into 15 independent republics on December 25, 1991. The Cold War is over. The US is the sole remaining superpower.

1994

Mandela Elected; Rwandan Genocide

South Africa's first free election — Mandela wins. Simultaneously, Hutu militias kill ~800,000 Tutsis in 100 days while the world watches and does nothing.

Fill in: Nelson Mandela spent _____ years imprisoned on Robben Island before his release in 1990 and his election as South Africa's first democratically elected president in ______.
Section 08

Historical Thinking Skills

1. Comparison — The Primary Skill for Decolonization

WEAK: "India and Algeria both gained independence from European empires."

STRONG: "Both India and Algeria gained independence through sustained nationalist movements that ultimately made colonial rule too costly for the imperial power to maintain, but they differed fundamentally in method and human cost. India's independence was primarily achieved through Gandhi's non-violent civil disobedience — mass protest that mobilized the majority population and made British rule administratively unmanageable — whereas Algeria's liberation required eight years of brutal armed conflict between the FLN and French forces, resulting in over one million Algerian deaths. The difference reflects the specific nature of colonial rule in each case: Britain governed India through a relatively thin colonial administration that depended on Indian cooperation; France had established over a million European settlers in Algeria who had every incentive to resist independence violently."

2. Continuity and Change Over Time

CONTINUITIES
  • Inequality between wealthy industrialized nations and developing nations continues
  • Nationalism continues to drive political conflict
  • Great power competition continues (US vs. USSR replaces European imperial rivalry)
  • Authoritarian governance continues in many regions
  • Migration continues as a global phenomenon
CHANGES
  • European formal empires are dismantled; sovereignty transferred to formerly colonized peoples
  • Nuclear weapons make great power direct conflict unthinkable; competition goes cold
  • Direction of mass migration reverses: colonial peoples migrate to metropoles (instead of Europeans to colonies)
  • Decolonization creates dozens of new independent nations; UN membership triples
  • International institutions (UN, International Court of Justice) have mechanisms for enforcing human rights norms
Practice: Write a two-sentence thesis for this DBQ prompt: "Evaluate the extent to which the Cold War shaped the process of decolonization between 1945 and 1975."
SAMPLE THESIS
"The Cold War significantly shaped decolonization by giving independence movements superpower support, creating proxy conflicts in newly independent states, and pressuring colonial powers to decolonize quickly to avoid newly independent nations aligning with the Soviet Union; however, the primary drivers of decolonization were internal nationalist movements and the military and economic exhaustion of colonial powers following World War II, which would have produced decolonization even without Cold War pressure."
Section 09

Common Pitfalls & How to Avoid Them

❌

Pitfall 1: Treating the Cold War as Only US vs. USSR

The Problem: Students write the Cold War as a simple two-sided conflict and ignore the Non-Aligned Movement entirely. The result: essays that miss 30+ nations with their own agendas, and analysis that misses how newly independent nations navigated between superpowers.

✅ The Fix: Always include Team C. India's Nehru, Egypt's Nasser, Ghana's Nkrumah, and Yugoslavia's Tito all pursued independence from both blocs. The Bandung Conference (1955) is your go-to example.

❌

Pitfall 2: Thinking All Decolonized States Became Democracies

The Problem: "Decolonization" means political independence — it does NOT mean democratization. Many newly independent states became military dictatorships (Idi Amin in Uganda), one-party states, or authoritarian regimes. Conflating independence with democracy is historically wrong.

✅ The Fix: Distinguish between decolonization (political independence from colonialism) and democratization (establishment of democratic governance). Idi Amin, Pinochet, and Mao all represent independence from Western imperialism without democracy.

❌

Pitfall 3: Ignoring How Colonial Borders Created Post-Independence Conflicts

The Problem: Students treat decolonization as a success story: "The colonies won independence and were free." They ignore that colonial borders, which cut across ethnic and cultural lines, created the conditions for post-independence civil wars, ethnic conflicts, and genocides.

✅ The Fix: For every decolonization example, note the border problem: India/Pakistan partition violence; the Rwandan Genocide (rooted in Belgian colonial exploitation of Hutu-Tutsi divisions); the Angolan civil war. The borders created at the Berlin Conference (Unit 6) caused the post-independence conflicts of Unit 8.

❌

Pitfall 4: Forgetting That Proxy Wars Had Real Human Costs

The Problem: Students describe proxy wars in abstract strategic terms without noting that real people died in enormous numbers. The Vietnam War killed ~2 million Vietnamese. The Angolan civil war killed 500,000. The Afghan war killed millions.

✅ The Fix: For every proxy war, always state the human cost alongside the strategic outcome. The pattern: superpowers competed; local populations paid the price.

❌

Pitfall 5: Simplifying Mao's China to Just "Communism"

The Problem: Students write "Mao made China communist" and move on. But the AP tests the specific policies: land reform, the Great Leap Forward (~50 million dead), and the Cultural Revolution (attacking the "Four Olds").

✅ The Fix: Know all three stages of Mao's rule as a sequence: land reform (1949–53) → Great Leap Forward (1958–62) → Cultural Revolution (1966–76). Each had distinct goals, methods, and consequences.

A student writes: "After independence, African nations became democracies and could finally govern themselves freely." What is wrong with this statement?
Section 10

Sample DBQ — With Full Breakdown

📊 DBQ Basics

DBQ = 25% of AP exam. 7 documents, 60 minutes (15 min reading). Thesis + contextualization + document analysis + outside evidence + sourcing + complexity.

"Evaluate the extent to which the Cold War shaped the process of decolonization between 1945 and 1975."
1
Decode

Command: "Evaluate the extent to which" — HOW MUCH did the Cold War shape decolonization? Other factors also shaped it (WWII exhaustion of colonial powers, nationalist movements, UN pressure) — those are your counterevidence.
The prompt assumes the Cold War DID shape decolonization — your job is to argue how much, compared to other factors.

✅ Sample Thesis

"The Cold War significantly shaped decolonization by giving independence movements superpower support, creating proxy conflicts in newly independent states, and pressuring colonial powers to decolonize quickly to avoid newly independent nations aligning with the Soviet Union; however, the primary drivers of decolonization were internal nationalist movements and the military and economic exhaustion of colonial powers following World War II, which would have produced decolonization even without Cold War pressure."

📝 Sample Contextualization

"The wave of decolonization that transformed the global political map between 1945 and 1975 emerged from the wreckage of World War II. The war had simultaneously demonstrated the physical limits of European imperial power (Japan had briefly occupied most of British, French, and Dutch Southeast Asia, showing that Europeans were not invincible) and morally delegitimized empire (a war fought against fascist racial hierarchy was difficult to square with the continuation of colonial racial hierarchies). Colonial peoples who had fought in the war — a million Indians in WWI, fifteen million British colonial subjects in WWII — returned home expecting the self-determination that Allied rhetoric had promised. The UN Charter's commitment to 'the principle of equal rights and self-determination of peoples' made colonialism legally indefensible. Into this context, the Cold War introduced a new dynamic: two superpowers with global reach competing to win the allegiance of newly independent nations."

2
Essay Outline
  • Body ¶1 — HOW COLD WAR SHAPED DECOLONIZATION: Superpower support — US ideologically opposed colonialism and supported independence movements to win newly independent nations to the capitalist bloc; USSR supported revolutionary independence movements (Ho Chi Minh, MPLA in Angola). Both superpowers pressured colonial powers (Suez Crisis 1956: US forced Britain and France to withdraw from Egypt).
  • Body ¶2 — HOW COLD WAR COMPLICATED DECOLONIZATION: Proxy wars in newly independent states — Angola gained independence in 1975 immediately followed by a US vs. USSR proxy civil war lasting until 2002; Vietnam's decolonization from France produced a Cold War proxy conflict; Korea's division prevented reunification after Japanese colonial rule ended.
  • Body ¶3 — OTHER CAUSES (limiting Cold War's role): Ghana: Nkrumah's independence movement succeeded primarily through internal nationalist organizing — the Cold War was peripheral. India: Gandhi's movement drove British withdrawal; the Cold War played almost no role. WWII exhaustion: Britain and France couldn't have maintained their empires even without Cold War pressure.

DBQ Rubric — Maximum 7 Points

1pt
ThesisDirectly addresses "to what extent" (significant but not primary cause), lists competing causes, and establishes a line of reasoning.
1pt
ContextualizationFull paragraph explaining WWII's role in exhausting and delegitimizing colonialism, connecting to WHY decolonization happened when it did regardless of Cold War.
2pts
Evidence: Document ContentDocuments might include: independence declarations, Cold War propaganda, UN resolutions, Bandung Conference declaration, nationalist leaders' speeches, superpower policy documents. Use at least 3 (1pt) and at least 6 (2nd pt).
1pt
Evidence: Beyond DocumentsSuez Crisis (US forced Britain and France out), Angola proxy war, Cuban Missile Crisis dynamic, Vietnam proxy war, Non-Aligned Movement at Bandung, Ghana's negotiated independence without Cold War involvement.
1pt
Analysis: SourcingExample: "A Soviet propaganda document supporting Angolan independence reflects the USSR's goal of winning newly independent African nations to the communist bloc, not a principled commitment to anti-colonialism — the same Soviet Union that forcibly dominated Eastern Europe."
1pt
Analysis: ComplexityShow that the Cold War both accelerated decolonization (superpower pressure on colonial powers) AND complicated it (proxy wars in newly independent states). Or: the Non-Aligned Movement was itself evidence that decolonization was not fully shaped by the Cold War — many nations actively rejected both blocs.
Section 11

Sample LEQ — With Full Breakdown

📊 LEQ Basics

LEQ = 15% of AP exam. 40 minutes. From memory — no documents. Choose from three prompts.

"Compare the factors leading to independence in India and Algeria."
1
Decode

Command: "Compare" — BOTH similarities AND differences required
Topic: FACTORS leading to independence (why/how it happened — not the events of independence itself)
Both countries must be substantial in your response

✅ Sample Thesis

"Both India and Algeria gained independence through sustained nationalist movements that made continued colonial rule unacceptably costly for the imperial power, but they differed fundamentally in method and human cost: India achieved independence primarily through Gandhi's non-violent civil disobedience, which exploited Britain's economic dependence on Indian cooperation and its post-WWII weakness, while Algeria required eight years of armed conflict and over one million deaths because France's large European settler population had both the political power and the motivation to resist independence violently, making negotiated departure impossible until the war's cost became unsustainable for France itself."

2
Essay Outline
  • Contextualization: Both India and Algeria decolonized in the post-WWII wave, when colonial powers were exhausted, the UN's self-determination principle delegitimized empire, and Cold War competition gave independence movements leverage. But the specific nature of colonial rule determined whether negotiation or armed struggle would succeed.
  • Body ¶1 — SIMILARITIES: Both had organized nationalist movements (Indian National Congress; Algerian FLN). Both were accelerated by WWII (Britain couldn't maintain India; France couldn't maintain Algeria after Dien Bien Phu). Both faced international pressure from the UN and Cold War dynamics favoring decolonization.
  • Body ¶2 — DIFFERENCES: India: Gandhi's non-violent civil disobedience (Salt March 1930, Quit India Movement 1942). Britain governed through a thin colonial administration dependent on Indian cooperation. Algeria: France had 1 million European settlers (pieds-noirs) who would lose everything; FLN used guerrilla warfare; France used brutal counter-insurgency; over 1 million Algerians died.
  • Body ¶3 — COMPLEXITY: Both resulted in difficult post-independence situations — India: Partition violence killed 1+ million; Kashmir dispute remains unresolved. Algeria: FLN established an authoritarian single-party state; pieds-noirs expelled; economic challenges persisted. Independence was not the end of the struggle.

LEQ Rubric — Maximum 6 Points

1pt
ThesisIdentifies a similarity (both used nationalist movements to make colonial rule costly), identifies the key difference (non-violence vs. armed struggle because of the settler population), and connects to a line of reasoning about WHY they differed.
1pt
ContextualizationFull paragraph explaining the post-WWII decolonization wave and WHY this moment enabled independence movements globally — colonial exhaustion, UN pressure, Cold War dynamics.
2pts
EvidenceIndia: INC, Gandhi's Salt March and Quit India Movement, Jinnah and Muslim League, partition violence. Algeria: FLN, guerrilla war, Battle of Algiers, pieds-noirs settlers, French counter-insurgency, over 1 million dead.
2pts
Analysis & Reasoning: ComparisonEarn both by: (1) making explicit comparisons (not describing each separately) AND (2) explaining WHY the differences matter — the presence or absence of a large settler population determined whether negotiation was possible.
Section 12

Unit Summary — The Big Picture

Can you answer ALL of these from memory?

What are the three "teams" of the Cold War — give a country and leader for each? What is containment? What is the Domino Theory? What is MAD — and why didn't the US attack Cuba even though it had many more nukes? What is a proxy war — give two examples with human cost figures. What are Mao's three steps, and what is the death toll of the Great Leap Forward? What is the White Revolution in Iran — how does its land redistribution differ from China's? What are the two paths to decolonization — give one specific example of each? What is a metropole and why do post-colonial migrants go there? Who is Nelson Mandela — what did he do before, during, and after his 27 years in prison? What is apartheid? What is Operation Condor — who was Pinochet? What did Nasser, Bandaranaike, and Nyerere each do for their new states? How is al-Qaeda connected to the Soviet-Afghan War?
FULL ANSWERS
Three teams:
Team A: US-led Western bloc (NATO) — capitalist democracies. Examples: US, UK, France, West Germany.
Team B: Soviet-led Eastern bloc (Warsaw Pact) — authoritarian communist states. Examples: USSR, East Germany, Poland, Czechoslovakia.
Team C: Non-Aligned Movement — refused both blocs. Examples: India (Nehru), Egypt (Nasser), Ghana (Nkrumah), Yugoslavia (Tito). Founded at Bandung Conference 1955.

Containment + Domino Theory: Containment (Truman Doctrine, 1947) = preventing communism from spreading beyond its 1945 borders. Domino Theory = if one country falls to communism, its neighbors will follow in sequence. Together, these justified US intervention everywhere from Korea to Vietnam to Nicaragua.

MAD: Mutually Assured Destruction — both sides built so many nukes that either side attacking the other would guarantee their own destruction in retaliation. The US didn't attack Cuba during the Cuban Missile Crisis because even 3,000 Soviet warheads in Cuba was enough to devastate the US — numerical superiority (26,000 US vs. 3,000 Soviet) didn't matter when even the smaller arsenal could destroy civilization.

Proxy war examples:
Vietnam: ~2 million Vietnamese and 58,000 Americans dead; communist North unified the country in 1975 — US costliest Cold War defeat.
Angola: independence 1975 → immediately became a proxy civil war; 500,000 dead; lasted until 2002 — 27 years after independence.
Afghanistan: 15,000 Soviet soldiers + millions of Afghans killed; helped bring down the Soviet Union; CIA-funded mujahideen became the Taliban and al-Qaeda.

Mao's three steps:
1. Land reform (1949–53): Landlords executed, land redistributed to peasants. Created CCP's rural support base.
2. Great Leap Forward (1958–62): Forced agricultural collectivization + rapid industrialization. ~50 million dead — largest man-made famine in history. Government hid famine and continued exporting food.
3. Cultural Revolution (1966–76): Destroy "Four Olds" (old customs, culture, habits, ideas). Red Guards attacked teachers, intellectuals, temples. Hundreds of thousands killed; China set back a generation. Ended with Mao's death (1976).

Iran's White Revolution vs. China's land reform: Same goal (land to peasants). China: landlords executed, land seized — revolutionary violence. Iran: government bought land from landlords at below-market price and sold to peasants — market transaction, landlords compensated. Same destination, radically different methods and political contexts.

Two paths to decolonization: Negotiated: Ghana (1957) — Nkrumah's positive action, non-violence + education; first sub-Saharan African independent nation. Armed struggle: Algeria (1962) — FLN vs. French + 1 million pieds-noirs settlers; 1 million Algerians dead; 8 years of war.

Metropole: The mother country that ruled the colony. Post-colonial migrants go there because colonial rule created cultural and linguistic familiarity. South Asians → Britain. North/West Africans → France. Filipinos → US. Caribbean peoples → their respective metropoles.

Mandela: Apartheid = South Africa's racial segregation system (1948–1994). Before prison: lawyer, ANC activist, helped found ANC armed wing. 27 years on Robben Island (1964–1990); "Free Mandela" became global cause with concerts, boycotts, sanctions. After release: negotiated end of apartheid with F.W. de Klerk; elected president 1994; served one term; shared Nobel Peace Prize.

Pinochet/Operation Condor: 1973 US-backed military coup overthrew democratically elected socialist Allende in Chile. Operation Condor = coordinated program among South American military dictatorships (with US intelligence support) to track and kill leftist dissidents across national borders. Clearest example of Cold War logic enabling authoritarianism: US overthrew democracy because it was socialist, supported dictatorship because it was anti-communist.

New state leaders: Nasser (Egypt) — built Aswan High Dam; nationalized Suez Canal; Suez Crisis showed end of British/French imperial power. Bandaranaike (Sri Lanka) — world's first non-hereditary female head of government (1960); nationalized private schools. Nyerere (Tanzania) — built TAZARA Railway connecting landlocked Zambia to coast; pan-African advocate; drove Idi Amin out of Uganda (1979).

Al-Qaeda + Soviet-Afghan War: CIA armed and funded Afghan mujahideen (through Pakistan's ISI) to fight the Soviet occupation (1979–89). When Soviets withdrew, mujahideen fighters — combat-hardened, CIA-trained and equipped — formed the Taliban government and al-Qaeda. Osama bin Laden was a Saudi fighter who helped lead the mujahideen against the Soviets. Al-Qaeda carried out 9/11 (2001) — the deadliest terrorist attack in history. The US literally helped create the organization that attacked it.
📝 One-Paragraph Summary of Unit 8

Unit 8 (c.1900–present, primarily 1945–1991) runs two massive stories simultaneously. The Cold War: the US-led capitalist bloc (NATO) and the Soviet-led communist bloc (Warsaw Pact) compete for global dominance through nuclear deterrence (MAD), proxy wars (Korea, Vietnam, Angola, Nicaragua, Afghanistan), and ideological competition, while the Non-Aligned Movement (India, Egypt, Ghana, Yugoslavia) refuses to pick a side. Nuclear proliferation makes direct superpower war suicidal; 60+ proxy wars kill millions of people in the Global South instead. Meanwhile, decolonization dismantles the European empires of Unit 6: some colonies negotiate independence (Ghana, India — non-violence works where colonial rule depends on local cooperation); others fight for it (Algeria, Angola, Vietnam — armed struggle where settlers or strategic interests make peaceful withdrawal impossible). Newly independent states face three immediate problems: colonial borders that cut across ethnic lines creating conflict; economies designed for extraction that must be rebuilt for development (Nasser's Aswan Dam, Nyerere's TAZARA Railway); and the pull of the metropole driving post-colonial migration. Global resistance: Pinochet's US-backed coup in Chile (Cold War logic enabling authoritarianism); Nelson Mandela's 27-year imprisonment and eventual triumph over apartheid (Unit 8's MVP — non-violence succeeds again); Idi Amin's destabilizing military dictatorship in Uganda; al-Qaeda (rooted in the Afghan proxy war). Mao's China shows communist expansion through three stages: land reform, the Great Leap Forward (~50 million dead), and the Cultural Revolution. The Cold War ends 1989–91 as Gorbachev's reforms dissolve the Soviet Union. The global map is transformed.

Check-In

Do You Think You Can Move On?

Eight units down. One to go. This is the final check before the finish line.

On a scale from 1–10, how confident do you feel about Unit 8? What feels solid? What still feels fuzzy?
WHAT "READY" MEANS FOR UNIT 8
You are ready to move on to Unit 9 if you can:

✅ Name all three Cold War "teams" with specific countries and leaders for each
✅ Explain containment and the Domino Theory and how they relate
✅ Explain MAD and why the US didn't attack Cuba during the Cuban Missile Crisis
✅ Name three proxy wars with human cost figures
✅ Name all three of Mao's steps with their death toll and consequences
✅ Explain the difference between China's land reform and Iran's White Revolution
✅ Name the two paths to decolonization with one specific example of each
✅ Explain what a metropole is and why post-colonial migrants go there
✅ Describe Nelson Mandela's path: before prison, during his 27 years, and after
✅ Explain Operation Condor and what it reveals about Cold War US foreign policy
✅ Explain how al-Qaeda connects to the Soviet-Afghan War

Fuzzy on any? Spend 10 focused minutes there. You're almost at the finish line.

Unit 9 is Globalization — the most connected world in human history. You're almost there.

Eight units down. One left. The finish line is right there.

Unit 09 · c. 1900–Present · ~10% of AP Exam

Globalization

0%
🌐 Globalization — The final unit. You've made it this far. Finish strong.
Sections
00Introduction
